note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of coronary artery disease (CAD) post drug-eluting stent (DES) to left anterior descending artery (LAD), prostate cancer post prostatectomy, Hodgkin's Lymphoma post splenectomy, chemotherapy and radiation, gout, tonsillectomy, spontaneous subarachnoid hemorrhage treated conservatively, hemorrhoids, herpes zoster and simplex, hyperlipidemia, asthma, and peptic ulcer disease, presented for a routine stress echo. During the stress test, he developed left shoulder pain similar to previous anginal pains and ST changes on EKG. Echocardiography revealed severe aortic regurgitation. The patient was asymptomatic except for faint chest pain with maximal exercise. He was sent to the emergency department (ED) where an EKG showed no ischemic changes and a CTA chest was negative for aortic dissection. 

The patient's father had a myocardial infarction at an unspecified age and died at an unspecified age. The patient's physical exam on admission and prior to discharge was largely unremarkable, with no signs of heart failure. 

Lab results showed abnormal RBC count of 4.23* and Hgb of 13.2* on admission, which improved to 4.39* and 13.7* respectively prior to discharge. Urea Nitrogen was elevated at 23* on admission but normalized to 16 prior to discharge. HCO3 was elevated at 33* prior to discharge. Cholesterol was elevated at 241* and Triglycerides were elevated at 199*. LDL was also elevated at 145*. 

Imaging showed dense aortic arch and coronary artery calcifications, a 3-mm right lower lobe pulmonary nodule, and numerous calcified pleural plaques. The stress test showed anginal type symptoms with borderline ischemic EKG changes at a high cardiac demand and good functional capacity. Resting systolic hypertension with blunted hemodynamic response to physiologic stress was noted. Echo demonstrated severe aortic regurgitation and mild mitral regurgitation with no aortic stenosis or significant resting LVOT gradient. 

The patient's aortic insufficiency was of unclear etiology. Dissection was ruled out by CTA. There was no root dilation seen on TTE so it was more likely valvular in origin. The patient had no clinical signs of endocarditis and blood cultures had no growth to date. The patient's positive stress test and history of CAD suggested that his coronary artery disease may have progressed. However, it may be that he had demand ischemia from his aortic insufficiency that was further compounding the problem. 

The patient's medications upon admission included testosterone, omega-3 fatty acids-vitamin E, arginine, lysine, Magnesium Citrate, Guaifenesin, Glutamine, Pravastatin, phosphatidylcholine, Nitroglycerin, budesonide, Glucosamine-Chondroitin Complex, methylsulfonylmethane, Clopidogrel, selenium, Aspirin, Cyanocobalamin, Folic Acid, Vitamin D, Vitamin E, Diltiazem Extended-Release, and Niacin SR. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of aortic regurgitation, aortic stenosis, and coronary artery disease. His condition upon discharge was clear and coherent, alert and interactive, and ambulatory - independent. He was instructed to follow up with his cardiologist for further evaluation.

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to chest pain, dyspnea with exertion, and fatigue. He has a known history of coronary artery disease with a chronic total occlusion of the right coronary artery and a past drug eluting stent to the left anterior descending artery. He also has a history of prostate cancer, Hodgkin's Lymphoma, gout, spontaneous subarachnoid hemorrhage, hemorrhoids, herpes zoster and simplex, hyperlipidemia, asthma, and peptic ulcer disease. He underwent aortic valve replacement and coronary artery bypass grafting during his stay. 

The patient's symptoms included fatigue and limited tolerance for exercise. His physical exam showed a systolic murmur and a diastolic murmur. His vital signs were stable with a blood pressure of 123/59 on the right and 134/69 on the left, heart rate of 96, respiratory rate of 16, and oxygen saturation of 100%. 

The patient's echocardiography report showed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 50% to 55%, which is slightly below the normal range. The left ventricular fractional shortening was also below the normal range at 0.26. The aortic valve showed moderate stenosis and regurgitation. The mitral valve showed trivial regurgitation. There were multiple complex atheroma in the descending thoracic aorta, at least two of which had mobile components. 

The patient's medications upon admission included Atenolol, Budesonide Nasal Inhaler, Clopidogrel, Lansoprazole Oral Disintegrating Tab, Lisinopril, Metoprolol Tartrate, AndroGel, Aspirin, coenzyme Q10, Guaifenesin ER, lysine, Magnesium Citrate, methylsulfonylmethane, Fish Oil, selenium, and Vitamin E. 

The patient's family history includes his father having a myocardial infarction at an unspecified age and dying at an unspecified age. 

The patient was discharged in good condition, alert and oriented, with steady gait and managed sternal pain. His sternal incision was healing well with no signs of erythema or drainage. His discharge diagnosis was coronary artery disease and aortic insufficiency.

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted for asymptomatic carotid stenosis. He underwent a right carotid endarterectomy as a preventative measure due to progressive carotid stenosis, which had increased to over 80%. The patient was informed of the risks associated with the procedure, including stroke, cranial nerve injury, restenosis, and bleeding. 

His past medical history includes coronary artery disease (CAD) with a drug-eluting stent (DES) to the left anterior descending artery (LAD), prostate cancer treated with prostatectomy, Hodgkin's Lymphoma treated with splenectomy, chemotherapy, and radiation, gout, tonsillectomy, spontaneous subarachnoid hemorrhage treated conservatively, hemorrhoids, herpes zoster and simplex, hyperlipidemia, asthma, and peptic ulcer disease. The patient's father had a myocardial infarction (MI) and passed away at an unspecified age.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Testosterone Cypionate, Fish Oil (Omega 3), Niacin, Diltiazem Extended-Release, Budesonide Nasal Inhaler, lysine, Vitamin E, Vitamin D, Aspirin, and Clindamycin. He has allergies to Penicillins, Tetracycline Analogues, Amoxicillin, IV Dye, Iodine Containing Contrast Media, Lactose, Statins-Hmg-Coa Reductase Inhibitors, and metoprolol.

Physical examination revealed no abnormal findings. However, lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (3.78), Hgb (12.2), Hct (37.8), MCV (100), MCH (32.3), RDW (15.6), RDWSD (57.1), and Calcium (7.8). 

During his hospital stay, the patient's pain was well managed with Tylenol and oxycodone. He was encouraged to ambulate and use the incentive spirometer. His diet was initially kept NPO after the procedure and was later advanced to a regular diet. A Foley catheter was removed at the time of discharge, and the patient was voiding without difficulty. His vital signs were monitored for signs of infection and fever, and he was started on/continued on antibiotics as needed. His blood levels were checked postoperatively to monitor for signs of bleeding. He was evaluated by Physical Therapy due to a history of tremor/postural tremor and unsteadiness on his feet. 

Upon discharge, the patient was clear and coherent, alert and interactive, and ambulatory - independent. He was discharged to extended care. His discharge instructions included information on what to expect post-surgery, medication instructions, activity restrictions, and when to call the office for certain symptoms.

note_context: While AVMs may occur sporadically in the general population, AVMs occur in high numbers in multiple organs in HHT patients. The most locations are distributed clinically relevant among the lungs (50%), the liver (30%) and the brain (10%) (Cottin et al. 2004; Fulbright et al. 1998; Piantanida et al. 1996). A further pulmo- nary manifestation of HHT is severe pulmonary artery hypertension (PAH) arising mainly from 2 sources: (1) high output heart failure secondary to hepatic AVM shunting and (2) primary PAH without signs of heart failure (Govani and Shovlin 2009).
note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of Hepatitis C-induced cirrhosis, was admitted due to a two-day history of subjective fevers, constant bifrontal headache, and lower back pain. He denied experiencing chills, decreased oral intake, loss of appetite, weight loss or gain, visual changes, photophobia, neck pain, or stiffness. He also reported an occasional nonproductive cough but denied shortness of breath, chest pain, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, dysuria, focal weakness, numbness, or tingling. Upon admission, the patient was given 1g IV Vancomycin, 2g IV CTX, and 600mg Motrin. The patient reported complete resolution of back pain and headache the following morning.

The patient's past medical history includes cirrhosis secondary to Hepatitis C, a history of CVA with mild residual right-sided weakness, heterozygosity for H63D for hemochromatosis, hypertension, and osteoporosis. His family history is significant for Alzheimer's disease in his mother and unspecified cancer in his father and brother.

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to have icteric sclera, minimal ascites, 2+ edema (R>L), and a petechial rash on the left lower extremity around the sock. His lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (3.58), Hgb (12.7), Hct (34.8), MCH (35.6), MCHC (36.6), Plt Ct (30), PTT (39.3), Glucose (119), ALT (41), AST (83), TotBili (4.9), Albumin (3.1), and Phos (2.5). His urine studies showed RBC of 6. Imaging studies showed bilateral pleural effusions, small pocket of ascites in the right lower quadrant, small right pleural effusion, and marked splenomegaly. No hydronephrosis was observed.

During his hospital stay, the patient developed acute renal failure, with creatinine levels increasing from 1.1 to 2.4 before trending down to 1.9. This was potentially due to the effects of medication or prerenal azotemia from dehydration. The patient was given NS boluses and IVF, which improved his renal function. His diuretics were held. His fever and headache resolved upon admission, and antibiotics were discontinued as a bacterial infection was deemed unlikely. His anemia remained stable, and his stools were guaiac negative. His B12 and folate levels were within normal limits. His osteoporosis was managed with continued Boniva.

Upon admission, the patient was on Ciprofloxacin, Folic acid, Furosemide, Boniva, Lactulose, Nadolol, Compazine, Rifaximin, Spironolactone, Caltrate plus, and MVI. His primary discharge diagnosis was acute renal failure, with secondary diagnoses of cirrhosis and Hepatitis C. His renal function was improving, and he was hemodynamically stable and afebrile upon discharge.

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of liver cirrhosis secondary to hepatitis C, ascites, and a recent umbilical hernia repair, presented with serosanguinal leakage from the incision. He denied experiencing fever, nausea, vomiting, or changes in bowel movements and was tolerating a regular diet. His past medical history includes cirrhosis, hepatitis C, a history of cerebrovascular accident with mild residual right-sided weakness, heterozygosity for H63D for hemochromatosis, hypertension, and osteoporosis. His cirrhosis has led to grade II varices, ascites requiring paracentesis every 2-4 weeks (though this is now well-controlled), hepatic encephalopathy, and spontaneous bacterial peritonitis on ciprofloxacin prophylaxis. His hepatitis C, genotype 1, has a viral load of 412,000 IU/mL and did not respond to interferon treatment due to thrombocytopenia.

The patient's family history is significant for Alzheimer's disease in his mother and unspecified cancer in his father and brother. On physical examination, the patient was found to have a 1x1cm opening at the umbilicus with serosanguinal discharge resembling ascites fluid, and chronic 2+ edema in the extremities, more pronounced on the right side. 

Lab results on admission showed abnormalities in WBC (3.8), RBC (3.01), Hgb (10.8), Hct (30.5), MCV (102), MCH (36.0), MCHC (35.5), RDW (15.3), Plt Ct (43), PTT (39.7), Glucose (132), UreaN (24), Creat (1.6), AST (54), TotBili (2.2), Albumin (2.8), and Calcium (8.3). At discharge, abnormalities were noted in WBC (2.4), RBC (2.87), Hgb (10.1), Hct (29.5), MCV (103), MCH (35.4), Plt Ct (47), and UreaN (22). 

The patient's incision was opened and drained at the bedside, and he was ordered to have normal saline wet to dry dressings twice daily. An abdominal ultrasound showed no drainable fluid collection. His medications upon admission included Cephalexin, Ciprofloxacin, Folic Acid, Furosemide, Ibandronate, Nadolol, Compazine, Rifaximin, Spironolactone, Calcium Carbonate-Vit D3, a multivitamin, and Omeprazole. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of seroma, and his condition was stable. He was instructed to keep his wound clean and dry, and to change his dressing twice daily. His doses of Lasix (furosemide) and aldactone (spironolactone) were decreased. He was advised to call his doctor or return to the ER for worsening pain, fever, serious changes in symptoms, or new symptoms. He was also advised not to drive or operate heavy machinery while taking any narcotic pain medication.

note_context: verted nares, and micrognathia (Fig. 13.1). Severe pulmonary emphysema usually becomes obvious in the first months of life. However, in some patients who survived beyond childhood, emphysema can be mild or even absent, even though lung function tests shows obstructive lung disease (Fig. 13.2) (Urban et al. 2009; Su et al. 2015). Precipitating factors to respiratory failure include recurrent respiratory infections, pulmo- nary hypertension, tracheomalacia, and positive pressure ventilation (Urban et al. 2009; Callewaert et al. 2013; Su et al. 2015). Bladder diverticula can be found in about 50% of patients, appear and enlarge progressively, and, in addition to ure- thral weakness and prolapse, contribute to void- ing problems. VUR and fragility of the collective system can induce hydronephrosis. Patients are at risk of upper and lower urinary tract infections (Urban et al. 2009; Callewaert et al. 2013). Gastrointestinal abnormalities are a discernable feature of LTBP4-related CL rarely
note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of liver cirrhosis secondary to hepatitis C, ascites, and a recent umbilical hernia repair, presented with serosanguinal leakage from the incision. He denied experiencing fever, nausea, vomiting, or changes in bowel movements and was tolerating a regular diet. His past medical history includes cirrhosis, hepatitis C, a history of cerebrovascular accident with mild residual right-sided weakness, heterozygosity for H63D for hemochromatosis, hypertension, and osteoporosis. His cirrhosis has led to grade II varices, ascites requiring paracentesis every 2-4 weeks (though this is now well-controlled), hepatic encephalopathy, and spontaneous bacterial peritonitis on ciprofloxacin prophylaxis. His hepatitis C, genotype 1, has a viral load of 412,000 IU/mL and did not respond to interferon treatment due to thrombocytopenia.

The patient's family history is significant for Alzheimer's disease in his mother and unspecified cancer in his father and brother. On physical examination, the patient was found to have a 1x1cm opening at the umbilicus with serosanguinal discharge resembling ascites fluid, and chronic 2+ edema in the extremities, more pronounced on the right side. 

Lab results on admission showed abnormalities in WBC (3.8), RBC (3.01), Hgb (10.8), Hct (30.5), MCV (102), MCH (36.0), MCHC (35.5), RDW (15.3), Plt Ct (43), PTT (39.7), Glucose (132), UreaN (24), Creat (1.6), AST (54), TotBili (2.2), Albumin (2.8), and Calcium (8.3). At discharge, abnormalities were noted in WBC (2.4), RBC (2.87), Hgb (10.1), Hct (29.5), MCV (103), MCH (35.4), Plt Ct (47), and UreaN (22). 

The patient's incision was opened and drained at the bedside, and he was ordered to have normal saline wet to dry dressings twice daily. An abdominal ultrasound showed no drainable fluid collection. His medications upon admission included Cephalexin, Ciprofloxacin, Folic Acid, Furosemide, Ibandronate, Nadolol, Compazine, Rifaximin, Spironolactone, Calcium Carbonate-Vit D3, a multivitamin, and Omeprazole. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of seroma, and his condition was stable. He was instructed to keep his wound clean and dry, and to change his dressing twice daily. His doses of Lasix (furosemide) and aldactone (spironolactone) were decreased. He was advised to call his doctor or return to the ER for worsening pain, fever, serious changes in symptoms, or new symptoms. He was also advised not to drive or operate heavy machinery while taking any narcotic pain medication.

note_context:
There are a few limitations in our study. All data were col- lected retrospectively from medical records and the selection of the treatment modality was dependent on the physician and pa- tients’ choice. Some of pneumothorax occurred in our MFS patients might not have been captured in this study, especially when the diagnosis and treatment of a patient were performed in another hospital. Our hospital is equipped with a multi-disciplinary clinic team designated to treat MFS patients. This could lead to a selec- tion bias, and as such, the patient population in this study may not be an adequate representation of MFS patients.

Conclusion
note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of cirrhosis secondary to Hepatitis C acquired from a blood transfusion, chronic thrombocytopenia, and hypertension, was admitted from the emergency department with concerning findings on a chest X-ray of bilateral pneumothorax (PTX) and a new left pleural effusion. The symptoms started two days prior to admission with progressively worsening shortness of breath followed by central/right-sided pleuritic chest pain associated with a dry cough. The patient denied associated fever, chills, or sputum production. He had no recent air travel, diving, or other changes in altitude or travel. No recent emesis or procedures such as paracentesis or surgery were reported. 

Upon admission, the patient was given vancomycin/zosyn for a possible infectious etiology. He also received his home doses of Spironolactone and Lasix and morphine IV with marked improvement in chest pain. However, he reported recurrence of chest pain since he received morphine several hours prior and persistent shortness of breath which was unchanged from earlier. 

The patient's past medical history includes cirrhosis secondary to Hepatitis C, a history of CVA with mild residual right-sided weakness, heterozygosity for H63D for hemochromatosis, hypertension, and osteoporosis. His family history is significant for Alzheimer's disease in his mother and an unspecified cancer in his father and brother. 

Abnormal lab results include Urea Nitrogen at 21, Creatinine at 1.3, Calcium at 8.3, RBC at 3.27, Hemoglobin at 11.5, Hematocrit at 32.5, MCV at 99, MCH at 35.0, MCHC at 35.2, RDW at 15.1, PTT at 36.0, and Platelet count at 34. Pleural fluid analysis showed 5000 WBCs, 44 Polys, 12 Lymphs, 29 Monos, 3 Eos, 2 Mesos, and 10 Macros. 

The patient underwent a thoracentesis procedure. The cause of the bilateral PTX was unclear, but it resolved with intervention and was no longer visible on a chest x-ray done later. The patient also had a new moderate to large left pleural effusion, which was drained and sent for further testing. The fluid had multiple WBCs and RBCs but no organisms, and cytology was negative for malignancy. The patient was observed off antibiotics, as he did not have any active signs of infection. 

The patient's dyspnea and chest pain were likely secondary to PTX. No ECG changes suggested ischemia, cardiac enzymes were negative, and no pulmonary embolism was found on CTA. The patient's cirrhosis was stable, and his thrombocytopenia was stable at baseline. His hypertension was well controlled on home medications of Lasix and Aldactone. 

Upon discharge, the patient was determined to not have tuberculosis, no longer had an oxygen requirement at rest and with ambulation, was afebrile, with stable vital signs, and had been deemed safe to leave the hospital by physical therapy. The patient was scheduled for follow-up in the pulmonary clinic.

note_context: Both conservative treatments such as supplemental oxygen therapy and chest tube drainage and surgical treatments such as blebectomy are used for pneumothorax. The general guidelines on the management of primary spontaneous pneumothorax were published by the British Thoracic Society (BTS) and the American College of Chest Physicians (ACCP) [4-5] . However, the manage- ment of pneumothorax is not fully standardized and the treatment modality varies depending on the radiological ﬁndings (X-ray or computed tomography) and clinical symptoms [6] . Moreover, these guidelines are particularly applicable to adults. Thus, to date the management of pneumothorax in children has been largely depen- dent on data obtained from studies in adults. Although recently some evidence has been collected gradually in children, there is a controversy in particular about the timing of surgery (primary or secondary), and the ways of conservative treatment (chest tube drainage, simple aspiration, or observation

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of Hepatitis C and Liver cirrhosis, was admitted due to complaints of difficulty with gait, which had been progressing since the previous day. He reported feeling weak and had significant difficulty moving, to the point where his legs gave out and he fell. He also reported a persistent tingling in his left hand for the past six days. He denied any weakness in his upper extremities or other parts of his body. He had recently received a flu shot and started a new medication (citalopram) for depression. 

His past medical history includes cirrhosis secondary to Hepatitis C, a history of CVA with mild residual right-sided weakness, heterozygosity for H63D for hemochromatosis, hypertension, and osteoporosis. His family history is significant for Alzheimer's disease in his mother and unspecified cancer in his father and brother.

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to have normal bulk bilaterally, normal tone, and no observed myoclonus or tremor or asteryxis. However, he had a positive right pronator drift and his right dorsiflexion did not have full range of motion. His reflexes were brisker on the right in the upper extremities compared to the left. His right patellae were somewhat brisker than the left. His toes were downgoing on the left and upgoing on the right. He was able to stand but felt very weak and had difficulty walking.

Lab results showed an elevated ammonia level of 108, lactate level of 2.6, glucose level of 136, creatinine level of 1.3, sodium level of 132, AST level of 53, total bilirubin level of 3.6, albumin level of 3.1, phosphate level of 2.6, WBC count of 3.3, RBC count of 3.34, hemoglobin level of 11.6, hematocrit level of 33.3, MCV of 100, MCH of 34.6, neutrophils of 77.9, eosinophils of 4.4, and platelet count of 34. His PTT was also elevated at 36.0. His urine test showed positive nitrite, trace protein, trace ketone, small bilirubin, and a urobilinogen level of 8. 

An MRI of the brain showed progressive confluent periventricular and subcortical T2 hyperintensities, likely reflecting progressive microvascular white matter ischemic disease. There was no acute infarct. 

The patient's hospital course was notable for inattention, bilateral proximal weakness with right hemiparesis, and right-sided hyperreflexia. There were also concerns about parkinsonian features including bradykinesia, hypomimia, and postural instability. His spinal imaging did not clearly support a structural myelopathy. His MRI brain did not reveal any significant acute pathology. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Nadolol, Ciprofloxacin, Folic Acid, Furosemide, Lactulose, Rifaximin, Spironolactone, Omeprazole, and Citalopram. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of bilateral lower extremity weakness, end-stage liver disease, and prior left basal ganglionic infarction. His condition upon discharge was stable, with right hemiparesis, bilateral proximal weakness, hypomimia, bradykinesia, hypertonia with cogwheeling at wrists left more than right, mild postural instability, and a right-sided spastic gait.

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to hematuria and shortness of breath. He has a history of Hepatitis C cirrhosis and is on the transplant list. He also has a history of CVA with mild residual right-sided weakness, heterozygous for H63D for hemochromatosis, hypertension, osteoporosis, and progressive weakness thought to be related to parkinsonism or manganism due to chronic liver disease. He has had multiple paracenteses, thoracenteses, and an ORIF of a hip fracture. 

Upon admission, the patient was found to have a platelet count of 39 and INR of 1.9, which is his baseline. His urine analysis showed evidence of hematuria without evidence of red cell casts. His chest X-ray showed a left-sided pleural effusion. He underwent thoracentesis due to continued shortness of breath and a large left pleural effusion. His respiratory status did not improve after 3.5 liters of fluid were removed. He fell and broke his left hip with a femoral neck fracture. He was intubated but had difficulty continuously desatting on his lateral side so the procedure was aborted. His oxygenation improved after a right paracentesis was done in PACU, with 2.5 liters of serosanguinous fluid removed.

His past medical history includes cirrhosis secondary to hepatitis C, for which he is listed for liver transplant with a MELD score of 19. He has a history of hepatic encephalopathy and SBP on cipro prophylaxis. He also has a history of CVA with mild residual right-sided weakness, heterozygous for H63D for hemochromatosis, hypertension, osteoporosis, and a history of PTX with pleural effusion thought to be transudative by Pulm in the clinic. His family history is significant for Alzheimer's disease in his mother and an unspecified cancer in his father and brother.

His labs showed ALT: 20, AP: 164, Tbili: 4.3, AST: 39, Lip: 44, Hct: 33.3, and INR: 1.9. His abdominal ultrasound showed a cirrhotic liver with sequela of portal hypertension including worsening ascites and unchanged splenomegaly. No focal hepatic lesion was found. His pleural fluid was transudative and negative for malignant cells. His CT head was non-con and showed no fracture or recent hemorrhage. His bilateral hip films showed a displaced left femoral neck fracture.

During his hospital stay, he had altered mental status secondary to hepatic/metabolic encephalopathy, an in-hospital fall resulting in a left femoral neck fracture requiring ORIF followed by an ICU stay after the procedure for hypotension requiring pressors and prolonged intubation, as well as multiple paracenteses and thoracenteses. His hematuria resolved spontaneously with no evidence of obstruction or clots. His thrombocytopenia/anemia was likely due to hepatic dysfunction and splenomegaly/splenic sequestration. He responded to 8 units of platelets and remained stable on discharge. His anemia was felt in part due to recent surgical intervention, recent hematuria, chronic disease, low-grade hemolysis, and DIC by labs. He was transfused aggressively with 12 units of pRBC in the setting of his recent surgery and concern for hypovolemic shock.

His medications upon admission included cipro, citalopram, folic acid, furosemide, lactulose, nadolol, compazine, rifaximin, spironolactone, calcium Carb - Vit D3, multivit, and omeprazole. He has no known drug allergies.

note_context: an individual with aortic aneurysm; MASS, myopia, mitral valve prolapse, borderline (Z<2) aortic root dilatation, striae, skeletal ﬁndings phenotype; MFS, Marfan syndrome; MVPS, mitral valve prolapse syndrome; Syst, systemic score (see box 2); and Z, Z-score.

His labs showed ALT: 20, AP: 164, Tbili: 4.3, AST: 39, Lip: 44, Hct: 33.3, and INR: 1.9. His abdominal ultrasound showed a cirrhotic liver with sequela of portal hypertension including worsening ascites and unchanged splenomegaly. No focal hepatic lesion was found. His pleural fluid was transudative and negative for malignant cells. His CT head was non-con and showed no fracture or recent hemorrhage. His bilateral hip films showed a displaced left femoral neck fracture.

During his hospital stay, he had altered mental status secondary to hepatic/metabolic encephalopathy, an in-hospital fall resulting in a left femoral neck fracture requiring ORIF followed by an ICU stay after the procedure for hypotension requiring pressors and prolonged intubation, as well as multiple paracenteses and thoracenteses. His hematuria resolved spontaneously with no evidence of obstruction or clots. His thrombocytopenia/anemia was likely due to hepatic dysfunction and splenomegaly/splenic sequestration. He responded to 8 units of platelets and remained stable on discharge. His anemia was felt in part due to recent surgical intervention, recent hematuria, chronic disease, low-grade hemolysis, and DIC by labs. He was transfused aggressively with 12 units of pRBC in the setting of his recent surgery and concern for hypovolemic shock.

His medications upon admission included cipro, citalopram, folic acid, furosemide, lactulose, nadolol, compazine, rifaximin, spironolactone, calcium Carb - Vit D3, multivit, and omeprazole. He has no known drug allergies.

The patient is a male with a history of hepatitis C cirrhosis, acquired via blood transfusion, which has led to grade I-II esophageal varices, episodes of spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP), and hepatic encephalopathy. He is listed for a liver transplant. Other past medical conditions include a history of cerebrovascular accident with mild residual right-sided weakness, heterozygosity for H63D for hemochromatosis, hypertension, osteoporosis, and a history of pleural effusion thought to be transudative. He also has progressive weakness thought to be related to parkinsonism or manganism due to chronic liver disease. 

The patient presented with fatigue and malaise, and had a scheduled therapeutic paracentesis but felt too weak to travel. He reported increasing fatigue over the past few days, diffuse abdominal pain, and weight gain. He denied fevers, chills, nausea, vomiting, melena, and bright red blood per rectum. His appetite was okay. Initial vital signs in the emergency department showed a fever of 102.4°F, heart rate of 83, blood pressure of 102/57, respiratory rate of 18, and oxygen saturation of 100% on 2 liters nasal cannula. Right upper quadrant ultrasound showed a patent portal vein. Diagnostic paracentesis showed a white blood cell count greater than 4000. Blood cultures were obtained, and the patient was given a dose of vancomycin and piperacillin-tazobactam. He was admitted to the ICU due to concern for his initial work of breathing. 

The patient's family history is significant for Alzheimer's disease in his mother and an unspecified cancer in his father and brother. 

Upon admission, the patient was alert, oriented, and in no acute distress. Physical examination revealed mildly icteric sclera, a soft and distended abdomen with tenderness in the left upper quadrant and lower quadrants without rebound or guarding, and normal neurologic examination. 

Laboratory results showed several abnormalities including low white blood cell count, red blood cell count, hemoglobin, hematocrit, and platelet count. The patient also had elevated neutrophils, lymphocytes, monocytes, eosinophils, and basophils. His glucose, urea nitrogen, and creatinine levels were elevated. Liver function tests showed elevated total bilirubin, aspartate aminotransferase, and gamma-glutamyl transferase. His phosphorus level was low. 

A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis showed a small bowel containing right inguinal hernia with dilated loops of mid and distal small bowel, moderate-sized left and small right pleural effusions, atherosclerotic disease, infrarenal aortic aneurysm, cholelithiasis, splenomegaly, intra-abdominal collateral vessels consistent with underlying portal hypertension, and a large right hydrocele. 

Blood cultures showed coagulase-negative Staphylococcus and Escherichia coli, both of which were sensitive to several antibiotics. Peritoneal fluid also showed Escherichia coli. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, hepatorenal syndrome, small bowel obstruction, coagulase-negative Staphylococcus bacteremia, and hepatitis C cirrhosis. He completed a course of antibiotics for the peritonitis and bacteremia. His creatinine levels remained stable but did not improve to his previous baseline level. He also had a partial small bowel obstruction due to a reducible inguinal hernia, which seemed to resolve over time. His hyponatremia resolved during admission with paracentesis and holding diuretics. 

Upon discharge, the patient was instructed to stop taking spironolactone, furosemide, ciprofloxacin, nadolol, and alendronate due to his kidney function and low blood pressure. His citalopram was increased from 10mg to 20mg daily, and he was started on cefpodoxime 100mg daily to prevent infection in his abdomen.

note_context: While AVMs may occur sporadically in the general population, AVMs occur in high numbers in multiple organs in HHT patients. The most locations are distributed clinically relevant among the lungs (50%), the liver (30%) and the brain (10%) (Cottin et al. 2004; Fulbright et al. 1998; Piantanida et al. 1996). A further pulmo- nary manifestation of HHT is severe pulmonary artery hypertension (PAH) arising mainly from 2 sources: (1) high output heart failure secondary to hepatic AVM shunting and (2) primary PAH without signs of heart failure (Govani and Shovlin 2009).

A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis showed a small bowel containing right inguinal hernia with dilated loops of mid and distal small bowel, moderate-sized left and small right pleural effusions, atherosclerotic disease, infrarenal aortic aneurysm, cholelithiasis, splenomegaly, intra-abdominal collateral vessels consistent with underlying portal hypertension, and a large right hydrocele. 

Blood cultures showed coagulase-negative Staphylococcus and Escherichia coli, both of which were sensitive to several antibiotics. Peritoneal fluid also showed Escherichia coli. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, hepatorenal syndrome, small bowel obstruction, coagulase-negative Staphylococcus bacteremia, and hepatitis C cirrhosis. He completed a course of antibiotics for the peritonitis and bacteremia. His creatinine levels remained stable but did not improve to his previous baseline level. He also had a partial small bowel obstruction due to a reducible inguinal hernia, which seemed to resolve over time. His hyponatremia resolved during admission with paracentesis and holding diuretics. 

Upon discharge, the patient was instructed to stop taking spironolactone, furosemide, ciprofloxacin, nadolol, and alendronate due to his kidney function and low blood pressure. His citalopram was increased from 10mg to 20mg daily, and he was started on cefpodoxime 100mg daily to prevent infection in his abdomen.

Furthermore, aneurysms are not restricted to the aorta, as cerebral, patent ductus arteriosus, and pulmonary artery aneurysms were also observed. Complete imaging from head to pelvis is therefore recommended in the management of this syndrome. Other cardiovascular features include atrial septal defect, aortic valve regurgita- tion, arterial tortuosity, and mitral valve prolapse, but these features have only been observed once (Meester et al. 2017).

12.3.3 Craniofacial Manifestations
note_summary:
The patient, a female of tall stature and thin build, presented to the emergency department with a four-day history of orthostatic headaches, which were worse when standing and improved when lying flat. The headaches were sometimes accompanied by nausea or a feeling of dysequilibrium when walking, and a 'muffled' sensation in her left ear. She had been previously diagnosed with a 'sinus headache' at an Urgent Care clinic and was prescribed Augmentin and Ibuprofen, which did not alleviate her symptoms. A head CT scan revealed a >10mm pituitary mass, likely a macroadenoma. The patient denied any prior history of headaches or endocrine symptoms such as menstrual irregularities, weight gain/loss, heat/cold intolerance, etc. She had a past medical history of an L4-5 bulging disk, for which she had received several epidural steroid injections. There was no family history of brain tumors, endocrine abnormalities, or migraines.

Upon admission, the patient was not on any medications. Her physical exam was largely unremarkable, with normal vitals and no signs of meningismus, edema, or rashes. Neurological examination was also normal, with no evidence of apraxia, neglect, or focal weakness. However, she did exhibit non-extinguishing horizontal nystagmus on endgaze bilaterally. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for Hgb (10.9), Hct (34.1), MCV (77), MCH (24.4), and LH (<1.0), and Cortisol (23.1). A pituitary protocol MRI recommended nonemergent evaluation for a high attenuation lesion within the sella turcica measuring 12 x 10 x 13 mm, likely representing a pituitary adenoma or other neoplasm. The differential included a Rathke's cleft cyst.

During her hospital stay, the patient's headache improved significantly following IV fluid resuscitation, and she was able to sit up, stand, and ambulate without severe headache pain. She was discharged home with instructions to maintain hydration, complete a course of antibiotics for presumed sinus disease, and follow up in the pituitary tumor clinic and ophthalmology for formal visual field testing. The primary discharge diagnosis was a headache secondary to dehydration and sinus disease, with the inability to rule out intracranial hypotension due to a CSF leak. A secondary diagnosis was a likely pituitary macroadenoma vs Rathke's cyst.

Diffuse peripheral arterial aneurysms are well known features of other connective tissue disorders such as the Loeys-Dietz syndrome (genes: TGFBR1, TGFBR2) and Ehlers-Danlos syndrome of the vas- cular type (gene: COL3A1). They have traditionally not been associated with MFS, but the results of two recent studies suggest otherwise. In a retro- spective analysis of about 140 MFS patients (all with FBN1 mutations) who had undergone routine thoraco-abdominal CT or MRI as part of their follow-up, about one third had incidental ﬁndings of peripheral vascular aneurysms. note_summary:
The patient is a left-handed male with a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, obstructive sleep apnea, chest pain, migraines, asthma, irritable bowel syndrome, hemorrhagic colitis, gastroesophageal reflux disease, Schatzki's ring, and eczema. He has also undergone an appendectomy. He presented to the emergency department with blurred vision, left arm and leg heaviness, abdominal pain, and difficulty opening his cell phone. His stroke scale score was 0, indicating no stroke symptoms. His physical examination was largely normal, with no evidence of apraxia or neglect. His neurological examination showed normal bulk and tone throughout, with no pronator drift, tremor, or asterixis noted. He had giveway weakness in his left fingers and decreased velocity of finger tap on the left side compared to the right. His sensory examination was intact.

His family history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. There is no family history of stroke, cancer, intracerebral aneurysms/arteriovenous malformations, connective tissue disorders, autoimmune disorders, or inflammatory disorders.

Upon admission, the patient was taking albuterol sulfate, esomeprazole magnesium, Combivent, lisinopril, simvastatin, aspirin, a multivitamin, and omega-3 fatty acids.

His lab results showed abnormal RBC count (3.93), hemoglobin (13.2), hematocrit (38.0), MCH (33.6), RBC count (4.39), MCH (32.1), neutrophils (83.5), lymphocytes (12.8), PTT (20.7), glucose (131), cholesterol (211), triglycerides (193), and cholesterol (204). His urine tested positive for cocaine.

His CT scan of the head showed no acute intracranial abnormality, but his MRI showed an early subacute infarct involving the junction of the left putamen and the lateral aspect of the posterior limb of the internal capsule. His MRA was unremarkable. His TTE and TEE showed no cardiac source of embolism, preserved global and regional biventricular systolic function, and no significant valvular abnormality.

During his hospital stay, the patient was evaluated for possible stroke. His CT scan did not show acute bleeding, but his MRI showed an early subacute infarct. The most likely mechanism of stroke was thought to be microvascular. His blood alcohol level was 191 and his urine tested positive for cocaine. He was advised to never use cocaine again. His lipid profile showed high triglycerides and his HbA1c was 5.7. His aspirin was stopped and he was started on Plavix. His dose of simvastatin was increased from 20 mg to 40 mg/day and he was started on verapamil for control of migraines.

note_context:
There are a few limitations in our study. All data were col- lected retrospectively from medical records and the selection of the treatment modality was dependent on the physician and pa- tients’ choice. Some of pneumothorax occurred in our MFS patients might not have been captured in this study, especially when the diagnosis and treatment of a patient were performed in another hospital. Our hospital is equipped with a multi-disciplinary clinic team designated to treat MFS patients. This could lead to a selec- tion bias, and as such, the patient population in this study may not be an adequate representation of MFS patients.

Conclusion
note_summary:
The male patient was admitted to the CARDIOTHORACIC service due to left chest pain and shortness of breath (SOB). He has a history of two previous episodes of left pneumothorax (PTX) within the last three months, with the last episode requiring chest tube placement. He was discharged two days after the last episode with plans to follow up with a thoracic surgeon, which he has yet to do. The patient reported recurrent symptoms of left PTX, including dyspnea on exertion (DOE), SOB, and left chest pain. He denied any recent illnesses and his review of systems (ROS) was otherwise negative. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent a left pleural pigtail catheter placement and a left thoracoscopy with bleb stapling and abrasive pleurodesis. His physical examination was largely normal, with the exception of decreased breath sounds in the left lung. His laboratory results showed an elevated white blood cell count (WBC-13.1*) and elevated absolute neutrophil (AbsNeut-8.16*) and absolute monocyte (AbsMono-1.17*) counts. His chest X-ray revealed a small left apical pneumothorax, which remained unchanged even after the removal of the left pleural drainage catheter. There was also mild left pleural thickening, but no pleural effusion. 

The patient's hospital course was stable. He tolerated the surgical procedures well and his pain was controlled with IV Dilaudid and Tylenol. His chest tube had no air leak and remained on suction. His pain medication was later changed to oral Dilaudid and Gabapentin was added. His wounds were healing well and his oxygen was weaned off with room air saturations of 97%. His chest tube was eventually removed and his post pull chest x-ray revealed a stable left apical pneumothorax. He was using his incentive spirometer with much encouragement and was ambulating independently. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking Chantix (varenicline) 1 mg orally daily. There is no family history of any known illnesses. The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of recurrent left pneumothorax. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory. He was instructed to follow up with Dr. ___ in 2 weeks.

2019 were included. All data were collected retrospectively from patients’ medical records. Data on patients’ characteristics, the clinical course of treatments, and relapse of pneumothorax were obtained. We divided all patients into two groups, conservative and surgical treatments groups. The conservative treatment groups in- cluded patients who were admitted to the hospital and treated by ways other than operative intervention. The surgical treatment groups included patients who had operation as ﬁrst-line or second- line therapy followed by conservative treatment. All numerical data are expressed as median (interquartile range), unless otherwise mentioned. Statistical analyses were performed using JMP®Pro 15 (SAS Institute Inc., Cary, NC, USA). The cumulative treatment-failure and relapse free rate are illustrated by the Kaplan-Meier sur- vival curves. The ethics committee at our institution approved this study. (2996- (6))

The patient, a female, was admitted due to acute onset back pain. She has a history of chronic abdominal and back pain for several years, which is attributed to previous surgeries. The pain was accompanied by profuse vomiting. Upon examination at the emergency department, a Type B aortic dissection was identified through a CT scan. Her systolic blood pressure was over 200 upon arrival, but responded to nitroprusside and esmolol. She continued to experience abdominal and back pain, although the severity decreased with the administration of narcotics.

Her past medical history includes myocardial infarction, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, cerebrovascular accident, prolonged QT, rectocele, hypertension, and conversion disorder presenting with signs and symptoms of cerebrovascular accident. She has undergone cholecystectomy, total abdominal hysterectomy/bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, and appendectomy. She lives at home and continues to smoke. Her son has schizoaffective disorder and her daughter has bipolar disorder.

Physical examination revealed mild distress, clear lungs, regular rate and rhythm in the cardiovascular system, and a soft, distended, and obese abdomen with diffuse tenderness, particularly in the epigastric region. No palpable masses were found. Her lower extremities had scabs but no edema or cyanosis. 

Lab results showed abnormal calcium levels at 8.1, and a CT angiogram confirmed the Type B aortic dissection, starting just past the takeoff of the left subclavian artery and extending down to above the iliac bifurcation. There was a discrepancy in kidney perfusion, with the right kidney receiving less blood than the left. The right kidney also showed signs of an evolving infarct.

During her hospital stay, the patient was monitored in the ICU for strict blood pressure control. She showed no signs of mesenteric or peripheral limb ischemia. Despite an infarcted right kidney, her creatinine levels remained stable. She was found to have a urinary tract infection on the third day of her hospital stay and was started on a three-day course of ciprofloxacin. She experienced intermittent confusion, which was investigated and resolved. Her abdominal and back pain improved over the course of her stay.

Upon admission, her medications included Lactulose, Fosamax, Caltrate 600, Bentyl, Albuterol, Ativan, Aspirin, Hydrochlorothiazide, Cyclobenzaprine, Vicodin, Cymbalta, and Fioricet.

note_context:
A minority of MFS patients (10%–20%) show an ectasia of the descended and abdominal aorta, which can lead to a type B dissection.7,8

Another important common manifestation of MFS is mitral valve prolapse (MVP).9,10 Although, only a minority of MFS patients will meet surgery as a consequence of MVP. MVP leads to complications such as severe mitral valve regurgitation, especially in adolescent women, and can result in heart failure and pulmonary hypertension.8 In addition, calcifica- tion of the mitral valve can already be present at a young age.9 However, the same clinical determi- nants (echocardiographic criteria, age, body mass index, and body surface area) that predict outcomes in idiopathic mitral valve prolapse also predict out- comes in mitral valve prolapse associated with MFS.11 Incidence of aortic dilatation and mitral pro- lapse in patients with MFS is comparable in children and adults of the same sex.12

note_context: Individuals with MFS are also at risk for aortic dissections originating at a different aortic location, just distal to the orgin of the left subclavian artery (a branch of aortic arch going to the brain) and progressing down the descending aorta, termed type B dissections (Figure 1). These dissections less commonly cause sudden death and occur with little to no enlargement at the site of origin of the dissection. Complications requiring surgery can occur with type B dissection and include malperfusion of spinal arteries leading to paresis and paraplegia, malperfusion of visceral arteries leading to abdominal pain, and aortic rupture. In individuals with MFS, initial presentation with a type B dissection is less frequent than presentation for prophylactic aneurysm repair or type A dissection7. However, the observed rate of type B aortic dissection after prophylactic repair is increasing143. Some risk factors for type B aortic dissections that have been recognized include previous
note_summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to sudden onset of midback pain radiating to the front, similar to a previous episode. She had been diagnosed with a Type B aortic dissection in the past and was managed non-operatively with strict blood pressure control. She was discharged on Hydrochlorothiazide 25mg BID, Losartan 50mg BID, and Toprol 100mg daily. The patient also reported nausea, vomiting, fatigue, shortness of breath with exertion, and leg weakness. Her systolic blood pressure was found to be in the 190s. A CTA showed extension of the dissection, leading to her admission to the vascular surgery service.

Her past medical history includes MI, COPD, CVA, prolonged QT, rectocele, HTN, and conversion disorder presenting with signs/symptoms of CVA. She has undergone CCY, TAH/BSO, and appy. She has allergies to Amoxicillin, Lipitor, ketorolac, Sporanox, Latex, Lasix, Amitriptyline, Benadryl, Sulfa (Sulfonamide Antibiotics), Erythromycin Base, and Haldol. Her family history is non-contributory.

Upon physical examination, her vitals were T: 98.9 degrees Fahrenheit, BP: 150s-160s/70s, HR 75 bpm, RR 16 bpm, O2: 91% on RA. Her general appearance was pleasant and calm. No abnormalities were noted in her HEENT, neck, CV, lungs, abdomen, and extremities. Neurologically, she was alert and oriented, with grossly intact cranial nerves and strength in all extremities.

Abnormal lab results included PLT COUNT-786, NEUTS-76.8, LYMPHS-16.9, WBC-11.4, RBC-3.47, HGB-10.5, HCT-30.7, GLUCOSE-109, SODIUM-131, and CHLORIDE-93. A CTA of the torso showed a contained aortic rupture at the proximal descending thoracic aorta with a small periaortic mediastinal hematoma, aneurysmal descending thoracic aorta up to 4.4 cm, interval progression of type B aortic dissection with compromised perfusion of the right kidney, and bilateral pulmonary granulomas and sub-4-mm nodules.

During her hospital stay, her blood pressure was monitored strictly and controlled with an IV nitroglycerin drip. Despite uptitration of her metoprolol and losartan, she remained hypertensive in the 160s. Her medications were switched to labetalol and lisinopril, which resulted in excellent control. She experienced recurrent abdominal pain after eating, which resolved after a bowel movement. At discharge, her blood pressure had been well controlled for 2 days, she was tolerating a regular diet without pain, and she was ambulating without difficulty.

Her medications upon admission were Famotidine 20 mg BID, Duloxetine 60 mg DAILY, Cyclobenzaprine 10 mg TID, Albuterol sulfate 2.5 mg /3 mL (0.083 %) One puff Q4H as needed for SOB/wheezing, Dicyclomine 20 mg QID as needed for GI spasms, Tramadol 50 mg Q4H as needed for pain, Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg DAILY, Losartan 50 mg BID, and Toprol 100 mg DAILY.

There are a few limitations in our study. All data were col- lected retrospectively from medical records and the selection of the treatment modality was dependent on the physician and pa- tients’ choice. Some of pneumothorax occurred in our MFS patients might not have been captured in this study, especially when the diagnosis and treatment of a patient were performed in another hospital. Our hospital is equipped with a multi-disciplinary clinic team designated to treat MFS patients. This could lead to a selec- tion bias, and as such, the patient population in this study may not be an adequate representation of MFS patients.

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of Asperger's syndrome, Tourette's syndrome, and recurrent pneumothorax, was admitted due to a first-time seizure. He was found by his father in a state of altered mental status, shaking, and post-ictal confusion/weakness. The patient had no history of febrile seizures as a child, encephalitis, or meningitis. He had undergone surgeries for recurrent pneumothorax. 

The patient's symptoms included right arm soreness, tongue biting, and generalized tonic-clonic movements. He was given 2 mg IV ativan after a second seizure episode. He was somnolent following the medication administration. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was thin, pale, and appeared younger than his stated age. He was somnolent, arousable to touch and noxious stimuli. Neurological examination revealed that he was unable to maintain wakefulness for a long time and unable to complete a full evaluation due to his current mental status. 

Lab results showed abnormal glucose levels at 107, chloride levels at 110, total CO2 at 20, AST(SGOT) at 49, LD(LDH) at 299, phosphate at 2.2, WBC at 12.1, RBC at 4.27, HGB at 13.0, HCT at 37.2, NEUTS at 88.0, LYMPHS at 6.9, and PTT at 21.3. MRI results showed no intracranial abnormality, specifically normal signal intensity in the bilateral mesial temporal regions. EEG and spinal fluid cytology results were pending. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was loaded with Keppra, initially at 750mg BID, which was increased to 1000mg BID after additional events. EEG monitoring showed no epileptiform activity while on Keppra. CSF obtained by lumbar puncture was unremarkable for infection or other abnormalities. The patient was also maintained on Guanfacine for his Tourette's motor tics. His mood and personality improved over hospital day #2. 

The patient was pancultured for an initial temperature of 100.3 at the outside hospital. Urinalysis and Gram Stain of the CSF were unremarkable. The patient's LFT were slightly elevated with an AST of 49 and LDH of 299. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Guanfacine 1 mg TID and Duocal 1 scoop QID. The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of generalized seizures. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was instructed to continue the Keppra for seizure control and follow up with appointments.

Data on patients’ characteristics, the clinical course of treatments, and relapse of pneumothorax were obtained. We divided all patients into two groups, conservative and surgical treatments groups. The conservative treatment groups in- cluded patients who were admitted to the hospital and treated by ways other than operative intervention. The surgical treatment groups included patients who had operation as ﬁrst-line or second- line therapy followed by conservative treatment. All numerical data are expressed as median (interquartile range), unless otherwise mentioned. Statistical analyses were performed using JMP®Pro 15 (SAS Institute Inc., Cary, NC, USA). The cumulative treatment-failure and relapse free rate are illustrated by the Kaplan-Meier sur- vival curves. The ethics committee at our institution approved this study. (2996- (6))

note_summary:
The patient, a female with a history of cryptogenic cirrhosis, was admitted due to gastrointestinal bleeding and thrombocytopenia. She reported seeing bright red blood in her stool for one day. She had a similar episode several years ago but has not recurred since. She was initially presented to an OSH, where she was noted to have a Hct of 28.5 with platelets of 33. She was transfused one unit of platelets and transferred for further evaluation. She denies abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting but does note some slight fatigue. She also notes intermittent diarrhea over the last month. 

Her past medical history includes cryptogenic cirrhosis with documented esophageal varices, splenectomy of unknown cause, type 2 diabetes mellitus, colonic polyps, pulmonary hypertension, hypertension, left rotator cuff injury, chronic kidney disease with baseline creatinine of 1.4, appendectomy, macular degeneration, congestive heart failure, gait disturbance, paraproteinemia, and total abdominal hysterectomy. Her family history includes her mother dying of a blood clot and her father dying of diphtheria. She has two deceased siblings, one from coronary artery disease.

Upon physical examination, she was alert, oriented, and in no acute distress. Her vitals were stable and her physical exam was largely unremarkable. Her labs showed a Hct of 28.1, platelets of 85, and creatinine of 1.6. She received pantoprazole 40mg IV x 1. 

Her abdominal ultrasound suggested a liver mass in segment V/VIII and showed a heterogeneous liver with slightly decreased portal venous flow consistent with cirrhosis. However, the MRI of the abdomen did not detect any focal hepatic lesions. The MRI also showed a few cystic lesions of the pancreas measuring up to 14 mm, renal cysts, and cholelithiasis. A TTE showed mild aortic valve stenosis, minimal mitral stenosis, mild pulmonary artery systolic hypertension, and normal biventricular cavity sizes with preserved global and regional biventricular systolic function. 

Her CBC showed consistently low RBC, Hgb, Hct, and platelet count. Her chemistry panel showed elevated urea nitrogen and creatinine, and low potassium. Her LFTs were largely normal, except for an elevated LDH. Her D-dimer was elevated, but her fibrinogen, haptoglobin, and LDH were normal. Her hepatitis serology was negative. 

She underwent an EGD and colonoscopy, which showed diverticulosis, rectal varices, and internal hemorrhoids, but no active bleeding. Her C. diff test was negative. Her thrombocytopenia is thought to be related to her hepatic disease, and she was transfused a total of 3 bags of platelets during her admission. Her medications upon admission included Protonix, Nadolol, MVI, Feosol, Glyburide, Calcium/Vitamin D, Colace, Lasix, and Lisinopril.

note_summary:
The patient is a female with a history of cryptogenic cirrhosis, thrombocytopenia, chronic kidney disease (CKD), type 2 diabetes mellitus (DM2), congestive heart failure (CHF), paraproteinemia, gait disturbance, and left rotator cuff injury. She was admitted due to sudden onset altered mental status (AMS) including confusion, sensation of heat, lightheadedness, slurred speech, and left hand weakness. This occurred after being discharged from a previous hospitalization for evaluation of anemia. 

Upon admission, her blood pressure was 212/84 and her blood glucose was 36. She was given D50 and became more alert, but was still not back to her normal self. Labs were notable for hematocrit of 26, platelets of 74K, and INR of 1.1. A non-contrast head CT showed a linear pontine left greater than right hemorrhage. 

Her physical exam revealed that she was awake, cooperative, and not in acute distress. She had normal bulk and tone throughout, with no pronator drift bilaterally. Her sensory exam showed intact light touch and cold sensation, but impaired vibratory sense bilaterally to the ankle and impaired proprioception at the great toes bilaterally. 

Abnormal lab results included RBC of 3.02*, Hgb of 9.0*, Hct of 27.4*, RDW of 15.9*, and Plt Ct of 58*. Her glucose was 106*, UreaN was 37*, and Creat was 1.3*. LD(LDH) was 266*. Calcium was 8.3*. 

A CT scan showed an unchanged pontine focal hyperdensity concerning for acute hemorrhage. An MRI of the brain without contrast revealed a chronic small left pontine hemorrhage. A chest X-ray showed no evidence of pneumonia but small bilateral pleural effusions were present. 

The patient's medications upon admission included nadolol, multivitamin, ferrous sulfate, glyburide, calcium + D, colace, furosemide, lisinopril, and pantoprazole. 

The patient's family history includes hypertension and coronary artery disease/peripheral vascular disease. There is no family history of cerebrovascular accident/transient ischemic attack, intracerebral aneurysms/arteriovenous malformation, connective tissue disorders, or autoimmune disorders. 

The patient's altered mental status was likely due to a hypoglycemic episode. Seizure was less likely given no loss of consciousness and subcortical symptoms, but still possible. The hyperdensity's etiology is uncertain, possibly calcification in chronic cirrhosis versus hypertensive bleed. The patient was neurologically stable at the time. 

During her hospital stay, she had an episode of hypoglycemia to 46. Her insulin sliding scale was discontinued and she was encouraged to eat all her meals. She was restarted on her glyburide. She was also started on PredniSONE 60 mg daily due to her history of thrombocytopenia. Prior to discharge, her platelet count increased to 74. 

At discharge, her neurologic exam was significant for being at her baseline mental status and able to ambulate with assistance. Her discharge diagnoses included pontine hemorrhage (chronic), thrombocytopenia (unknown cause), chronic kidney disease, anemia, hypertension, and diabetes.

note_context: (Figure 1)9. In the majority of MFS patients, thoracic aortic disease begins as an asymptomatic enlargement of the aortic root, which progressively enlarges over time to form an aneurysm (a weakening of the artery wall leading to a bulge or distention). Medications can slow the rate of enlargement but do not prevent it. The aortic aneurysm becomes unstable as it enlarges and ultimately may lead to an acute ascending aortic dissection (known as the type A dissection based on Stanford classification), which is a life-threatening complication of MFS and can lead to decreased life expectancy 10,11. A dissection is a tear in the inner lining (intima) of the aorta that allows blood to enter the wall of the aorta and split through the middle layer (media) of the wall, causing the layers of the aorta to separate or dissect. (Figure 1) Type A aortic dissections are associated with high morbidity and mortality, and prior to the availability of aortic surgery, a large proportion of individuals
note_summary:
The patient, a female with a history of diabetes mellitus, congestive heart failure, cryptogenic cirrhosis as noted by CT/US, status post splenectomy, and thrombocytopenia, presented to the emergency department from the clinic for gastrointestinal bleeding. She had recently been admitted for gastrointestinal bleeding and thrombocytopenia. During that admission, her colonoscopy showed sigmoid diverticulosis, large rectal varices without signs of recent bleeding, and grade 1 internal hemorrhoids. She was also evaluated for thrombocytopenia, with the most likely causes thought to be idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP) and cirrhosis. 

On the morning of admission, she had a large loose bowel movement with bright red blood mixed with dark colored stool. She reported having loose stools once to twice daily over the past several months, which were always dark due to her iron supplement. She also reported feeling more drained in the past week with increased fatigue and mild dyspnea on exertion and occasional lightheadedness. 

Upon physical examination, her rectal exam was notable for maroon stools, guaiac positive. Her labs showed leukocytosis (WBC 20.2), low hematocrit (23.4), platelet count of 103, INR 0.9, and elevated BNP (3645). She was given pantoprazole 40mg IV and started on octreotide given her history of rectal varices. 

In the ICU, a flexible sigmoidoscopy showed large rectal varices without bleeding, and ethiodol injection with hemostasis in the rectal varices was performed. Her hematocrit remained stable and she received 1 unit of packed red blood cells. Her anti-hypertensives were held overnight and then resumed, with furosemide 40 mg being held currently. 

Her past medical history also includes anemia possibly related to chronic renal failure, hypertension, pulmonary hypertension, mild aortic stenosis, mild mitral stenosis, mild mitral regurgitation on echo, chronic kidney disease with a baseline creatinine of 1.4, paraproteinemia, left rotator cuff injury status post motor vehicle accident, status post appendectomy, left macular degeneration, history of gait disturbance, and status post total abdominal hysterectomy and unilateral oophorectomy. 

Her family history is positive for hypertension and coronary artery disease/peripheral vascular disease, but negative for cerebrovascular accident/transient ischemic attack, intracerebral aneurysms/arteriovenous malformations, connective tissue disorders, and autoimmune disorders. 

Abnormal lab results on admission included elevated WBC (20.2), low RBC (2.47), low hemoglobin (7.2), low hematocrit (23.4), low MCHC (30.6), elevated neutrophils (86.9), low lymphocytes (8.7), elevated urea nitrogen (66), elevated creatinine (1.5), and elevated total protein (6.3). Abnormal lab results on discharge included low RBC (3.11), low hemoglobin (8.8), low hematocrit (27.7), elevated RDW (16.2), elevated glucose (183), elevated urea nitrogen (60), elevated creatinine (1.3), low chloride (109), and low calcium (7.7). 

Her chest x-ray showed increasing prominence of interstitial markings consistent with developing pulmonary edema and small pleural effusions. A CT of the head without contrast showed a focal hyperdensity within the midline pons, unchanged in appearance compared to a prior study. 

Her medications upon admission included ferrous sulfate, prednisone, nadolol, glyburide, furosemide, lisinopril, pantoprazole, amlodipine, colace, and calcium + D.

note_summary:
The patient, a female with a history of diabetes mellitus, congestive heart failure, cryptogenic cirrhosis as noted by CT/US, status post splenectomy, and thrombocytopenia, presented to the emergency department from the clinic for gastrointestinal bleeding. She had recently been admitted for gastrointestinal bleeding and thrombocytopenia. During that admission, her colonoscopy showed sigmoid diverticulosis, large rectal varices without signs of recent bleeding, and grade 1 internal hemorrhoids. She was also evaluated for thrombocytopenia, with the most likely causes thought to be idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP) and cirrhosis. 

On the morning of admission, she had a large loose bowel movement with bright red blood mixed with dark colored stool. She reported having loose stools once to twice daily over the past several months, which were always dark due to her iron supplement. She also reported feeling more drained in the past week with increased fatigue and mild dyspnea on exertion and occasional lightheadedness. 

Upon physical examination, her rectal exam was notable for maroon stools, guaiac positive. Her labs showed leukocytosis (WBC 20.2), low hematocrit (23.4), platelet count of 103, INR 0.9, and elevated BNP (3645). She was given pantoprazole 40mg IV and started on octreotide given her history of rectal varices. 

In the ICU, a flexible sigmoidoscopy showed large rectal varices without bleeding, and ethiodol injection with hemostasis in the rectal varices was performed. Her hematocrit remained stable and she received 1 unit of packed red blood cells. Her anti-hypertensives were held overnight and then resumed, with furosemide 40 mg being held currently. 

Her past medical history also includes anemia possibly related to chronic renal failure, hypertension, pulmonary hypertension, mild aortic stenosis, mild mitral stenosis, mild mitral regurgitation on echo, chronic kidney disease with a baseline creatinine of 1.4, paraproteinemia, left rotator cuff injury status post motor vehicle accident, status post appendectomy, left macular degeneration, history of gait disturbance, and status post total abdominal hysterectomy and unilateral oophorectomy. 

Her family history is positive for hypertension and coronary artery disease/peripheral vascular disease, but negative for cerebrovascular accident/transient ischemic attack, intracerebral aneurysms/arteriovenous malformations, connective tissue disorders, and autoimmune disorders. 

Abnormal lab results on admission included elevated WBC (20.2), low RBC (2.47), low hemoglobin (7.2), low hematocrit (23.4), low MCHC (30.6), elevated neutrophils (86.9), low lymphocytes (8.7), elevated urea nitrogen (66), elevated creatinine (1.5), and elevated total protein (6.3). Abnormal lab results on discharge included low RBC (3.11), low hemoglobin (8.8), low hematocrit (27.7), elevated RDW (16.2), elevated glucose (183), elevated urea nitrogen (60), elevated creatinine (1.3), low chloride (109), and low calcium (7.7). 

Her chest x-ray showed increasing prominence of interstitial markings consistent with developing pulmonary edema and small pleural effusions. A CT of the head without contrast showed a focal hyperdensity within the midline pons, unchanged in appearance compared to a prior study. 

Her medications upon admission included ferrous sulfate, prednisone, nadolol, glyburide, furosemide, lisinopril, pantoprazole, amlodipine, colace, and calcium + D.

note_context:
There are a few limitations in our study. All data were col- lected retrospectively from medical records and the selection of the treatment modality was dependent on the physician and pa- tients’ choice. Some of pneumothorax occurred in our MFS patients might not have been captured in this study, especially when the diagnosis and treatment of a patient were performed in another hospital. Our hospital is equipped with a multi-disciplinary clinic team designated to treat MFS patients. This could lead to a selec- tion bias, and as such, the patient population in this study may not be an adequate representation of MFS patients.

note_summary:
The patient is a male with minimal past medical history who presented with left-sided chest pain that radiated up to his left shoulder blade. The pain was worse with deep breaths, felt sharp, and was accompanied by shortness of breath. The pain did not go away over the course of the day and had no relationship to movement or exertion. The patient denied any diaphoresis, presyncope, syncope, nausea, or abdominal pain. There was no recent history of congestion, cough, or viral syndrome. The patient had previously been seen in the ED for atypical left-sided abdominal pain and was discharged on famotidine after a benign CT abdomen. 

Upon admission, the patient was given 80 mg enoxaparin SC, 325 mg ASA, started on a nitroglycerin drip, and morphine *1. His pain improved dramatically with these interventions. In the ED, his vitals were T 98.6, P 74, BP 140/70, O2 Sat 100% on 2L. He was continued on NTG drip. His pain got worse briefly and he was given one dose of morphine 4 mg IV with improvement. Cardiology evaluated his ECG and were unimpressed. 

The patient's lab results showed a D-DIMER of 223, GLUCOSE of 100, UREA N of 16, CREAT of 1.0, SODIUM of 138, POTASSIUM of 4.6, CHLORIDE of 101, TOTAL CO2 of 28, ANION GAP of 14, CK(CPK) of 153, cTropnT of <0.01, CK-MB of 3, WBC of 8.5#, RBC of 4.91, HGB of 15.0, HCT of 42.8, MCV of 87, MCH of 30.5, MCHC of 34.9, RDW of 13.3, PLT COUNT of 216, NEUTS of 59.8, MONOS of 4.7, EOS of 2.6, and BASOS of 1.1. 

A chest x-ray revealed a linear structure at the right lung apex, which was confirmed to be a right apical pneumothorax. However, a subsequent PA/LAT CXR showed that the lungs were fully expanded and clear, with no pneumothorax or pleural effusion. 

The patient's chest pain was likely caused by a spontaneous primary pneumothorax (PSP). He had no risk factors for PSP, including no family history of PSP, was a lifetime nonsmoker, and had no history of any genetic disorders. ACS and PE were ruled out due to poor story for angina, no EKG changes, negative enzymes, no risk factors, and a negative D-dimer. The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of right apical pneumothorax, in a stable, afebrile, chest pain-free condition. His medication upon admission was famotidine OTC PRN.

note_summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to a long-standing history of various symptoms including headache, inattention, generalized fatigue, sleep disturbance, memory loss, anxiety, OCD-like behavior, arthralgias, and myalgias. These symptoms began after an febrile illness associated with extreme fatigue, diagnosed as Mono, around the same time her parents were diagnosed with a condition based on a classic EM rash and history of tick bite. The patient was not treated despite similar exposure. She has seen many medical providers and been admitted multiple times in the past. Her symptoms have become more persistent and consistent, leading to her inability to obtain a job after completing esthetician school. She can perform ADL's, but has difficulty with iADL's, so she has moved back with her parents. All her prior workup had been negative, and her symptoms were attributed to psychosomatic, and she has been on many psychiatric medications. 

During her stay, she underwent a lumbar puncture and was started on high-dose CTX. The lumbar puncture showed no WBC's, normal protein, and normal glucose. She also received 2gm IV ceftriaxone. Her other routine labs were normal. A PET-CT head scan showed mild relative hypometabolism in the anteromedial temporal lobes bilaterally. 

She denies fevers, night sweats, focal weakness, numbness or tingling but endorses a 10 lb weight loss over a vague duration. She also notes lots of stretch marks on her skin, but did have intentional weight loss of 100 lbs in the past, after putting on 100 lbs due to side effects of Prozac. She has also developed a tic of her jaw, which causes significant discomfort at her left TMJ. She denies any bowel or bladder incontinence. Her parents report a withdrawn personality, which is a dramatic difference from her childhood personality and has worsened with time.

Her past medical history includes anxiety, depression, and migraines. She has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. Upon admission, she was on Topiramate (Topamax) 100 mg PO DAILY, Amphetamine-Dextroamphetamine 15 mg PO QAM:PRN fatigue, Venlafaxine XR 37.5 mg PO DAILY, ClonazePAM ___ mg PO QHS:PRN sleep, and Naltrexone Dose is Unknown  PO Frequency is Unknown. 

Her physical exam showed no nuchal rigidity, no murmur, no crackles, no edema, and no erythematous rashes. She had many linear stretch marks / striae on her abdomen, back, and all extremities. She was alert and oriented, with no asterixis, and fluent speech. She appeared mildly anxious and easily frustrated. 

The patient was discharged to extended care with a diagnosis of headache and fatigue. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory and independent.

note_context: problems arise. In many instances, the prevalence and rate of progression of such issues have not been studied adequately, and for most, no effective therapies have been tested. With age, fluid-filled cysts can develop in the kidneys or liver in individuals with MFS. Typically, these cysts are painless and cause no functional problems, and they are often incidental findings on radiologic imaging of the aorta172. Due in part to laxity of the oropharyngeal musculature and a receded mandible, obstructive sleep apnoea is common 173. It typically presents with snoring, but sleep-disordered breathing can contribute to fatigue and difficulty with mental functions. In women, urogenital laxity occurs at younger ages and produces all of the typical problems, such as incontinence. Management can be quite inadequate and frustrating.
