note_summary:
The male patient was admitted due to erythema at a previously removed IV site, with concerns of cellulitis or thrombophlebitis, and neutropenia. He has a history of Acute Myelogenous Leukemia (AML) and is currently on clofarabine/ara-C. He had a line pulled from a previous IV site that had become erythematous. He reported no fever, recent infections, or fevers, and the site on his left arm was tender to touch but stable. He also reported a resolving rash, mainly located in the inferior part of his abdomen, and left lower extremity swelling with signs of petechiae. He had a recent admission for lower extremity DVT on the left and was found to have PE. He was on anticoagulation with lovenox but this was stopped due to low counts.

His past medical history includes AML, right cataract surgery, left cataract, hereditary hemochromatosis, and DVT. His family history includes a mother with cervical cancer, a sister with lung cancer and a brain tumor of unknown etiology, and multiple brothers who passed away from coronary disease.

Upon physical examination, he had a macular rash in the inferior portion of his abdomen, a nodule on his left upper extremity, and petechiae on his left lower extremity. His discharge physical exam showed improved left upper extremity erythema and induration.

His admission labs showed abnormal values for WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCH, MCHC, RDW, Plt Ct, Neuts, and Lymphs. His discharge labs showed abnormal values for WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCH, MCHC, Plt Ct, Neuts, Lymphs, Atyps, and Glucose. His imaging results showed findings compatible with superficial thrombophlebitis in the left antecubital fossa and enlargement of the cardiac silhouette without definite vascular congestion.

During his hospital stay, he was started on vanc/cefepime for presumed cellulitis. He spiked one low-grade temperature but remained afebrile since then. His cellulitis significantly improved during his stay, and he was switched to augmentin and levaquin. He was discharged on augmentin and cipro for 2 weeks. His medications upon admission included ondansetron, docusate sodium, multivitamin with minerals, omeprazole, and fluconazole.

His primary discharge diagnosis was cellulitis and recurrent Acute Myelogenous Leukemia, with a secondary diagnosis of cataracts. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory upon discharge.

note_context: 8.2.2.6 Cardiovascular Manifestations Cardiovascular disease is the primary determi- nant of lifespan in MFS patients (van Karnebeek et al. 2001). The phenotype varies widely by age of presentation and severity of disease. The spectrum of MFS-related cardiovascular disease includes aneurysmal dilation of the aortic root, and proximal pulmonary arteries, thoracic aortic dissection and aneurysm, mitral and tricuspid valve prolapse, cardiomyopathy, and supraven- tricular arrhythmias (Figueiredo et al. 2001; Takeda et al. 2016). Of these, aortic aneurysm and dissection are the most immediately life- threatening (Groth et al. 2018). In the early 1970’s, the outcomes of emergent surgical repair of the aortic valve and root in MFS patients were unacceptably poor. With the development of the Bentall procedure (Bentall and De Bono 1968), which was first performed on a patient with MFS, outcomes were dramati- cally improved and use of this composite graft (Bentall) procedure, rapidly became the
note_summary:
The patient is a male with a history of relapsed Acute Myelogenous Leukemia (AML), now in remission after induction with clofarabine & ARA-C and one cycle of consolidation. He was admitted for an allogenic stem cell transplant (allo SCT). He reported feeling well with no acute complaints, except for slight soreness where his tunneled line was placed after admission. He had dental work done, fillings on 4 teeth, and has healed well from this. He denies pain, soreness, or trouble chewing/swallowing. He reported a good appetite and eating well. His lower extremity edema has resolved, and he is able to wear his regular shoes without difficulty. He continued on Lovenox injections to treat his Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) until the day before admission. 

His past medical history includes right cataract surgery, left cataract, hereditary hemochromatosis, DVT, Inferior Vena Cava (IVC) filter placement, and a history of syncopal episodes related to voiding vasovagal episodes. His family history includes a mother with cervical cancer, a sister with lung cancer and a brain tumor of unknown etiology, and multiple brothers who passed away from coronary disease.

Upon admission, his physical exam was normal except for 2+ pretibial bilateral edema, slightly greater on the left than the right. His labs showed abnormal values for WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDW, and Plt. His glucose levels were also elevated, and he had abnormal ALT, AST, LD(LDH), and TotBili levels. His urine test showed the presence of RBCs.

During his hospital stay, he received his allo SCT without complications. His conditioning for the allo SCT included Fludarabine, busulfan, and ATG rabbit. He had a transient reaction to the first dose of ATG rabbit, but tolerated the remainder of his treatments well. He was transitioned from IV to PO cyclosporine without issue and was started on fluconazole, bactrim, acyclovir, and ursodiol prophylaxis prior to discharge.

His medications upon admission included amlodipine, enoxaparin, ondansetron, docusate sodium, a multivitamin, and omeprazole. His discharge diagnosis was Allogenic Stem Cell Transplant and AML. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory upon discharge.

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of Acute Myelogenous Leukemia (AML), was admitted due to abnormal liver function tests (LFTs). He had previously undergone a mini-allo stem cell transplant for relapsed AML. He reported low-grade fevers, decreased energy levels, and mild throat and sinus discomfort, which he attributed to seasonal allergies. He denied any alcohol use, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, rash, skin changes, shortness of breath, or mouth pain. 

His past medical history includes multiple rounds of chemotherapy, a diagnosis of hereditary hemochromatosis, a history of syncopal episodes related to voiding vasovagal episodes, and a history of deep vein thrombosis (DVT). He has also undergone right cataract surgery, inferior vena cava (IVC) filter placement, and endoscopy and colonoscopy procedures. His family history includes a mother with cervical cancer, a sister with lung cancer and a brain tumor of unknown etiology, and multiple brothers who passed away from coronary disease.

Upon admission, the patient underwent a liver biopsy and a bone marrow biopsy. His physical examination was largely unremarkable, with no acute distress noted. His vital signs were stable. 

Admission labs showed abnormal values for RBC (2.87), Hgb (10.3), Hct (31.2), MCV (109), MCH (35.8), Neuts (77), Lymphs (10), Atyps (1), Metas (1), Myelos (1), and PTT (39.3). His liver function tests were also abnormal, with elevated ALT (140), AST (116), LD (349), and AlkPhos (175). 

During his hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with Post-Transplant Lymphoproliferative Disorder (PTLD), confirmed by liver biopsy. His immunosuppression drugs were decreased to allow his immune system to fight the virus causing PTLD. He also had a fever and was started on antibiotics. His LFTs improved and he was restarted on his home medications. 

Discharge labs showed continued abnormalities in RBC (2.44), Hgb (8.5), Hct (26.2), MCV (107), MCH (34.9), RDW (15.8), Neuts (42.9), Lymphs (48.0), PTT (40.2), ALT (78), LD (264), AlkPhos (213), and Calcium (8.3). His glucose was also elevated (114) and he tested positive for Smooth Muscle Antibodies. 

Imaging studies, including a liver ultrasound, chest x-ray, CT sinus, and PET scan, were largely unremarkable, with the exception of a mucous retention cyst in the left maxillary sinus noted on the CT sinus and multilevel FDG avid lymph nodes noted on the PET scan, which were compatible with PTLD. 

Upon discharge, the patient was instructed to follow up with his oncologist for ongoing management of his PTLD. His medications upon admission included acyclovir, amlodipine, cyclosporine, enoxaparin, fluconazole, folic acid, lorazepam, sulfamethoxazole-trimethoprim, ursodiol, magnesium oxide, and a multivitamin. His cyclosporin dosage was decreased during his hospital stay.

Discharge labs showed continued abnormalities in RBC (2.44), Hgb (8.5), Hct (26.2), MCV (107), MCH (34.9), RDW (15.8), Neuts (42.9), Lymphs (48.0), PTT (40.2), ALT (78), LD (264), AlkPhos (213), and Calcium (8.3). His glucose was also elevated (114) and he tested positive for Smooth Muscle Antibodies. 

Imaging studies, including a liver ultrasound, chest x-ray, CT sinus, and PET scan, were largely unremarkable, with the exception of a mucous retention cyst in the left maxillary sinus noted on the CT sinus and multilevel FDG avid lymph nodes noted on the PET scan, which were compatible with PTLD. 

Upon discharge, the patient was instructed to follow up with his oncologist for ongoing management of his PTLD. His medications upon admission included acyclovir, amlodipine, cyclosporine, enoxaparin, fluconazole, folic acid, lorazepam, sulfamethoxazole-trimethoprim, ursodiol, magnesium oxide, and a multivitamin. His cyclosporin dosage was decreased during his hospital stay.

note_summary:
The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted due to nausea, vomiting, abdominal distention, and anorexia. He has a history of relapsed Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) after treatment with clofarabine & ARA-C and one cycle of consolidation. He is now 100 days post an unrelated allogenic hematopoietic stem cell transplant. His post-transplant course has been complicated by Post-Transplant Lymphoproliferative Disorder (PTLD) diagnosed by a liver biopsy, a high EBV viral load, and marrow involvement on biopsy. His EBV viral load declined after Velcade/Rituxan treatment. He also has a history of hereditary hemochromatosis, Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT), and micturational vasovagal syncope. He has undergone right cataract surgery and has a left cataract. 

The patient's family history includes a mother with cervical cancer, a sister with lung cancer and a brain tumor of unknown etiology, and multiple brothers who passed away from coronary disease. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking oxycodone 5mg as needed for back pain and amlodipine 5mg for hypertension. He also reported taking acyclovir, cyclosporine, fluconazole, folic acid, hydrocortisone, lorazepam, sulfameth/trimethoprim, ursodiol, docusate sodium, magnesium, and multivitamins with minerals. 

The patient's symptoms included constipation, abdominal distention, decreased appetite, nausea, and vomiting. He also experienced one episode of lightheadedness. He denied having fevers, chills, jaundice, peripheral edema, or headache. 

Abnormal lab results included elevated urea nitrogen and creatinine levels, indicating acute kidney injury likely due to pre-renal etiology. This responded to IV fluids and increased oral fluid intake. His creatinine level decreased from 1.7 on admission to 0.9 upon discharge. Other abnormal lab results included low red blood cell count, hemoglobin, hematocrit, and platelet count, as well as elevated neutrophils, monocytes, and myelocytes. 

Imaging revealed gaseous distention of some loops of large colon, consistent with ileus. There was no evidence of free intraperitoneal air. A CT scan of the abdomen showed mild distention of the large bowel with gas and no obstruction. 

The patient was diagnosed with acute colonic pseudo-obstruction, likely related to his chemotherapy treatment. He was treated with supportive care, including IV fluids and laxatives, and his condition resolved. He was also diagnosed with hypercalcemia, which downtrended with IV hydration. 

Upon discharge, the patient was clear and coherent, alert and interactive, and able to move independently. He was instructed to continue drinking plenty of water and fluids at home.

note_summary:
The patient is a male with a history of relapsed Acute Myelogenous Leukemia (AML) after clofarabine & ARA-C and one cycle of consolidation. He underwent a hematopoietic stem cell transplant and his post-transplant course has been complicated by Post-Transplant Lymphoproliferative Disorder (PTLD) confirmed on liver biopsy. He was previously admitted with colonic ileus thought to be secondary to Velcade therapy, which resolved with IVF and laxatives. He was found to be febrile during a clinic visit and was admitted for workup. He also has a history of hereditary hemochromatosis, Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT), cataract surgery, and micturational vasovagal syncope.

His family history includes a mother with cervical cancer, a sister with lung cancer and a brain tumor of unknown etiology, and multiple brothers who passed away from coronary disease.

During this admission, he presented with a fever and an erythematous line insertion site. A CT chest showed new peripheral consolidations in the left upper lobe and left lower lobe consistent with multifocal pneumonia. He was started on vancomycin, cefepime, and azithromycin to cover atypical organisms. His workup for pneumococcus and legionellosis was negative. He remained afebrile, had no cough or chest pain, and maintained a good appetite and regular bowel movements.

His admission labs showed abnormal RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDW, and LD(LDH). His discharge labs showed abnormal WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDW, Neuts, Lymphs, LD(LDH), IgG, and IgA.

His medications upon admission included Acyclovir, Folic Acid, Lorazepam, Oxycodone, Sulfameth/Trimethoprim, Omeprazole, Ursodiol, Milk of Magnesia, Amlodipine, Docusate Sodium, Mg-Plus-Protein, Multivitamins, and CycloSPORINE.

His discharge diagnosis included pneumonia, acute myelogenous leukemia, and post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory and independent. He was instructed to start levofloxacin, an antibiotic, for 7 more days of treatment.

note_summary:
The patient is a male with a history of Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) who underwent allogeneic Bone Marrow Transplant (BMT) and Donor Lymphocyte Infusion. He presented to the clinic with symptoms of headache, malaise, and rash. He had been feeling unwell for several weeks, experiencing intermittent headaches, trouble sleeping, decreased appetite, and a general feeling of being cold. He also reported a scratchy throat, nasal congestion, and some nausea. He had a rash on his chest and the soles of both feet, and some scattered red spots on both arms. The patient's past medical history includes right cataract surgery, left cataract, Hereditary Hemochromatosis, and two instances of Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT). His family history includes a grandson with Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL), a mother with cervical cancer, a sister with lung cancer and a brain tumor, and multiple brothers who died from Coronary Artery Disease (CAD).

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to have inspiratory and expiratory wheezing heard on the left, lower than the upper lobe, and expiratory wheeze heard on the right. He had a diffuse, erythematous, blanching rash on his chest and both plantar surfaces were erythematous and warm. His lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (4.21), MCH (34.0), Neuts (77.1), Lymphs (11.9), UreaN (23), and LD(LDH) (261). Imaging results showed no evidence of acute or subacute intracranial process, but mucosal thickening was noted in the left frontal and maxillary sinus, suggesting an ongoing inflammatory process. His CT scan showed chronic-appearing inflammatory disease involving the left maxillary sinus, with occlusion of its ostiomeatal complex, as well as the left fronto-ethmoidal recess, where there may be an acute component. 

The patient's hospital course was complicated by several issues. He was initially suspected to have viral meningitis due to his presenting symptoms, but his CSF studies were negative. He was empirically started on IV acyclovir, which was discontinued after two days when CSF studies came back negative for HSV. He was given a 7-day course of doxycycline for sinusitis seen on MRI and CT. His headaches resolved following this. He was also diagnosed with graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) of the skin and gut. His rash showed some improvement with initiation of IV methylprednisolone for gut GVHD and eventually resolved with GVHD therapy. His diarrhea did not improve with treatment for C. diff infection, and he was started on IV methylprednisolone, made NPO for bowel rest, and TPN initiated. He underwent upper endoscopy which showed extensive erosions from the stomach to the duodenum. Biopsies were consistent with GVHD. He was increased to 80mg BID methylprednisolone and also placed on 1500mg BID Cellcept, and 3mg TID oral budesonide. After several days of no effect on stool output, Etanercept was started. His stool output decreased to around 2L/day, however his abdomen became increasingly distended and firm. CT abdomen showed diffuse gut inflammation from esophagus to rectum. He also became hypoalbuminemic, hyponatremic, and developed a non-gap metabolic acidosis. He also had a rise in his SCr. These abnormalities were thought to be due to intravascular volume depletion evidenced by significant peripheral edema and urine Na<10. He had several episodes of hypotension which were temporarily fluid responsive. He also became tachypneic, thought to be secondary to diaphragm restriction from bowel edema vs fluid overload. Given his tenuous volume status and pending respiratory compromise, he was transferred to the ICU for further management. He was transferred back to the floor for further management of his GVHD. His stool output began to decrease, however he developed ascites and increasing bilirubin. He was started on cyclosporin 100 mg IV BID which was discontinued due to worsening kidney function. Etanercept was discontinued and he received rituxan. He also received pentostatin 2 mg daily. Steroids were slowly tapered as his stool output decreased. His immunosuppression was altered to include IV cellcept and pentastatin every two weeks. 

The patient also developed hyperbilirubinemia and ascites. His liver improved by holding the TPN and giving him medication. His GVHD slowly improved, and most of his immunosuppressants were weaned off and his diet was advanced. By discharge, his TPN was discontinued and his bowel movements had returned to normal. He was discharged to a rehabilitation facility for physical therapy given how deconditioned he was following this hospital stay. His discharge diagnosis included viral meningitis, acute graft-versus-host disease (gut and skin), Clostridium difficile associated diarrhea, and acute kidney injury. His medications upon admission included Acyclovir, Folic Acid, Omeprazole, Ondansetron, and Multivitamins.

note_summary:
The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted due to a rapid weight gain of 17lbs in one week, abdominal distention, and lower extremity edema. His past medical history is significant for Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML), for which he underwent Allogenic Stem Cell Transplant (AlloSCT). Post-transplant, he was diagnosed with gut and skin Graft Versus Host Disease (GVHD) and Post-Transplant Lymphoproliferative Disorder (PTLD) based on a liver biopsy. He also has a history of right cataract surgery, left cataract, hereditary hemochromatosis, and two instances of Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) for which an Inferior Vena Cava (IVC) filter was placed and later removed. 

The patient's family history includes a grandson with Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL), a mother with cervical cancer, a sister with lung cancer and a brain tumor, and multiple brothers who died from Coronary Artery Disease (CAD). 

Upon admission, the patient's physical exam revealed tachycardia, abdominal distention, and a superficial hematoma on the left inner thigh. His lab results showed abnormal levels of Red Blood Cells (RBC), Hemoglobin (Hgb), Hematocrit (Hct), Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV), Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH), Red Cell Distribution Width (RDW), Platelet Count (Plt Ct), Neutrophils, Alanine Aminotransferase (ALT), Aspartate Aminotransferase (AST), Lactate Dehydrogenase (LDH), Alkaline Phosphatase (AlkPhos), Albumin, Urea Nitrogen (UreaN), Creatinine (Creat), and Immunoglobulin G (IgG). 

Radiology results showed mild dilation in the left atrium, aortic root, and ascending aorta, as well as mild symmetric left ventricular hypertrophy. There was also a nonocclusive thrombus in the left common femoral vein and an occlusive thrombus in the right common femoral vein extending to the superficial femoral and deep femoral veins as well as down to the popliteal vein. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was treated for abdominal distention, ileus, nausea/vomiting, nutrition issues, Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT), and thrombocytopenia. His abdominal distention was likely due to ileus, and his thrombocytopenia was likely multifactorial, possibly related to GVHD, chemotherapy, drug-induced causes, or a hypocellular bone marrow. His acute drop in platelets was most likely related to consumption by the clot. 

Upon discharge, the patient's primary diagnoses were ileus, Inferior Vena Cava (IVC) thrombosis extending to lower extremities, chronic GVHD, thrombocytopenia, and poor oral intake requiring parenteral nutrition. His secondary diagnoses were Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) status-post allogenic stem cell transplant, Vitamin D deficiency, and depression. His discharge medications included Acyclovir, Azithromycin, Budesonide, Calcitriol, Lidocaine Jelly, Micafungin, Miconazole Powder, Nystatin Oral Suspension, Pantoprazole, Sertraline, Spironolactone, Sulfameth/Trimethoprim, Vitamin D, Enoxaparin Sodium, Simethicone, Folic Acid, Furosemide, Multivitamins, Promethazine, Methylprednisolone Sodium Succ, and Lorazepam.

The patient's family history includes a grandson with Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL), a mother with cervical cancer, a sister with lung cancer and a brain tumor, and multiple brothers who died from Coronary Artery Disease (CAD). 

Upon admission, the patient's physical exam revealed tachycardia, abdominal distention, and a superficial hematoma on the left inner thigh. His lab results showed abnormal levels of Red Blood Cells (RBC), Hemoglobin (Hgb), Hematocrit (Hct), Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV), Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH), Red Cell Distribution Width (RDW), Platelet Count (Plt Ct), Neutrophils, Alanine Aminotransferase (ALT), Aspartate Aminotransferase (AST), Lactate Dehydrogenase (LDH), Alkaline Phosphatase (AlkPhos), Albumin, Urea Nitrogen (UreaN), Creatinine (Creat), and Immunoglobulin G (IgG). 

Radiology results showed mild dilation in the left atrium, aortic root, and ascending aorta, as well as mild symmetric left ventricular hypertrophy. There was also a nonocclusive thrombus in the left common femoral vein and an occlusive thrombus in the right common femoral vein extending to the superficial femoral and deep femoral veins as well as down to the popliteal vein. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was treated for abdominal distention, ileus, nausea/vomiting, nutrition issues, Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT), and thrombocytopenia. His abdominal distention was likely due to ileus, and his thrombocytopenia was likely multifactorial, possibly related to GVHD, chemotherapy, drug-induced causes, or a hypocellular bone marrow. His acute drop in platelets was most likely related to consumption by the clot. 

Upon discharge, the patient's primary diagnoses were ileus, Inferior Vena Cava (IVC) thrombosis extending to lower extremities, chronic GVHD, thrombocytopenia, and poor oral intake requiring parenteral nutrition. His secondary diagnoses were Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) status-post allogenic stem cell transplant, Vitamin D deficiency, and depression. His discharge medications included Acyclovir, Azithromycin, Budesonide, Calcitriol, Lidocaine Jelly, Micafungin, Miconazole Powder, Nystatin Oral Suspension, Pantoprazole, Sertraline, Spironolactone, Sulfameth/Trimethoprim, Vitamin D, Enoxaparin Sodium, Simethicone, Folic Acid, Furosemide, Multivitamins, Promethazine, Methylprednisolone Sodium Succ, and Lorazepam.

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of relapsed Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML), was admitted due to concerns of Graft Versus Host Disease (GVHD) and liver infection. He had previously undergone an unrelated donor stem cell transplant and Donor Lymphocyte Infusion (DLI) for his AML, which was complicated by Grade 4 GVHD. He had also been diagnosed with Post-Transplant Lymphoproliferative Disorder (PTLD) based on a liver biopsy and high Epstein-Barr Virus (EBV) viral load. His EBV viral load normalized after treatment with Velcade/Rituxan and reduced immunosuppression. He had also been treated with Dacogen for his relapsed AML, with subsequent bone marrow biopsies showing no morphological evidence for leukemia. 

During this admission, the patient had rising transaminases and bandemia, with a liver biopsy showing possible changes consistent with chronic infection. However, he denied any infectious symptoms and stated that he felt well. Imaging studies showed probable left lobe hepatic hemangioma, slight interval increase in the thickening of the distal ileum, stable extensive thrombus within the infrarenal Inferior Vena Cava (IVC) extending into the iliac and femoral veins bilaterally, and hemosiderosis with evidence of iron deposition in the liver and spleen. 

The patient's family history includes Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL) in his grandson, cervical cancer in his mother, and lung cancer and brain tumor in his sister. Multiple brothers died with Coronary Artery Disease (CAD). 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Acyclovir, Budesonide, Calcitriol, Folic Acid, Furosemide, Lidocaine Jelly, Lorazepam, Methylprednisolone Sodium Succ, Micafungin, Multivitamins, Mycophenolate Mofetil, Nystatin Oral Suspension, Pantoprazole, Promethazine, Sertraline, Simethicone, Spironolactone, Sulfameth/Trimethoprim SS, and Enoxaparin Sodium. 

Abnormal lab results included low White Blood Cell (WBC), Red Blood Cell (RBC), Hemoglobin (Hgb), Hematocrit (Hct), Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV), Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH), Red Cell Distribution Width (RDW), and Platelet Count (Plt Ct). The patient also had high bandemia, high Alanine Aminotransferase (ALT), Aspartate Aminotransferase (AST), Lactate Dehydrogenase (LDH), Alkaline Phosphatase (AlkPhos), and C-Reactive Protein (CRP). His Immunoglobulin G (IgG) was also elevated. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's transaminitis and bandemia improved with antibiotics. His GVHD was managed with methylprednisolone, and his volume overload improved with diuretics. He also received weekly Intravenous Immunoglobulin (IVIG) infusions. His cough resolved without any change in therapy. His lower extremity Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) extending to IVC filter was managed with Lovenox. His skin issues improved significantly during his stay. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with microscopic cholangitis, GVHD, bandemia, DVT, and volume overload. His discharge medications included Acyclovir, Budesonide, Calcitriol, Enoxaparin Sodium, Folic Acid, Furosemide, Lidocaine Jelly, Lorazepam, Micafungin, Multivitamins, Mycophenolate Mofetil, Nystatin Oral Suspension, Pantoprazole, Promethazine, Sertraline, Simethicone, Spironolactone, Sulfameth/Trimethoprim SS, Docusate Sodium, Potassium Chloride, and Methylprednisolone. He was discharged to an extended care facility.

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML), presented with left shoulder pain due to glenohumeral osteonecrosis. He underwent a Left Reverse Total Shoulder Arthroplasty. In the past, he had an allogeneic stem cell transplant for relapsed AML. Post-transplant, he was diagnosed with Post-Transplant Lymphoproliferative Disorder (PTLD) based on a liver biopsy and a high EBV viral load. He also had FDG-avid hepatic hilar adenopathy. His liver enzymes improved on reduced cyclosporine doses and his EBV viral load decreased to normal/negative after one dose of Velcade/Rituxan. A PET/CT showed him to be in remission. 

He had a relapse of AML with 11% blasts and chimerism 60% donor. He was started on Dacogen and after 3 cycles, a bone marrow biopsy showed no morphological evidence for leukemia. He was given another cycle of Dacogen. He presented to the clinic with increasing rash, fatigue, and fever. An upper endoscopy showed extensive erosions from the stomach to the duodenum, with biopsies consistent with Graft Versus Host Disease (GVHD). He was admitted with initial concern of GVHD flare and steroids were increased. He was found to have ileus. He also had a lower extremity clot bilaterally which extended up to the IVC filter. He was placed on a Heparin drip, then switched to Lovenox. His lower extremity edema improved somewhat, but remained. 

In terms of his AML and LPD, a CT of chest, abdomen, and pelvis showed no evidence of lymphoproliferative disease. His last chimerism and bone marrow showed 95% donor with pancytopenic marrow, but no recurrence of AML. 

Family history includes a grandson with Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL), a mother with cervical cancer, a sister with lung cancer and brain tumor, and multiple brothers who died with Coronary Artery Disease (CAD). 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Acyclovir, albuterol sulfate, Azithromycin, Folic Acid, Furosemide, Lorazepam, Mycophenolate Mofetil, Pantoprazole, Potassium Chloride, Prednisone, and Rivaroxaban. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent the aforementioned shoulder surgery without complications and tolerated the procedure well. His postoperative course was unremarkable and his pain was controlled with a combination of IV and oral pain medications. He restarted Rivaroxaban on postoperative day 1. At the time of discharge, he was tolerating a regular diet, was afebrile with stable vital signs, and his hematocrit was acceptable. His operative extremity was neurovascularly intact and the wound was benign. His weight-bearing status is non-weight bearing on the operative extremity. He was discharged to home in stable condition.

note_summary:
The patient, a middle-aged male with no significant past medical history, was admitted due to a new diagnosis of leukemia. His chief complaint was fatigue. He had previously undergone right cataract surgery and had a left cataract. His family history includes his mother with cervical cancer, a sister with lung cancer and a brain tumor of unknown etiology, and multiple brothers who passed away from coronary disease.

Upon admission, the patient presented with fatigue, dizziness with standing, fever, and chills. He had anemia and thrombocytopenia with 50% blasts in his peripheral smear and CD positivity. He also had mild hematuria. A bone marrow biopsy was performed, which was consistent with acute myeloid leukemia with partial monocytic differentiation. His initial vitals were: T99.0 P72 BP142/76 RR16 O2Sat99% RA. A differential demonstrated 58% blasts. He had no known allergies to drugs.

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent a central line placement and a bone marrow biopsy. He was started on Hydroxyurea 500mg BID & Allopurinol on HD1. 7+3 chemotherapy was then initiated on HD 2 after a normal echocardiogram. His LFT's were noted to rise and they were trended carefully until midnight on day 6, his total bilirubin rose to 9.9 and his chemotherapy was stopped. A right upper quadrant ultrasound was performed that demonstrated only fatty liver. His LFT's were followed closely and trended down without evidence of blasts in the peripheral smear. 

The patient was noted to have an incidental finding of free air below the diaphragm on CXR. He remained hemodynamically stable and asymptomatic. CT abdomen / pelvis with gastrografin PO contrast was performed an revealed air in lesser sac but no evidence of extravasation. The patient was immediately started on Vancomycin, Flagyl and Meropenem for broad antibiotic coverage. 

The patient was noted to have persistent high fevers after neutrophil counts had recovered with Tmax of 103.5 and no identified source. A thorough workup was done for infection, including CT abdomen and chest, without any significant findings. Patient's pantoprazole and all antibiotics were stopped and patient was monitored for 4 days as fevers trended down and patient felt better. 

After recovery of his neutrophil count, patient was having loose stools, about one per day. He was treated empirically with metronidazole, and C difficile toxin assay was found to be negative; all other stool studies were canceled by laboratory because stools were not of appropriate consistency. He was evaluated by Gastroenterology team. Loose stools resolved by time of discharge. 

Abnormal lab results include: RBC-2.80*, Hgb-9.3*, Hct-26.8*, MCH-33.3*, RDW-15.6*, Plt Ct-55*, Neuts-8*, Lymphs-13*, Monos-18*, Atyps-3*, Glucose-107*, ALT-43*, LD(LDH)-287*, ALT-72*, AST-40, LD(LDH)-744*, TotBili-2.2*, DirBili-0.4*, IndBili-1.8, Albumin-3.4*, Phos-2.6*#. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with acute monoblastic leukemia. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory and independent. He was instructed to start taking Multivitamins with Minerals daily.

note_context: Suspicion of MFS in primary care Making the correct diagnosis of MFS in a patient may eventually have great impact on morbidity and the disease— mortality. However, either by the patient or his physicians—is the key to referral. In this respect the revised nosology may be unnecessarily complex for the primary care setting, while also lacking clear recommendations specialised centre. on whom to refer Sheikhzadeh et al6 aimed to develop a simpliﬁed model to identify patients with possible MFS who require deﬁnite workup. Of 329 consecutive patients thoroughly evaluated for MFS, 208 were randomly assigned to a derivation group. Using multivariate logistic regression, 14 clinical variables —all generally easily available without any imaging modalities—were analysed for their association with a positive MFS diagnosis. Seven of these vari- ables were included in a simple prediction score and receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis was used to deﬁne cut-off points. Interestingly,

A thorough workup was done for infection, including CT abdomen and chest, without any significant findings. Patient's pantoprazole and all antibiotics were stopped and patient was monitored for 4 days as fevers trended down and patient felt better. 

After recovery of his neutrophil count, patient was having loose stools, about one per day. He was treated empirically with metronidazole, and C difficile toxin assay was found to be negative; all other stool studies were canceled by laboratory because stools were not of appropriate consistency. He was evaluated by Gastroenterology team. Loose stools resolved by time of discharge. 

Abnormal lab results include: RBC-2.80*, Hgb-9.3*, Hct-26.8*, MCH-33.3*, RDW-15.6*, Plt Ct-55*, Neuts-8*, Lymphs-13*, Monos-18*, Atyps-3*, Glucose-107*, ALT-43*, LD(LDH)-287*, ALT-72*, AST-40, LD(LDH)-744*, TotBili-2.2*, DirBili-0.4*, IndBili-1.8, Albumin-3.4*, Phos-2.6*#. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with acute monoblastic leukemia. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory and independent. He was instructed to start taking Multivitamins with Minerals daily.

note_summary:
The patient is a male with a history of Post-Transplant Lymphoproliferative Disorder (PTLD) and relapsed Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML), following an unrelated donor transplant. He has also been diagnosed with Grade 4 Graft Versus Host Disease (GVHD) of the gastrointestinal tract. He has had a history of increased lower extremity edema, bilateral Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) extending to the Inferior Vena Cava (IVC), bandemia, and transaminitis. Liver biopsies have shown infection and GVHD. He has been admitted due to a wasting syndrome and has undergone endoscopy, colonoscopy, and G-tube placement.

The patient has lost approximately 70 pounds in one year, initially intentional but continued due to loss of appetite and altered taste perception. He denies nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and diarrhea. He has had a Mohs procedure on his right ear with no acute issues reported. 

His oncologic history includes AML, treated with induction chemotherapy and three cycles of HiDAC, followed by a relapse and retreatment with Clofarabine and low dose ARA-C. He underwent a nonmyeloablative allo Stem Cell Transplant (SCT) and was diagnosed with PTLD based on liver biopsy and high EBV viral load. He has had multiple treatments and medications for these conditions, including Dacogen, IV methylprednisolone, CellCept, budesonide, Cyclosporin, Rituxan, and pentostatin. He has also had multiple hospital admissions for complications such as rash, fatigue, fever, abdominal pain, diarrhea, and increased transaminases. 

His family history includes Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL) in his grandson, cervical cancer in his mother, and lung cancer and brain tumor in his sister. Several of his brothers have died from Coronary Artery Disease (CAD).

Upon admission, the patient's physical exam was largely normal, with no acute distress, normal vital signs, and no significant findings on cardiovascular, pulmonary, abdominal, limb, skin, and neurological examination. However, his discharge physical exam showed dry mucous membranes and cracked lips, mild tenderness around the newly placed PEG, and old bruising on both hands.

Admission labs showed abnormal neutrophils, lymphocytes, eosinophils, red blood cell count, hemoglobin, hematocrit, RDW, albumin, ALT, AST, ALK PHOS, and glucose. Discharge labs showed abnormal red blood cell count, hemoglobin, hematocrit, MCHC, RDW, lymphocytes, monocytes, creatinine, ALT, AST, ALK PHOS, albumin, and glucose. Imaging showed a new trace left pleural effusion, a 6 cm segment of upper esophagus demonstrating circumferential wall thickening, unchanged aneurysmal dilatation of the ascending aorta, severe degenerative changes at the right glenohumeral joint, and mild compression deformity of L3. A cecal polyp biopsy showed invasive adenocarcinoma, moderately differentiated.

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of Failure to Thrive, cecal adenocarcinoma, AML, DVT, chronic airway disease, and GVHD. His discharge medications included Jevity 1.5 Cal, Thiamine, Acyclovir, Azithromycin, Betamethasone Dipro, Cyanocobalamin, Dexamethasone Oral Soln, Erythromycin, Fluticasone Propionate, Folic Acid, Guaifenesin-CODEINE Phosphate, LORazepam, Multivitamins, Mupirocin Ointment, Mycophenolate Mofetil, Nystatin Oral Suspension, Pantoprazole, PredniSONE, Promethazine, Rivaroxaban, Sulfameth/Trimethoprim SS, Tacrolimus, Vitamin D, and Zinc Sulfate. He was advised to continue taking all of his medications as prescribed and to follow up with his outpatient team.

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) and Post-Transplant Lymphoproliferative Disorder (PTLD), was admitted for surgical resection of a cecal adenocarcinoma. His AML had relapsed and was treated with Clofarabine and low dose ARA-C, followed by a nonmyeloablative allo stem cell transplant. He also had a history of graft vs host disease, which was managed with various immunosuppressive therapies. He had a history of weight loss and had a PEG tube placed for feeding. He also had a history of bilateral DVTs, for which he was on anticoagulation therapy. 

During his stay, he underwent a laparoscopic right colectomy, which was converted to open due to bleeding. Postoperatively, he required blood pressure support and transfusion of packed red blood cells due to intraoperative blood loss. He was extubated the following day and his hematocrit remained stable. He was started on oral medications and a clear liquid diet, which was later advanced to a regular diet. His home tube feedings were also restarted. 

His laboratory results showed persistent abnormalities in his complete blood count, with low RBC, Hgb, Hct, and elevated RDW. His blood chemistry also showed abnormalities, with low creatinine and elevated glucose and urea nitrogen. His calcium, phosphorus, and magnesium levels were also consistently low. 

His family history includes a grandson with Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL), a mother with cervical cancer, a sister with lung cancer and a brain tumor, and multiple brothers who died from coronary artery disease. 

Upon discharge, the patient was stable and ready to return home. He was instructed to monitor his bowel function closely, watch for signs of infection at the incision sites, and manage his pain with Tylenol and Advil. He was also advised to avoid heavy lifting and long-distance travel until his follow-up visit. His discharge medications included Jevity, Acyclovir, Azithromycin, Betamethasone, Cyanocobalamin, Dexamethasone, Folic Acid, Lorazepam, Multivitamins, Mycophenolate Mofetil, Nystatin, Pantoprazole, Prednisone, Promethazine, Rivaroxaban, Sulfameth/Trimethoprim, Tacrolimus, Thiamine, Vitamin D, Zinc Sulfate, Erythromycin, Fluticasone, Anoro Ellipta, and Montelukast.

note_summary:
The patient is a male with a complex medical history including Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) complicated by grade IV Graft Versus Host Disease (GVHD), Post-Transplant Lymphoproliferative Disorder (PTLD), adenocarcinoma of the colon post resection, and wasting syndrome post G-tube placement. He presented with approximately two weeks of increasing dyspnea on exertion and increased fatigue. His cough persists but is stable and chronic. He has been using his inhalers as directed and continues on Singulair and Zithromax as per pulmonary. He takes cough medication once per day. At presentation, his oxygen saturation was 88% and remained low at 88-90%. He underwent Chest CTA and was admitted for further workup and management of his hypoxia and dyspnea. 

The patient's past medical history is significant for multiple episodes of AML, PTLD, and GVHD. He has undergone multiple treatments including chemotherapy, stem cell transplant, and Dacogen. He has also had multiple hospital admissions for complications including viral meningitis, C. difficile infection, and severe diarrhea. He has a history of bilateral DVTs extending to the IVC, liver biopsies showing GVHD and infection, and severe narrowing of the IVC requiring angioplasty. 

The patient's family history includes a grandson with Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL), a mother with cervical cancer, a sister with lung cancer and a brain tumor, and multiple brothers who died from Coronary Artery Disease (CAD). 

Upon admission, the patient's labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.59), Hgb (11.4), Hct (36.5), MCV (102), MCHC (31.2), RDW (16.8), RDWSD (62.9), Neuts (76.9), Lymphs (5.8), Monos (14.6), AbsNeut (7.58), AbsLymp (0.57), AbsMono (1.44), Glucose (109), HCO3 (33), ALT (85), AST (77), LD(LDH) (269), AlkPhos (312), and UricAcd (2.7). His IgG level was 739. 

A Chest CTA showed no evidence of pulmonary embolism or aortic abnormality, but did show minimal atelectatic changes and minimal lower lobe, patchy, ground-glass opacification, which may be inflammatory in nature. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's dyspnea and hypoxia were managed with supplemental oxygen. His AML and PTLD were monitored with daily CBCs and he received an IVIG infusion. His wasting syndrome was managed with bolus tube feedings and his GVHD was managed with continued Cellcept and prednisone. 

Upon discharge, the patient's labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.26), Hgb (10.4), Hct (33.1), MCV (102), MCHC (31.4), RDW (16.5), RDWSD (62.6), and HCO3 (36). His Calcium level was 8.3. 

The patient was discharged with instructions to use supplemental oxygen when exerting himself or walking around, to take all of his prescribed medications, and to follow up with his doctor. He was also advised to call his oncologist if he experienced any worsening shortness of breath, fevers, chills, or cough.

note_summary:
The patient is a middle-aged male with a history of Acute Myelogenous Leukemia (AML) who had recently undergone induction chemotherapy. He also has a history of right cataract surgery and left cataract. He was admitted due to transaminitis noted during a follow-up visit. The patient reported no abdominal pain and had been eating better at home. He also reported a 'tickle' in his throat that comes and goes with a dry cough for a couple of days, which worsens when he lies back. He denied any fevers, chills, shortness of breath, abdominal pain, chest pain, constipation, diarrhea, hematochezia, urinary difficulties, lightheadedness, dizziness, and nausea. 

The patient's family history includes his mother with cervical cancer, a sister with lung cancer and a brain tumor of unknown etiology, and multiple brothers who passed away from coronary disease. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to have a diffuse inspiratory wheeze, increased in the right middle posterior lung field. His abdomen was soft, non-tender, non-distended, with no hepatosplenomegaly. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (2.93*), Hgb (8.8*), Hct (27.0*), RDW (16.7*), Neuts (75*), Lymphs (11*), Metas (3*), ALT (217*), AST (92*), LD(LDH) (301*), AlkPhos (725*), and calTIBC (198*). 

Radiology reports showed mildly increased echogenicity of the liver, unchanged from prior studies, and a small amount of sludge in the gallbladder. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's transaminitis was investigated. It was noted that he had a brief transaminitis associated with cytarabine therapy during induction chemotherapy in his last hospitalization, which resolved after stopping chemotherapy. Viral hepatitis was considered but ruled out as viral hepatitis antibody titers were negative; HCV viral load is undetectable, and HBV viral load is still pending. Autoimmune hepatitis was also ruled out as AMA were negative, and IgG level was normal. 

The patient was discharged with instructions to return to the clinic for a follow-up in two days. His discharge diagnosis included transaminitis and Acute Myelogenous Leukemia. His only medication upon admission was a multivitamin with minerals.

note_context: Suspicion of MFS in primary care Making the correct diagnosis of MFS in a patient may eventually have great impact on morbidity and the disease— mortality. However, either by the patient or his physicians—is the key to referral. In this respect the revised nosology may be unnecessarily complex for the primary care setting, while also lacking clear recommendations specialised centre. on whom to refer Sheikhzadeh et al6 aimed to develop a simpliﬁed model to identify patients with possible MFS who require deﬁnite workup. Of 329 consecutive patients thoroughly evaluated for MFS, 208 were randomly assigned to a derivation group. Using multivariate logistic regression, 14 clinical variables —all generally easily available without any imaging modalities—were analysed for their association with a positive MFS diagnosis. Seven of these vari- ables were included in a simple prediction score and receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis was used to deﬁne cut-off points. Interestingly,

note_summary:
The patient is a middle-aged male with a history of Acute Myelogenous Leukemia (AML) who has undergone induction chemotherapy. He was admitted for consolidation therapy with HiDAC. He had previously experienced transaminitis in the context of chemotherapy, which had normalized after cessation of treatment. He was also found to have asymptomatic free air under the diaphragm, suspected to be due to microperforation of the duodenum or sigmoid colon. An EGD and colonoscopy revealed an ulcer at the GE junction, for which he was started on omeprazole. He reported feeling well, eating well, and denied any specific complaints or symptoms such as fevers, chills, shortness of breath, abdominal pain, chest pain, nausea, vomiting, constipation, diarrhea, hematochezia, urinary difficulties, lightheadedness, dizziness, or nausea. His past medical history also includes right cataract surgery and left cataract. 

His family history includes a mother with cervical cancer, a sister with lung cancer and a brain tumor of unknown etiology, and multiple brothers who passed away from coronary disease. 

Upon physical examination, he was found to be in a generally good state. His vital signs were stable and within normal limits. His physical examination was largely unremarkable, with no signs of distress, rashes, ecchymoses, petichiae, hepatosplenomegaly, cyanosis, clubbing, or edema. Neurologically, he was alert and oriented, with intact sensation, strength, and coordination. 

Lab results showed several abnormal findings. His hemoglobin, hematocrit, and platelet count were consistently low. His glucose levels were elevated on multiple occasions. His alkaline phosphatase and total bilirubin were also elevated. His phosphorus level was high on one occasion. 

During his hospital stay, he received HiDAC per protocol for his AML. He was given IV fluids and anti-emetics per protocol, as well as steroid eye drops. His liver function tests were monitored due to his history of transaminitis, and remained within normal limits except for a total bilirubin of 1.8 at the time of discharge. He was also monitored for cerebellar function, which remained normal. 

His medications upon admission included a multivitamin and omeprazole 20mg daily. His gastritis was managed with continuation of his home proton pump inhibitor. He was on a regular diet and his DVT prophylaxis was with ambulation and pneumoboots. 

Upon discharge, his mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted for the second cycle of HiDAC therapy for his Acute Myelogenous Leukemia (AML) with inversion 16. He had previously undergone induction chemotherapy and had a history of right cataract surgery and left cataract. His family history includes his mother having cervical cancer, a sister with lung cancer and a brain tumor of unknown etiology, and multiple brothers who passed away from coronary disease. 

The patient's chief complaint was AML requiring HiDAC chemotherapy. He had previously been admitted after experiencing fatigue, dizziness with standing, fever, and chills. Work-up showed anemia and thrombocytopenia with 50% blasts in his peripheral smear and CD positivity. He had a negative CT abdomen/pelvis, and U/A positive for mild hematuria. He was diagnosed with presumed AML and referred for further treatment. He had a history of transaminitis and hyperbilirubinemia, neutropenic fever, and an incidental finding of subdiaphragmatic free air. An abdominal CT suggested either duodenal or sigmoid diverticulum perforation. He was started on Vancomycin, Flagyl, and Meropenem and made NPO with TPN support and NG tube to suction. A bone marrow test showed no evidence of disease. He had an endoscopy and colonoscopy, during which a polyp was removed. Diverticulosis and a 5mm ulcer in the GE junction were noted. 

During his current admission, the patient noted left leg swelling, erythema, and tenderness. He was found to have a left lower extremity DVT and was started on Lovenox. He had an IVC filter placed during chemo as his platelets dropped to 16k last cycle and he would be unable to continue anticoagulation at those levels. 

Lab results showed abnormal LDH at 372, WBC at 19.1, RBC at 3.10, HGB at 9.4, HCT at 28.7, RDW at 16.9, NEUTS at 76, LYMPHS at 7, METAS at 5, and PLT COUNT at 971. 

During his hospital stay, the patient received Cytarabine 5580 mg IV Q12H on Days 1, 3, and 5. He was premedicated with Ondansetron 24 mg IV Q24H for 5 days, Lorazepam 0.5-2 mg PO/IV Q4H, and Dexamethasone 10 mg IV daily for 5 days. He also received Prednisolone Acetate 1% Ophth. Susp. 1 DROP BOTH EYES TID. He had some mild facial flushing during and after HiDAC infusion. 

The patient also developed a left lower extremity DVT for which he was started on Lovenox q12h. He had an IVC filter placed with no complications. He was continued on Lovenox given his elevated platelets, and will be discontinued when he becomes thrombocytopenic. 

On a few occasions, the patient was found to have a heart rate in the low range. He had an EKG done showing sinus bradycardia. He remained asymptomatic throughout and was discharged with a heart rate >60. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Enoxaparin [Lovenox]80 mg/0.8 mL Syringe 1 injection every twelve (12) hours, Multivitamins with Minerals 1 Tablet(s) by mouth once a day, and Omeprazole 20 mg Capsule, Delayed Release(E.C.) 1 Capsule(s) PO daily.

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted for a scheduled HiDAC chemotherapy course. He has a history of Acute Myelogenous Leukemia (AML) with inversion 16, and has previously undergone induction chemotherapy with 7+3 and two cycles of HiDAC. He also has a history of right cataract surgery and left cataract. His symptoms of fever and cough have subsided since starting Ciprofloxacin, and his calf tenderness and swelling have resolved since starting Lovenox. He denies experiencing a range of symptoms including nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, anorexia, weight loss, malaise, fever, chills, night sweats, cough, shortness of breath, chest pain, palpitations, dysuria, bruising, bleeding, numbness or weakness in the extremities, confusion, and depression. 

The patient's family history includes a mother with cervical cancer, a sister with lung cancer and a brain tumor of unknown etiology, and multiple brothers who passed away from coronary disease. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Ciprofloxacin and Enoxaparin (Lovenox). His physical exam was largely normal, with the exception of trace non-pitting edema in the left lower extremity and ecchymosis on the abdomen from Lovenox injections. 

Admission labs showed abnormal values for WBC (18.5), RBC (3.63), Hgb (10.9), Hct (33.4), Bands (9), Lymphs (3), Monos (24), Metas (4), Myelos (3), ALT (41), and LD (363). Discharge labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.67), Hgb (11.1), Hct (33.5), Neuts (94.4), Lymphs (2.8), Glucose (128), UreaN (21), ALT (67), and Phos (5.3). A chest X-ray showed some indistinctness of pulmonary vessels, but no evidence of acute focal pneumonia. 

During his hospital stay, the patient received a 5-day course of HiDAC per protocol. He developed oral thrush on the tongue, which was treated with Nystatin swish and swallow QID. He was discharged with prednisolone eye drops, but showed no evidence of keratitis. He had no problems with peripheral edema. He also continued on a course of Ciprofloxacin for 3 days for a recent upper respiratory infection, and continued Enoxaparin for a history of deep venous thrombus. 

Upon discharge, the patient's primary diagnosis was Acute Myelogenous Leukemia, with secondary diagnoses of Oropharyngeal candidiasis and a history of deep venous thrombus. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.

note_context:
There are a few limitations in our study. All data were col- lected retrospectively from medical records and the selection of the treatment modality was dependent on the physician and pa- tients’ choice. Some of pneumothorax occurred in our MFS patients might not have been captured in this study, especially when the diagnosis and treatment of a patient were performed in another hospital. Our hospital is equipped with a multi-disciplinary clinic team designated to treat MFS patients. This could lead to a selec- tion bias, and as such, the patient population in this study may not be an adequate representation of MFS patients.

Conclusion

The patient is a male with a history of Acute Myelogenous Leukemia (AML), who was admitted for his fourth cycle of HiDAC chemotherapy. He has previously tolerated this therapy well, with no changes in cerebellar function or mental status. He reported feeling in his normal state of health on the day of admission, denying any fevers, chills, nausea, vomiting, shortness of breath, chest pain, abdominal pain, diarrhea, constipation, or dysuria/hematuria. His initial vital signs on the floor were normal.

The patient's past oncologic history includes a diagnosis of AML after presenting with fatigue, dizziness, fever, and chills. Work-up revealed anemia, thrombocytopenia, and 50% blasts in his peripheral smear. He had a negative CT abdomen/pelvis, and urinalysis showed mild hematuria. He has undergone multiple cycles of chemotherapy, with a few admissions for complications such as transaminitis, hyperbilirubinemia, and a duodenal or sigmoid diverticulum perforation. He also had a left lower extremity Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT). His other past medical history includes right cataract surgery, left cataract, hemochromatosis, and DVT.

The patient's family history includes a mother with cervical cancer, a sister with lung cancer and a brain tumor of unknown etiology, and multiple brothers who passed away from coronary disease.

On physical examination, the patient was alert, oriented, and in no acute distress. His lungs were clear to auscultation bilaterally, and his cardiovascular exam was normal. His abdomen was soft, non-tender, and non-distended, with no rebound tenderness or guarding. He had mild edema in the left leg. His cerebellar exam was normal.

Admission labs showed an LDH of 305, RBC of 3.12, HGB of 9.8, HCT of 29.3, RDW of 19.4, Neutrophils of 80, Lymphocytes of 6, Atypical cells of 1, Metamyelocytes of 2, and a platelet count of 657, all of which were abnormal.

During his hospital stay, the patient was administered his fourth course of HiDAC according to protocol over a five-day course. He developed no side effects and remained in excellent spirits throughout his stay. He was maintained and discharged on prednisolone eyedrops and developed no keratitis, mucositis, or thrush. He also remained on Lovenox for his recent DVT, with platelets remaining above 400 throughout the hospital course. He reported no calf pain, shortness of breath, or chest pain.

Upon admission, the patient was on Lovenox 80mg every 12 hours, a multivitamin, and Omeprazole 20mg daily. His primary diagnosis upon discharge was AML, with a secondary diagnosis of a history of DVT. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was ambulatory and independent.

Conclusion
note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of Acute Myelogenous Leukemia (AML), was admitted following a syncopal episode at home and a fever. He had recently completed cycle 4 of HiDAC chemotherapy and had not previously experienced febrile neutropenia. His past medical history includes AML (with inv16) post induction chemotherapy, right cataract surgery, left cataract, hemochromatosis, and a deep vein thrombosis (DVT). His family history includes a mother with cervical cancer, a sister with lung cancer and a brain tumor of unknown etiology, and multiple brothers who passed away from coronary disease.

Upon admission, the patient was found to be febrile with an ANC of 824. He was empirically started on vancomycin and cefepime. His physical exam was largely unremarkable, with a noted bruise over the bridge of his nose and a 3cm ecchymotic area over his left elbow from his fall. 

Admission labs showed several abnormalities including a platelet count of 14, neutrophils at 91.6, lymphocytes at 6.4, WBC at 0.9, RBC at 2.39, hemoglobin at 7.4, hematocrit at 21.7, RDW at 17.0, ALT at 45, glucose at 141, and lactate at 2.6. Urinalysis showed large amounts of blood but was otherwise normal. 

Imaging studies, including a CT head scan and chest X-rays, showed no acute intracranial or cardiopulmonary abnormalities. However, a CT of the torso showed the development of a nonspecific opacity in the right upper lobe, which could represent an atypical pneumonia. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's syncopal episode was attributed to enhanced vagal tone while urinating and dehydration secondary to his fevers. His febrile neutropenia was managed with antibiotics, and his neutropenia resolved. However, his daily fevers persisted, leading to a consultation with the ID and pulmonary teams. A flexible bronchoscopy was performed due to concerning CT findings, but the bronchoalveolar lavage results were within normal limits. 

The patient also presented with microscopic hematuria on repeat urinalysis, for which he was advised to establish outpatient urologic follow-up. His DVT was managed with Lovenox, which was initially held due to thrombocytopenia and a recent fall, but was restarted as his platelet count rebounded. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Lovenox 80 mg/0.8 mL every 12 hours, a multivitamin, and Omeprazole 20mg daily.

note_context: The incidence of primary spontaneous pneumothorax is esti- mated to be 5–10 per 10 0,0 0 0 individuals (0.0 05–0.0 01%). How- ever, the incidence of pneumothorax in MFS patients is estimated to be 4–11% [3] . In our study, 31 of 1016 patients (3%) with MFS were diagnosed with pneumothorax and this result is in line with a previously published study [3] . Pneumothorax in MFS patients is often characterized by early onset at a young age and a high fre- quency of bullae [2-3] . Moreover, the ﬁrst episode of pneumotho- rax was observed in 23 (74%) patients who were less than 20 years old. In this cohort, the median age was 15 years old. Bullae at the time of onset were detected in 21 cases (91%) and these results are consistent with previously published reports [2-3] .

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of Acute Myelogenous Leukemia (AML) in remission following 7+3 induction and 4 cycles of HiDAC, was admitted due to a Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT). He had a recent syncopal episode at an outside hospital (OSH) where he was observed overnight with a negative workup. The patient reported feeling hot with sweats overnight and had a fainting episode in the bathroom. He denied chest pain, shortness of breath, or reduced functional status. During a clinic appointment, he was found to have a large DVT and a falling white count. A bone marrow biopsy was performed in the clinic and he was admitted for DVT management. 

The patient has a past medical history of AML (with inv16) in remission following induction chemotherapy and 4 cycles of HiDAC, right cataract surgery, left cataract, hereditary hemochromatosis, and a previous DVT. His oncologic history includes a diagnosis of presumed AML, multiple chemotherapy cycles, transaminitis, hyperbilirubinemia, and a bone marrow biopsy without evidence of disease. He also had an endoscopy and colonoscopy where a polyp was removed and diverticulosis and a 5mm ulcer in the GE junction were noted. 

His family history includes a mother with cervical cancer, a sister with lung cancer and a brain tumor of unknown etiology, and multiple brothers who passed away from coronary disease. 

Upon admission, the patient's vitals were stable. His physical exam was largely unremarkable, with clear lungs, regular heart rate and rhythm, and a soft, non-tender abdomen. His extremities showed 3+ LLEE and Trace RLEE. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for WBC (1.6*), RBC (2.80*), Hgb (10.6*), Hct (29.3*), MCV (105*), MCH (37.7*), MCHC (36.0*), and UreaN (23*). His PTT was also elevated at 150*. 

During his hospital stay, a CTA showed large scale acute pulmonary thromboembolism in the right lung and minimal chronic thrombosis in the left lower lobe. An IVC filter was placed infrarenally. His bone marrow biopsy showed a relapse of AML. 

The patient was treated with a heparin drip for his DVT and PE. His AML relapse was discussed with his primary oncologist and a plan for re-induction with clofarabine/Ara-C as an outpatient was made. He was started on hydroxyurea 500mg PO BID while inpatient. 

Upon discharge, his condition was improved. His discharge medications included enoxaparin 80mg SC BID until re-induction, hydroxyurea 500mg PO BID, and continuation of his multivitamin and omeprazole 20mg daily. His discharge diagnoses were DVT with PE and relapse of AML.

note_summary:
The patient is a female with a history of refractory Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (ITP), having undergone splenectomy and Intravenous Immunoglobulin (IVIG) treatment. She is maintained on prednisone and azathioprine daily. She was transferred for alcohol detoxification and further management of her thrombocytopenia. Her platelet count was 8 at the time of transfer, but upon repeat testing in the Emergency Department (ED), it was found to be 23, which is her baseline. She received 70mg prednisone in the ED. Other medications administered in the ED include buproprion, citalopram, lamictal, omeprazole 40mg daily, seroquel, and 1mg PO ativan for withdrawal symptoms. She denies experiencing headache, visual changes, chest pain, lightheadedness, dizziness, nausea, emesis, night sweats, and gum bleeding. She reported bruising (after a fall last week) and constipation. 

Her past medical history also includes alcohol withdrawal, Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD), hypothyroidism, and osteoarthritis. She lives with her boyfriend and is currently out of work. She has a long history of alcohol use but denies tobacco or intravenous drug use. She has no history of seizures. 

Physical examination revealed small petechiae on the soft palate, no gum bleeding, and bruises on the right extensor and flexor surfaces, less on the left side. Her vitals were stable and she was easily arousable. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for CBC (5.6 > 13.2/40.1 < 23), Chem (138 | 103 | 15 < 111, 4.6 | 25 | 0.9), INR (0.9), and LFTs (54 > ___ < 58). Haptoglobin was 16, Ca ___, Phos 3.9, Mg 2.3, and serum tox was negative. 

During her hospital stay, her platelet count was monitored and found to be 29, verified manually. It was suspected that the low platelet count at the outside hospital represented a clumped specimen. She was advised to stop drinking alcohol. She received ativan for alcohol withdrawal and was encouraged not to drink. She continued her synthroid for hypothyroidism. She was also on buproprion 150mg SR daily, citalopram 40mg daily, quetiapine 150mg at bedtime, lamictal 200mg in the Am, 400mg qPM, chlorpromazine 2mg qAM for possible bipolar disorder. 

Upon admission, her medications included Ativan, Folate, Lamictal, Bactrim DS, Thiamine, MVI, Ferrous sulfate, Wellbutrin, Celexa, Synthroid, Omeprzole, Prednisone, Azathioprine, and Thorazine. 

Upon discharge, her ITP was stable. She was advised to quickly taper her prednisone dosage and to discontinue drinking alcohol. She was also advised to consult her physician about the continuation of Thorazine.

note_summary:
The patient, a right-handed male, was admitted for evaluation of dizziness. His medical history includes Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (DM2), Hypertension (HTN), hemorrhagic cerebellar stroke, hypercholesterolemia, gout, renal insufficiency, diabetic neuropathy, osteoarthritis, diverticulosis, and a history of right foot cellulitis. He has undergone several surgical procedures including a laminectomy, left hip replacement, bilateral knee replacements, tonsillectomy, and bilateral meniscectomies. 

The patient's chief complaint was transient vertigo, similar to the symptoms he experienced during his cerebellar hemorrhage. However, these episodes were shorter and not accompanied by nausea. He also reported a high blood pressure reading of 220/105, which he attributed to not taking his morning dose of clonidine. He denied experiencing any other neurological symptoms such as headache, loss of vision, blurred vision, diplopia, dysarthria, dysphagia, lightheadedness, vertigo, tinnitus, hearing difficulty, focal weakness, numbness, parasthesiae, bowel or bladder incontinence or retention, or difficulty with gait. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to have an antalgic gait favoring the left side, mild limb ataxia on the left, and vertigo and nystagmus induced by a certain maneuver when turning his head to the right. His neurologic exam was otherwise normal. 

Lab results showed abnormal levels of RBC (3.54), Hgb (10.5), Hct (31.3), and Glucose (249). His CK(CPK) level was also abnormal at 28, and his cTropnT level was 0.02. An MRI/MRA of the brain revealed a high-grade stenosis in the left P2 segment of the PCA, a questionable 1-mm aneurysm of the left paraclinoid ICA, and a hypoplastic right A1 segment. 

The patient's family history is significant for both parents dying of stroke at the same age the patient will be turning next month. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking Clonidine, Pravachol, Benazepril, Metoprolol, Allopurinol, Spironolactone, Coumadin, and Flomax. He is allergic to Bactrim. 

The patient was diagnosed with Benign Positional Paroxysmal Vertigo. His vertigo was still present when rolling around in bed but had improved significantly by the time of discharge.

note_summary:
The patient, a right-handed male with a history of Insulin-Dependent Diabetes Mellitus (IDDM), Hyperlipidemia (HL), Hypertension (HTN), chronic kidney disease (with creatinine levels between 2.2-2.3), and a prior cerebellar peduncle hemorrhage, presented to the Emergency Department (ED) with worsening dizziness. He reported that his current symptoms were different from those he experienced with Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo (BPPV), which he had been diagnosed with and treated for in the past. The patient described his current symptoms as more of lightheadedness and dysequilibrium, which were exacerbated by positional changes. He also reported recent instances of low blood pressure, which is unusual for him as he is on multiple antihypertensives and his blood pressure tends to be labile.

His past medical history includes a hypertensive hemorrhagic stroke in the cerebellar vermis, a laminectomy, left hip replacement, controlled hypertension, hypercholesterolemia managed with statins, asymptomatic gout with adjustments in his allopurinol, asymptomatic uric acid renal stones, renal insufficiency with a baseline creatinine between 1.7-1.9, stable diabetic neuropathy, right foot cellulitis MSSA, osteoarthritis with status post bilateral knee replacements, diverticulosis with internal hemorrhoids confirmed by colonoscopy, tonsillectomy, and bilateral total knee replacements and meniscectomies. Both of his parents died of stroke.

Upon physical examination in the ED, the patient was comfortable, normocephalic, atraumatic, with intact extraocular muscles, clear chest auscultation, regular heart rate and rhythm, no cyanosis, clubbing or edema, warm and dry skin, fluent speech, equal, round, and reactive pupils, midline tongue, symmetric smile, full strength in bilateral upper extremities, and normal mood and mentation. However, he was extremely vertiginous and unable to walk when attempting gait. Further examination by the Neurology Team revealed a 15 point increase in heart rate when going from supine to standing, but no change in blood pressure. He was symptomatic during this change. The neurological examination was significant for a left exotropia (present since childhood), left ataxia/dysmetria (since the cerebellar hemorrhage) and a negative ___. He did not have a significant peripheral neuropathy. He was quite unsteady when we got him out of bed, and we were able to recapitulate the symptoms that brought him to the ED. He was not safe to take a few steps within the room.

Abnormal lab results included a glucose level of 277*, urea nitrogen level of 43*, creatinine level of 2.2*, and neutrophils at 72.9*. Urine analysis showed protein at 100, glucose at 1000, and 3 hyaline casts*. Imaging studies including CT, MRI, ECHO, and MRA of the neck and brain did not reveal any acute intracranial abnormalities, infarction, pseudoaneurysm, dissection or occlusive thromboembolic filling defect. However, a 1mm outpouching of the cavernous left internal carotid artery was noted, which may be a small aneurysm or infundibulum. The ECHO also showed mild symmetric left ventricular hypertrophy with preserved regional and global biventricular systolic function, aortic valve sclerosis, and a mildly dilated aortic arch.

During his hospital stay, the patient's orthostatic hypotension and peripheral vertigo were managed with IV fluids. His blood pressure remained labile, reaching a systolic blood pressure of 206 at one point. Metoprolol was discontinued and replaced with Carvedilol 6.25 mg PO/NG BID and Amlodipine 10 mg PO/NG HS. His blood pressure remained in the 150s-160 range for the remainder of his stay, and he was safely discharged home. His vertigo had improved significantly by this point. His A1c was elevated at 9.9 during his stay, but aggressive treatment was deferred due to his history of hypoglycemia.

Upon admission, the patient was on allopurinol, multag, lasix, metoprolol, pravachol, flomax, aldactone, humilin, vitamin D3, and cinnamon. He was discharged with a diagnosis of orthostatic hypotension and vertigo. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of insulin-dependent diabetes, chronic kidney disease, hemochromatosis, atrial fibrillation, cerebellar stroke, hyperlipidemia, and hypertension, presented with fever, vomiting, scleral icterus, and right upper quadrant pain. He had intermittent bouts of abdominal pain over the past month associated with nausea and vomiting following fatty and full meals. He had no hematemesis, melena, dysuria, chest pain, or shortness of breath. His past medical history also includes osteoarthritis, diverticulosis with internal hemorrhoids, bilateral total knee replacements, bilateral meniscectomies, and gout. His parents both died of stroke.

Upon admission, his vitals were stable but labs showed elevated WBC (18.1), AST/ALT (183/245), T-bili (5.1), and creatinine (2.7). He underwent an ERCP with sphincterotomy, stone extraction, and CBD stent placement. Two round stones causing partial obstruction were seen at the biliary tree and were successfully extracted. A biliary stent was placed due to suspicion for sepsis from cholangitis. His abdominal x-ray showed the biliary stent in the right upper quadrant with no evidence of bowel obstruction. His chest x-ray showed findings consistent with CHF, with bibasilar atelectasis and small bilateral effusions.

The patient was diagnosed with ascending cholangitis, likely due to an impacted stone in the common bile duct. His bilirubin slowly trended down over the coming days. He also had left hydronephrosis, which was likely incidental as he was asymptomatic and his creatinine was at baseline. His hypoxia was due to volume overload in the setting of resuscitation and underlying CKD, which resolved with diuresis.

His medications upon admission included allopurinol, dronedarone, vitamin D, tamsulosin, spironolactone, pravastatin, amlodipine, carvedilol, furosemide, levothyroxine sodium, and insulin. He was not interested in anticoagulation despite a CHADS2 score of 5. He will need a repeat ERCP in one month and is considering a cholecystectomy. His discharge diagnosis included cholangitis, chronic kidney disease, pulmonary edema/pleural effusions in the setting of volume resuscitation, atrial fibrillation, and insulin-dependent diabetes.

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus, chronic kidney disease, hemochromatosis, atrial fibrillation, and a cerebellar stroke, was admitted due to fever and chills, suspected to be related to E.coli bacteremia. He had recently undergone an ERCP procedure with sphincterotomy, stone extraction, and CBD stent placement for cholangitis. The patient presented with fever, rigors, and nausea, but no abdominal pain. Initial labs showed elevated BUN/Cre, AST, ALT, and AP. An ultrasound revealed gallstones but no intrahepatic bile duct dilation. The patient was started on vancomycin and zosyn.

Upon arrival, the ERCP fellow determined that there was no need for an ERCP at this time. The patient was admitted for a fever workup. He experienced another episode of rigors that resolved after antibiotic administration. His initial vitals were stable and he reported no abdominal pain. His review of systems was negative for fever, chills, night sweats, headache, vision changes, rhinorrhea, congestion, sore throat, cough, shortness of breath, chest pain, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, BRBPR, melena, hematochezia, dysuria, and hematuria.

The patient's past medical history includes a cerebellar vermis hypertensive hemorrhagic stroke, insulin-dependent diabetes, hypertension, uric acid renal stones, chronic renal insufficiency, osteoarthritis, diverticulosis with internal hemorrhoids, bilateral total knee replacements, remote bilateral meniscectomies, paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, hemochromatosis, hyperlipidemia, and a history of gout. His family history includes both parents dying of stroke.

Admission physical exam showed a harsh systolic murmur, somewhat diminished air movement bilaterally, and left-sided wheezes. His abdomen was nondistended and nontender. Admission labs showed elevated glucose, urea nitrogen, creatinine, chloride, total CO2, direct bilirubin, WBC, neutrophils, and lymphocytes. His RBC, HGB, and HCT were low.

During his hospital stay, the patient's fever and rigors were managed with antibiotics. Blood cultures grew pan-sensitive E. coli and he was narrowed to ciprofloxacin for a 9-day course. His ERCP stent was removed and sludge but no pus was noted. His atrial fibrillation was managed with home medications and his electrolytes were monitored and repleted as necessary. His creatinine remained stable throughout admission. His diabetes was managed with a reduced dose of home insulin, which was titrated up based on needs. His hypertension was managed with home medications. His hypothyroidism was managed with home levothyroxine and monitored clinically. His hyperlipidemia was managed with home pravastatin. His BPH was managed with home tamsulosin. His gout was managed with home allopurinol.

The patient's medications upon admission included amlodipine, carvedilol, dronedarone, levothyroxine sodium, tamsulosin, furosemide, pravastatin, spironolactone, vitamin D, allopurinol, and insulin. The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of bacteremia, hypertension, diabetes mellitus type II, and a history of cholangitis. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was instructed to continue taking his antibiotics until he had completed the entire course and to contact his doctor or return to the emergency room if he experienced recurrent fevers or chills, or any other concerning symptoms.
