note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted for surgery due to cholangitis. His medical history includes a cerebellar vermis hypertensive hemorrhagic stroke with no residual symptoms, insulin-dependent diabetes, hypertension, asymptomatic uric acid renal stones, chronic renal insufficiency, osteoarthritis with status post bilateral knee replacements and chondrocalcinosis, diverticulosis with internal hemorrhoids, bilateral total knee replacements, remote bilateral meniscectomies, paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, hemochromatosis, hyperlipidemia, and a history of gout. 

The patient underwent a laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Prior to this, he had an ERCP, sphincterotomy, and stent placement. The stent has since been removed. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including allopurinol, Norvasc, Coreg, dronedarone, Lasix, levothyroxine, pravastatin, spironolactone, Flomax, vitamin D3, Humulin, and Regular insulin. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was hemodynamically stable, with pain well controlled. His diet was progressively advanced to a regular diet with good tolerability. He required a straight catheter once for urinary retention, after which he voided without problem. He received subcutaneous heparin and venodyne boots were used during his stay. 

Abnormal lab results included a blood glucose level of 209, UreaN of 35, and Creatinine of 2.2. His urine test showed a protein level of 100 and glucose level of 300. 

The patient's parents both died of stroke at an unspecified age. 

Upon discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile with stable vital signs. He was tolerating a regular diabetic diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and his pain was well controlled. He was discharged home without services. His discharge diagnosis was cholangitis.

The patient is a male with a history of insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus, a hemorrhagic stroke with no residual symptoms, hypertension, paroxysmal atrial fibrillation not anticoagulated, chronic kidney disease, recent cholangitis post-ERCP, and a recent admission for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy. He also has a history of osteoarthritis, status post bilateral knee replacements, chondrocalcinosis, diverticulosis with internal hemorrhoids, bilateral total knee replacements, remote bilateral meniscectomies, hemochromatosis, hyperlipidemia, gout, and uric acid renal stones. 

The patient presented with a fever. He had been admitted previously with cholangitis thought to be due to an obstructive CBD stone, and had undergone ERCP with sphincterotomy and stent placement. He was readmitted with E. coli bacteremia and underwent ERCP with stent removal. He was initially on vancomycin, cefepime, and metronidazole, which was narrowed to ciprofloxacin (later changed to augmentin due to a medication interaction). He was admitted for an elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy, which was uneventful except for postoperative urinary retention requiring a straight catheter. 

On presentation, he was slightly fatigued and had a temperature of 101.5. He denied any abdominal pain other than slight discomfort at the site of his incisions, and denied nausea, vomiting, cough, or chest pain. He had not had a bowel movement for 4 days. His initial labs were significant for a WBC of 21.8, bicarbonate of 19, BUN/Cr of 45/2.6, and urinalysis with large leukocytes and few bacteria. His right upper quadrant ultrasound was negative for biliary dilation. 

His physical exam on admission showed decreased breath sounds in the right base, several laparoscopy incision sites healing well without surrounding erythema, and no signs of infection. His discharge physical exam showed slight suprapubic tenderness to palpation, no rebound or guarding, no CVAT, and 4 laparoscopy incision sites healing well without surrounding erythema, fluctuance, or drainage. 

His labs on admission showed a WBC of 21.8, RBC of 3.98, Hgb of 11.7, Hct of 37.0, Neuts of 89.9, Lymphs of 4.7, Glucose of 286, UreaN of 45, Creat of 2.6, HCO3 of 19, and urinalysis with RBC of 9 and WBC of over 182. His labs on discharge showed a WBC of 21.6, RBC of 4.07, Hgb of 11.7, Hct of 37.9, Glucose of 293, UreaN of 46, Creat of 2.4, and HCO3 of 21. 

His right upper quadrant ultrasound showed status post cholecystectomy with no intra or extrahepatic biliary dilatation, trace free fluid along the inferior edge of the liver, but no fluid collection in the gallbladder fossa, and a small right pleural effusion. Blood and urine cultures were pending at discharge. 

The patient's fever was determined to be secondary to a urinary tract infection, and he was treated with a 7-day course of levofloxacin. His incision did not appear infected, and there were no liver function test abnormalities, so wound site infection or recurrence of biliary process were unlikely. His leukocytosis was downtrending post-cholecystectomy. His paroxysmal atrial fibrillation was controlled with home medications of dronedarone and carvedilol, but he was not anticoagulated. His diabetes was managed with home insulin, his chronic kidney disease was managed with renally dosed medications, and his hypertension was managed with carvedilol, amlodipine, and spironolactone. 

Upon admission, the patient was on allopurinol, amlodipine, carvedilol, dronedarone, insulin, levothyroxine, pravastatin, spironolactone, and tamsulosin. His parents both died at age ___ of stroke.

note_summary:
The patient is a male with a history of atrial fibrillation/flutter, hemorrhagic cerebrovascular accident (CVA), hypertension (HTN), diabetes mellitus (DM), chronic kidney disease (CKD), and cholecystectomy. He presented with a two-month history of fever, nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain. His past medical history also includes cerebellar vermis hypertensive hemorrhagic stroke, insulin-dependent diabetes, hypertension, an episode of syncope associated with volume depletion, uric acid renal stones, chronic renal insufficiency, osteoarthritis with bilateral knee replacements, diverticulosis with internal hemorrhoids, bilateral total knee replacements, autonomic triggered atrial fibrillation, typical AVNRT with slow pathway modification, chronic pericardial effusion, mild aortic stenosis, and cholecystectomy. 

His symptoms worsened with subjective fever, shaking chills, daily retching, anorexia. Repeat labs showed leukocytosis (WBC 17), acute on chronic kidney disease (Cr up to 3.9) with hyperglycemia (gluc 500s), ESR 114, CRP 260. He was admitted twice for fever/hypotension/lactic acidosis of unknown etiology, AoCKD (BUN 136, Cr 3.4). Urine culture was positive for Klebsiella pneumonia. He was discharged without antibiotics. He was also admitted for fever, nausea, dry heaves, epigastric pain. All tests were normal. He was discharged with primary care physician (PCP) follow up. PCP started him on oral ciprofloxacin BID for one week, which he completed. His symptoms completely resolved while on antibiotics. 

However, he again developed fevers, shaking chills, and dry heaves. He represented for the third time with fever to 101, tachycardia (110), dry heaves, and shaking chills. He also reported a non-productive cough that he attributes to his medication usage. He denied headache, ocular symptoms, ear symptoms, sore throat, congestion, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, rash, travel, sick contacts. 

Physical exam showed spider angiomas, systolic ejection murmur, left lower lobe rales, non-tender prostate. Labs were notable for Cr 2.3 (baseline), BUN 38, Glucose 314, WBC 14.9 (87% N), H/H 9.5/30.3, plt 308, CRP 124, Iron 23, TSH 2.2, INR 2.5, lactate 1.6. Imaging showed pericardial effusion without tamponade, generally enlarged heart, no focal hepatic lesion, status post cholecystectomy, small right pleural effusion, low lung volumes exaggerate heart size, which is mildly enlarged, trace bilateral pleural effusions. 

He was given SC Insulin 12 Units. He has a history of E coli bacteremia with concern for chronic bacterial prostatitis. It is unclear whether this was treated. His parents both died at age ___ of stroke. 

Upon admission, he was on Allopurinol, Metoprolol Succinate XL, Rivaroxaban, Pravastatin, Furosemide, 70/30 insulin, esomeprazole magnesium, olmesartan, Tamsulosin, Levothyroxine Sodium, Calcitriol, and Vitamin D.

Upon admission, he was on Allopurinol, Metoprolol Succinate XL, Rivaroxaban, Pravastatin, Furosemide, 70/30 insulin, esomeprazole magnesium, olmesartan, Tamsulosin, Levothyroxine Sodium, Calcitriol, and Vitamin D.

The male patient, with a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, type II diabetes, chronic kidney disease, atrial fibrillation managed with Xarelto, congestive heart failure, and a cerebellar hemorrhage, was admitted due to malaise, chills, nausea/vomiting, and abdominal pain. He has had several recent admissions for recurrent fevers and intermittent nausea/vomiting of unclear etiology. The patient also has a history of sludge in the common bile duct, diabetic retinopathy, AVNRT, nephrolithiasis, osteoarthritis, cerebellar hemorrhage/CVA secondary to hypertension, BPPV, hypothyroidism, heterozygous for hemochromatosis, and bilateral knee replacements. His parents both died of CVAs.

Upon admission, the patient's vitals were stable, but labs showed a WBC of 17 (90% polys), and lactate of 5.8. LFTs showed cholestasis: ALP 214 and Tbili 1.7. CT abdomen/pelvis showed pneumobilia, suggestion of ductal dilation, and high density material in left hepatic duct. The patient was given IV fluids, IV levaquin and flagyl, and was transferred for possible ERCP for presumed cholangitis. 

The patient underwent ERCP and stent placement, as well as PICC placement. His symptoms improved while on antibiotics, but recurred when they were stopped. His endocrinologist prescribed him Cipro once weekly to suppress an unknown chronic indolent infection. 

Abnormal lab results included WBC 17, lactate 5.8, ALP 214, Tbili 1.7, and a number of other abnormal blood results including Hgb 13.1, Hct 39.1, RDW 17.8, RDWSD 53.6, Neuts 90.2, Lymphs 4.5, Eos 0.1, AbsNeut 13.67, AbsEos 0.01, PTT 37.0, AST 94, AlkPhos 162, cTropnT 0.24, TotProt 5.3, Calcium 8.3, %HbA1c 8.0, eAG 183, Glucose 121, UreaN 25, Creat 2.2, K 2.1, WBC 214, RBC 414, Polys 15, Lymphs 63, Monos 5, Plasma 2, Meso 6, Macro 4, Other 5, and RBC 3 in urine. 

Imaging showed pneumobilia without evidence of intrahepatic or extrahepatic ductal dilation, a stone within the proximal common hepatic duct which extends into the right hepatic duct, a stone in the central left hepatic duct, mild intrahepatic bile duct dilation upstream of these stones with mild associated active cholangitis, a 2.8 cm dermal/subcutaneous lesion along the right anterior abdominal wall, possible thickening of the urothelium of the proximal ureter with a small amount of proximal dilation, cardiomegaly and moderate bilateral nonhemorrhagic pleural effusions, severe aortic valve and coronary artery calcifications, subtotal atelectasis of the left lower lobe, and a dilated main pulmonary artery suggesting pulmonary hypertension. 

The patient was diagnosed with VRE bacteremia, endocarditis, aortic stenosis, acute on chronic systolic heart failure, and cholangitis. He was discharged with instructions to continue antibiotics for 6 weeks, monitor his weight daily, and follow up with infectious disease, urology, and dermatology. He will also need a repeat ERCP to remove the stent that was placed. His medications upon admission included Allopurinol, Calcitriol, Furosemide, Levothyroxine Sodium, Pravastatin, Rivaroxaban, Tamsulosin, 70/30 insulin, Ciprofloxacin HCl, Metoprolol Succinate XL, Promethazine VC-Codeine, and Zolpidem Tartrate.

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of hypertension (HTN), hyperlipidemia (HLD), type II diabetes mellitus (DMII), chronic kidney disease (CKD), atrial fibrillation (a-fib) on Xarelto, congestive heart failure (CHF), cerebellar hemorrhage, and recent admission for cholangitis, VRE bacteremia with possible endocarditis, and acute on chronic diastolic heart failure, presented from rehab for lower extremity edema and weight gain. During his recent admission, he underwent ERCP with stent placement and was started on IV antibiotics for a 6-week course of daptomycin. He also had a large transudative left-sided pleural effusion status post thoracentesis and was diuresed with IV Lasix. He was discharged to rehab on torsemide 40 mg twice daily. He had his biliary stent removed and was found to have pneumonia (PNA) and was started on levofloxacin. His creatinine was checked at rehab and was elevated from 2.3 baseline to 2.8, thus he was referred to the cardiac direct access unit for evaluation. 

Upon arrival, the patient complained of leg swelling and increased orthopnea. He denied fever, chills, chest pain, or shortness of breath (SOB). He was found to have a skin tear on his toe. His physical exam showed 3+ pitting edema bilaterally in lower extremities, a systolic murmur loudest at apex, and crackles at bases bilaterally. 

Admission labs showed abnormal neutrophils (76.8), lymphocytes (13.6), absolute neutrophils (7.29), RBC (4.03), HGB (10.7), HCT (34.1), MCHC (31.4), RDW (17.1), RDWSD (52.2), cTropnT (0.15), urea nitrogen (49), and creatinine (2.5). Discharge labs showed abnormal RBC (3.73), Hgb (9.5), Hct (31.3), MCH (25.5), MCHC (30.4), RDW (16.6), RDWSD (50.7), glucose (145), urea nitrogen (48), creatinine (2.5), and calcium (8.3). 

The patient's echocardiogram showed mild regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction with severe hypokinesis of the inferolateral wall, mild aortic valve stenosis, moderate mitral regurgitation, mild pulmonary artery systolic hypertension, and increased PCWP. His CXR showed improved lung volumes and ventilation at both lung bases, with persistent mild vascular congestion and moderate left and small right pleural effusions. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was diuresed with IV Lasix and placed on a Lasix drip with improvement in his lower extremity edema. He completed a course of levofloxacin for pneumonia diagnosed at rehab facility as well as finished course of daptomycin for presumed endocarditis. His discharge weight was 88.3kg. 

Conditions ruled out include infective endocarditis as repeat TTE did not show vegetation. His creatinine remained stable ~2.5 throughout admission, indicating no worsening of CKD. His hypertension was managed with lisinopril and his a-fib was managed with home rivaroxaban and metoprolol. His DMII was managed with home lantus and HISS. His gout was managed with home allopurinol. His insomnia was managed with home trazodone. His parents both died of CVAs.

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, type II diabetes, chronic kidney disease, atrial fibrillation on Xarelto, congestive heart failure, and cerebellar hemorrhage, was admitted due to shortness of breath and a large left pleural effusion. He reported that his symptoms started two weeks prior, with increasing dyspnea on exertion and orthopnea preventing him from sleeping. He denied any chest pain, cough, fevers, chills, leg swelling, changes in weight, or recent changes in medication. He was given Nitropaste and IV Lasix prior to transfer. His labs showed a Trop of 0.2 and BNP >35,000. He had a left pleural effusion which was tapped and found to be transudative. 

His past medical history includes sludge in the common bile duct, hypertension, type II diabetes on insulin, chronic kidney disease, diabetic retinopathy, atrial fibrillation on Xarelto, AVNRT, congestive heart failure with recovered LVEF (20% -> 65%), nephrolithiasis (uric acid stones), UTI (most recently pan-sensitive Klebsiella), osteoarthritis, cerebellar hemorrhage/CVA secondary to hypertension, BPPV, hypothyroidism, heterozygous for hemochromatosis, and bilateral knee replacements. His family history includes both parents dying of CVAs. 

Upon admission, his physical exam showed irregularly irregular heart rhythm with systolic murmur, decreased breath sounds on the left, and no cyanosis, clubbing, or edema. His admission labs showed abnormal glucose (412), urea nitrogen (53), creatinine (3.1), and lactate (2.5). His discharge labs showed abnormal RBC (4.13), hemoglobin (12.0), hematocrit (37.5), RDW (17.9), glucose (63), urea nitrogen (55), creatinine (3.2), and phosphate (4.8). 

His echocardiogram showed moderate global left ventricular hypokinesis (LVEF = 35%), moderate aortic valve stenosis, moderate (2+) mitral regurgitation, mild pulmonary artery systolic hypertension, and a small pericardial effusion. Bilateral pleural effusions were present. His chest X-ray showed a left pleural effusion and increased left retrocardiac opacification which could possibly represent atelectasis and/or consolidation. His pyrophosphate scan showed tracer uptake in the myocardium compatible with transthyretin-related amyloidosis. 

During his hospital stay, he was treated for acute and chronic systolic heart failure, left pleural effusion, acute on chronic kidney disease, atrial fibrillation, and type II NSTEMI. His home medications included allopurinol, calcitriol, levothyroxine, rivaroxaban, tamsulosin, trazodone, metoprolol, acetaminophen, potassium chloride, torsemide, insulin, lisinopril, and pravastatin. Upon discharge, he was prescribed hydralazine, isosorbide dinitrate, insulin, lisinopril, torsemide, acetaminophen, allopurinol, calcitriol, levothyroxine, pravastatin, rivaroxaban, tamsulosin, and trazodone. His metoprolol and potassium were held. His primary diagnosis upon discharge was acute on chronic systolic heart failure, transthyretin-related cardiac amyloidosis, and pleural effusion. His secondary diagnoses were acute on chronic kidney disease, type II NSTEMI, and atrial fibrillation.

note_summary:
The patient is a male with a past medical history of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, amyloidosis, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, type II diabetes mellitus, chronic kidney disease, atrial fibrillation managed with Xarelto, cerebellar hemorrhage, and bilateral knee replacements. He has undergone ERCP with sphincterectomy for sludge in the common bile duct and cholecystectomy. He also has a history of nephrolithiasis, urinary tract infections, osteoarthritis, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypothyroidism, and is heterozygous for hemochromatosis. His family history includes both parents dying from cerebrovascular accidents.

The patient presented with dyspnea and was found to have a congestive heart failure exacerbation. He reported a weight gain of approximately 10 lbs despite multiple titrations of his diuretic dosing. He was unable to lie flat due to severe dyspnea. His troponin was elevated. 

On physical examination, he had bibasilar crackles in the lower part of both lung fields and 1+ edema to mid-shin. His admission labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.98), Hgb (11.5), Hct (36.1), MCHC (31.9), RDW (17.5), RDWSD (57.7), PTT (40.3), Glucose (199), UreaN (48), and Creat (3.2). His discharge labs showed abnormal values for RBC (4.15), Hgb (12.0), Hct (38.0), MCHC (31.6), RDW (17.4), RDWSD (58.5), UreaN (58), Creat (3.4), and Cl (95). Imaging showed a left pleural effusion and cardiomegaly with stable pulmonary vascular congestion.

During his hospital stay, the patient's dyspnea was attributed to his congestive heart failure exacerbation and chronic left pleural effusion. He was diuresed with lasix and metolazone and transitioned to home torsemide. His home lisinopril was stopped due to cough and he was started on losartan. His atrial fibrillation was managed without beta blockers due to his cardiac amyloidosis. His Xarelto was discontinued due to insurance issues and he was started on apixaban. His creatinine levels increased to 3.4 from a baseline of 3.1, likely due to cardiorenal etiology initially, but remained stable at 3.4 at discharge.

Upon admission, the patient was on allopurinol, calcitriol, levothyroxine, lisinopril, rivaroxaban, pravastatin, tamsulosin, trazodone, hydralazine, torsemide, 70/30 insulin, isosorbide mononitrate, and ferrous sulfate.

note_summary:
The patient is an elderly male with a history of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) secondary to amyloidosis, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, diabetes, chronic kidney disease (CKD), atrial fibrillation on rivaroxaban, and a cerebellar hemorrhage. He was admitted due to shortness of breath and concerns of infection. He had recently fallen and was in rehab for physical therapy. He had a chest X-ray at rehab that indicated possible pneumonia and was started on levofloxacin. His white blood cell count was also elevated. His torsemide dosage was decreased due to concerns of prerenal azotemia. 

Upon admission, his vitals were stable, but labs showed elevated white blood cell count, low hemoglobin, low sodium, high BUN, high creatinine, and high troponin. His chest X-ray showed moderate left and small right pleural effusions with pulmonary vascular congestion, but no new consolidation. He was given IV vancomycin and IV zosyn. 

The patient reported a fall on his hip two weeks prior, which resulted in a 'chipped hip' and 'bruised shoulder.' He denied chest pain, shortness of breath, fevers, chills, congestion, and runny nose. He reported a worsening cough while at rehab, which he attributed to being given losartan. He denied any gastrointestinal symptoms and reported no lower extremity swelling or dizziness. 

His past medical history includes sludge in the common bile duct, hypertension, type 2 diabetes on insulin, CKD, diabetic retinopathy, atrial fibrillation on Xarelto, systolic congestive heart failure, nephrolithiasis, urinary tract infections, osteoarthritis, cerebellar hemorrhage, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypothyroidism, heterozygosity for hemochromatosis, and bilateral knee replacements. His parents both died of cerebrovascular accidents. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's kidney function worsened, leading to the initiation of hemodialysis. He developed a hematoma on his left lower extremity and his mental status worsened. He was found to have silent aspiration with all consistencies of liquid during a video swallow study and was made strict NPO. He also had a fracture of the right femoral greater trochanter and a non-displaced subtrochanteric fracture of the right femur. His heart failure was managed with diuretics and he was switched from apixaban to warfarin due to concerns of bleeding risk with renal function. His pneumonia and leukocytosis resolved with a 5-day course of antibiotics. 

Upon discharge, his medications included acetaminophen, acyclovir, ascorbic acid, Cepacol, Lidocaine patch, Miconazole powder, Nephrocaps, OxyCODONE, Ramelteon, sevelamer carbonate, TraMADol, Warfarin, Allopurinol, HydrALAZINE, Glargine, Isosorbide Mononitrate, Torsemide, TraZODone, Calcitriol, Ferrous Sulfate, Levothyroxine Sodium, Pravastatin, and Tamsulosin. His discharge diagnoses included acute heart failure exacerbation with reduced ejection fraction, amyloidosis, acute on chronic kidney disease, end stage renal disease requiring hemodialysis, left foot hematoma, pneumonia, right femoral greater trochanter fracture, diabetes, and hypertension. He was discharged to extended care.

note_summary:
The patient is an elderly male with a history of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (LVEF 35%) due to amyloidosis, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, diabetes, end-stage renal disease (ESRD) recently initiated on hemodialysis, atrial fibrillation on warfarin, and a recent fall resulting in a fracture of the right femoral greater trochanter. He was recently discharged after a complex hospital course that included worsening kidney disease leading to dialysis, a hematoma of the left lower extremity, and delirium. He was admitted due to hyperglycemia and altered mental status. 

The patient reported increased urination, thirst, occasional memory problems, and pain from a sacral pressure wound. He also mentioned dissatisfaction with his rehab and a belief that his blood glucose was high because 'they are out to get him'. Initial vitals and physical exam were notable for a chronically ill appearance, a systolic murmur, occasional nonsensical statements, and multiple skin ulcers. 

Lab results showed an INR of 4.9, PTT of 43.1, and a fluctuating fasting blood sugar level. Imaging revealed bilateral pulmonary opacities consistent with pulmonary edema and no acute abnormalities or hemorrhage on a non-contrast head CT. The patient was given Tylenol, insulin, and 500cc of normal saline. 

His past medical history includes cholecystectomy, hypertension, type 2 diabetes on insulin, chronic kidney disease, diabetic retinopathy, atrial fibrillation on Xarelto, systolic congestive heart failure, nephrolithiasis, urinary tract infections, osteoarthritis, cerebellar hemorrhage/CVA secondary to hypertension, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypothyroidism, heterozygosity for hemochromatosis, and bilateral knee replacements. His parents both died of cerebrovascular accidents. 

Admission labs showed abnormal values for RBC (4.44), Hgb (12.0), Hct (39.0), MCHC (30.8), RDW (19.1), RDWSD (58.2), PTT (43.1), glucose (239), urea nitrogen (58), creatinine (4.0), sodium (133), and potassium (5.6). Discharge labs showed abnormal values for RBC (4.17), Hgb (11.5), Hct (36.7), MCHC (31.3), RDW (19.3), RDWSD (58.8), PTT (43.3), glucose (128), urea nitrogen (35), creatinine (3.0), and chloride (93). 

During his hospital stay, the patient's altered mental status improved after dialysis, suggesting a possible uremic cause. His hyperglycemia was managed with insulin. His ESRD was managed with hemodialysis. His skin impairments were managed with wound care and dermatology consultations. His heart failure was managed with dialysis and Torsemide. His atrial fibrillation was managed by withholding warfarin due to a supratherapeutic INR. 

Upon discharge, the patient was confused at times but alert and interactive. He required assistance for ambulation. His discharge diagnosis was altered mental status. His discharge medications included Glargine, Acetaminophen, Allopurinol, Ascorbic Acid, Calcitriol, Cepacol, Ferrous Sulfate, Hydralazine, Isosorbide Mononitrate, Levothyroxine Sodium, Lidocaine Patch, Miconazole Powder, Nephrocaps, Oxycodone, Pravastatin, Ramelteon, Sevelamer Carbonate, Tamsulosin, Torsemide, Tramadol, Trazodone, and Warfarin (to be restarted when INR is within therapeutic range). He was advised to weigh himself daily and contact his doctor if his weight increased by more than 3 lbs. He was discharged to an extended care facility.

note_summary:
The patient, a female with a history of hyperlipidemia managed with Zetia and hypothyroidism post-thyroiditis, was admitted due to difficulties in finding words. She reported a history of aphasia for several weeks, during which she intermittently struggled to find the correct word or produced nonsensical words. She also reported a decline in vision, but did not provide further details. She denied experiencing diplopia, blurriness, dizziness, balance changes, weakness, or paresthesias. 

Upon admission, her vitals were stable and her physical examination was largely unremarkable. Neurologically, she was awake, alert, and oriented to person and place, but only partially to time. She demonstrated some difficulty with naming and complex arithmetic, but her speech was fluent, her verbal comprehension was intact, and she was able to read without hesitation or errors. Her cranial nerves were intact, and she showed no motor or sensory deficits. Her deep tendon reflexes were normal, and her coordination and gait were also normal. 

Imaging studies revealed a large left frontal extra-axial mass causing effacement of the left lateral ventricle and a 7 mm leftward midline shift, likely representing a meningioma. The mass was unchanged on subsequent MRI, which also ruled out the presence of new masses. 

During her hospital stay, the patient was initially evaluated by the neurology team and ruled out for stroke. She was started on steroids and Keppra. Despite initial refusal of medications, she agreed to her treatment regimen after discussion. She expressed a desire to be discharged and return for further workup. Her condition remained stable throughout her stay, with continued mild expressive aphasia. 

Upon admission, she was taking Levothyroxine 50mcg 5x/week, Zetia 1 tab daily, and Vitamin D/Calcium. Her discharge diagnosis was a left frontal brain lesion. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.

note_context:
Finally, a minority of affected individuals present developmental delay. When present, develop- mental delay is most often associated with cra- niosynostosis and/or hydrocephalus, suggesting that learning disability is an extremely rare pri- mary manifestation of LDS. Common neurora- diological findings are dural ectasia and Arnold-Chiari type I malformation (Rodrigues et al. 2009). The precise incidence of those two findings is unknown.

Other recurrent findings that need further doc- umentation include muscle hypoplasia, dental problems with enamel dysplasia, allergic disease with seasonal allergies, asthma/sinusitis, eczema and important gastro-intestinal problems: food allergy, eosinophilic esophagitis, inflammatory bowel disease.

note_summary:
The patient, a female with a history of hyperlipidemia managed with Zetia and hypothyroidism post-thyroiditis managed with Levothyroxine, was admitted due to word-finding difficulties. She reported a history of intermittent dysarthria, but her speech was clear upon examination. She denied experiencing chest pain, shortness of breath, fever, chills, dizziness, blurred vision, or diplopia. 

During her hospital stay, she underwent a left craniotomy for mass resection. The surgical pathology report identified the mass as an atypical meningioma, WHO Grade II. Pre-surgical planning wand study showed a relatively stable extra-axial mass lesion in the left operculum, likely consistent with meningioma. Postoperative CT and MR imaging showed expected postsurgical changes, including hyperdense blood products and pneumocephalus, and a persistent 7mm rightward shift of normally midline structures. No definite nodular area of enhancement was observed to suggest residual tumor, but nonspecific enhancement along the surgical resection cavity was noted, which could represent postsurgical changes or residual tumor.

The patient's hospital course was complicated by 600 mL blood loss during surgery and postoperative aphasia. She was also noted to be restless, requiring Benadryl and Ativan for agitation. Her neurological exam improved over time, with her speech returning albeit with mild expressive aphasia. She remained confused at times. Her lab results showed a rising BUN, which was managed with IV fluids and resolved with repeat testing. Physical therapy recommended acute rehabilitation services at discharge.

Upon admission, the patient was taking Levothyroxine 50mcg 5x/week, Zetia 1 tab daily, Vitamin D/Calcium, and a multivitamin. During her hospital stay, she was given a range of medications including Acetaminophen, Bisacodyl, Dexamethasone, Dextrose, Docusate Sodium, Famotidine, Glucagon, Glucose Gel, Heparin, HydrALAZINE, Insulin, LevETIRAcetam, Levothyroxine Sodium, OxycoDONE, Senna, Sodium Chloride, and Ondansetron.

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with a left frontal mass, specifically an atypical meningioma. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory but occasionally required assistance or aid. She was discharged to an extended care facility for further recovery and rehabilitation.

note_summary:
The patient is a female with a history of left fronto-parietal meningioma, hypothyroidism, and hyperlipidemia. She presented with an episode of transient aphasia. Prior to this episode, she had tripped while running and fell on her face, sustaining multiple facial bruises. She denies any loss of consciousness and says that the fall happened so quickly, she couldn't protect her face. No major medical needs were reported after this fall. 

The patient's neurological problem began when she presented with a complaint of word finding difficulty and speaking gibberish over three weeks prior to presentation. She understood written and spoken language and denied other cortical signs. A gadolinium-enhanced brain MRI was performed and disclosed an extra-axial, left frontotemporal mass with heterogeneous enhancement exerting mass on the adjacent left frontotemporal lobes and left insula with an 8 mm left-to-right midline shift. She underwent left-sided craniotomy and tumor resection, pathology was consistent with atypical meningioma, WHO Grade II. She was discharged on one gram of levetiracetam twice daily and dexamethasone 4 mg twice daily. However, the patient stopped taking levetiracetam as of an unspecified date.

Her family history does not include early stroke, but her father had an unspecified disease. Upon admission, her medications included Ezetimibe 10 mg PO DAILY, Levothyroxine Sodium 50 mcg PO DAILY, and Latanoprost 0.005% Ophth. Soln. 1 DROP BOTH EYES QHS.

During her hospital stay, her labs showed an elevated UREA N-21* and TSH-6.5*. Her head CT and CTA images showed post-op changes and mild encephalomalacia from the prior left fronto-parietal meningioma resection. No acute pathology was found. No major vessel stenosis was observed. An MRI of the brain showed a small left parafalcine SDH and left fronto-parietal post-op changes plus a 1.4cm enhancing lesion in that area suggestive of recurrent meningioma.

Her transient aphasia is thought to be due to a focal seizure. Given a focal lesion seen on MRI that may have been the seizure trigger, she was started on Keppra 500gm bid. She had no further events since being in the hospital. She was discharged with a diagnosis of seizure and meningioma. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory and independent upon discharge.

An MRI of the brain showed a small left parafalcine SDH and left fronto-parietal post-op changes plus a 1.4cm enhancing lesion in that area suggestive of recurrent meningioma.

Her transient aphasia is thought to be due to a focal seizure. Given a focal lesion seen on MRI that may have been the seizure trigger, she was started on Keppra 500gm bid. She had no further events since being in the hospital. She was discharged with a diagnosis of seizure and meningioma. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory and independent upon discharge.

The patient, a female under the OBSTETRICS/GYNECOLOGY service, was admitted due to advanced ovarian cancer. She had undergone a total laparoscopic hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, omentectomy, left ureterolysis, lysis of adhesions, and cystoscopy. She initially presented to her primary care physician with complaints of lower abdominal pain for two weeks. Initial tests including UA, KUB, and pelvic ultrasound were negative. A CT scan suggested a cecal mass, but a subsequent colonoscopy was unremarkable. She was found to have a small pleural effusion, which was tapped and found to contain malignant cells consistent with metastatic adenocarcinoma. The cells were positive for CK7, PAX8, and WT1 and negative for CK20, TTF-1, napsin A, CDX2, GCDSP-15, mammoglobin ER, and RCC antigen, suggesting a gynecologic primary site. She also reported dull lower abdominal pain, increased reflux, decreased appetite, 10-pound weight loss, and shortness of breath with exertion.

Her past medical history includes hypercholesterolemia, obesity, depression, esophageal reflux, allergic rhinitis, arthritis, type 2 diabetes, diverticulosis, sleep apnea, hypertension, and a history of basal cell carcinoma. She has had several surgeries including tonsils and adenoids removal, left inguinal hernia repair, umbilical hernia repair, bilateral knee replacement, D and Cs for perimenopausal bleeding, and removal of a ganglion from her right hand. She has a history of infertility and has never been on birth control pill or hormonal replacement therapy. 

Her family history is positive for breast cancer in her mother and a paternal cousin. There is no family history of ovarian, uterine, cervical, or colon cancers. There is a family history of diabetes, high blood pressure, and depression.

On the day of discharge, her physical exam was stable. Her blood work showed abnormal RBC count (3.48*), Hemoglobin (11.2*), Hematocrit (31.9*), MCH (32.1*), RDW (16.5*), and Phosphorus (4.7*). 

During her hospital stay, her pain was managed with IV toradol and dilaudid, and later transitioned to percocet and motrin. She was able to tolerate a regular diet, void spontaneously, and ambulate independently. She was discharged home in stable condition.

Her medications upon admission included amlodipine, fluticasone, lisinopril/HCTZ, metformin, methocarbamol, omeprazole, zofran, simvastatin, and effexor XR. She has an allergy to Iodinated Contrast Media - IV Dye.

The patient, a female with a past medical history of positive PPD, BMI 34, chronic hypertension, and prolactinoma with hyperprolactinemia, was admitted due to abdominal pain. She reported a sudden onset of pain in the epigastrium and right upper quadrant, which was crampy in nature. Accompanying symptoms included several episodes of nonbilious nonbloody emesis, subjective fevers, and chills. She had experienced similar symptoms in the past, which lasted only several hours. The patient had no known allergies except for ibuprofen. Her family history was non-contributory.

Upon admission, the patient was alert and oriented, with vitals of 97.9 91 137/95 16 99% RA. Her abdomen was soft, nondistended, and moderately tender in the right upper quadrant. She underwent a laparoscopic cholecystectomy due to her acute cholecystitis. Post-surgery, she was hemodynamically stable and her pain was well controlled with an oral regimen as needed. She was encouraged to mobilize out of bed and ambulate as tolerated, which she was able to do independently.

Lab results showed abnormal levels of MCHC at 31.8, glucose at 117, ALT at 356 and 296, AST at 277 and 341, AlkPhos at 139 and 128, and GGT at 154. All other lab results were within normal ranges. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of cholecystitis post laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory. She was given detailed discharge instructions regarding activity, how she may feel post-surgery, incision care, bowel management, pain management, and medication use. She was advised to follow up in the Acute Care Surgery clinic.

note_summary:
The male patient was admitted due to right knee pain. His past medical history includes a history of MRSA, hyperlipidemia, a lung nodule, and a splenectomy. He underwent a right total knee arthroplasty (TKA) during his stay. His family history is not clear. Upon physical examination, he appeared well and in no acute distress. His vital signs were stable and his pain was well-controlled. His lower extremity musculoskeletal examination showed that the incision was healing well with staples and scant serosanguinous drainage. His thigh was full but soft, with no calf tenderness. His strength was not specified, but he had no focal deficits neurologically. 

His lab results showed several abnormal findings. His RBC count was consistently low, ranging from 3.39 to 3.97. His hemoglobin and hematocrit levels were also consistently low, ranging from 10.7 to 12.3 and 30.3 to 35.8 respectively. His WBC count was consistently high, ranging from 10.7 to 19.7. His glucose level was slightly elevated at 118. 

During his hospital stay, he was taken to the operating room for the aforementioned procedure. The surgery was uncomplicated and he tolerated the procedure well. He received perioperative IV antibiotics. His postoperative course was remarkable for a postoperative fever, but his urinalysis, blood cultures, and chest X-ray were all negative. His pain was initially controlled with a PCA and then transitioned to oral pain medications. He received lovenox for DVT prophylaxis. His surgical dressing was changed and the incision was found to be clean and intact. He was seen daily by physical therapy and his labs were checked and repleted accordingly. 

Upon admission, he was on Pravastatin 20mg daily. His discharge diagnosis was right knee osteoarthritis. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. However, he required assistance or aid for ambulation. He was discharged to home with services in stable condition.

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted to the orthopaedics service due to a complaint of right knee flexion contracture. His orthopaedic history includes a total knee replacement, after which he experienced significant loss of range of motion. He attempted home physical therapy but reported stiffness in his knee, particularly when sitting down or getting into a car. His physical examination revealed a well-healed incision, flexion to just 85 degrees, and stability to anterior and posterior drawer, as well as varus and valgus stress. Radiographs showed a well-positioned total knee replacement with no evidence of hardware loosening. The patient was diagnosed with postoperative arthrofibrosis of the right knee and underwent a right knee manipulation under anesthesia. 

His past medical history includes a history of MRSA, hyperlipidemia, a lung nodule, and a splenectomy. His family history was not mentioned. Upon admission, he was taking pravastatin 40qd, vardenifil 10mg qhs, and loratidine as needed. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's pain was initially managed with an epidural and PCA, then transitioned to oral pain medications. He received lovenox for DVT prophylaxis starting on the morning of postoperative day 1. His foley was removed and he was voiding independently thereafter. Continuous passive motion (CPM) was started immediately postoperatively and the patient was seen daily by physical therapy. Labs were checked throughout his hospital stay and repleted accordingly. At the time of discharge, the patient was tolerating a regular diet, was afebrile with stable vital signs, and his pain was adequately controlled on an oral regimen. His operative extremity was neurovascularly intact and the incision sites remained well-healed. His weight-bearing status was weight bearing as tolerated on the operative extremity. 

The patient was discharged to home with services in stable condition. His discharge diagnosis was right knee contracture. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive, but required assistance or aid for ambulation. No abnormal lab or test results were reported.

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted to the orthopaedic service due to worsening left knee pain that was not relieved by over-the-counter medication and was interfering with his daily activities. His past medical history includes MRSA, hyperlipidemia, and a lung nodule. He has no known drug allergies. His family history was not discussed. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Keflex, Pravastatin, and Loratadine. He underwent a left total knee replacement procedure, which was uncomplicated and well-tolerated. Post-operatively, his pain was initially managed with a PCA and then transitioned to oral pain medications. He also received Lovenox for DVT prophylaxis. His surgical dressing was changed and the incision was found to be clean and intact. He was seen daily by physical therapy and was able to void independently after the removal of the foley on the second post-operative day. 

The patient's lab results showed several abnormal findings. His WBC was elevated at 18.0, 20.8, and 23.4. His RBC was low at 3.19 and 3.14. His Hgb was low at 9.9 and 11.0. His Hct was low at 29.5 and 29.4. His glucose was high at 216. His Phos was low at 2.4. 

The patient was discharged home in a stable condition with a diagnosis of left knee osteoarthritis. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory but required assistance or aid. His weight-bearing status was weight bearing as tolerated on the operative extremity. His hematocrit was acceptable and his pain was adequately controlled on an oral regimen. The operative extremity was neurovascularly intact and the wound was benign.

note_summary:
The patient, a female with a history of depression and infertility, was admitted due to abdominal pain and malaise. She had been feeling unwell for about a week, with symptoms including nausea, poor appetite, and upper abdominal pain. She had recently undergone hysteroscopy with propofol sedation and had taken Percocet and ibuprofen for post-procedure discomfort. She had not taken any acetaminophen since the procedure. She also reported more concentrated urine and lighter colored bowel movements. She had no vomiting or diarrhea, but had one day of mild constipation due to Percocet use. She had been eating crackers instead of normal meals to alleviate the pain. 

The patient's past medical history includes depression, migraines, ovarian cystectomy, a right patellar fracture, fibrocystic breasts, and multiple pregnancies via IVF, one of which resulted in a miscarriage at 6.5 weeks and another that was terminated due to Turner syndrome. She also had a cellulitis on her foot following ant bites and completed a course of Keflex. She takes Celexa, Klonopin, Folgard, and fish oil. 

Her family history includes a sister who is also homozygous for the MTHFR gene mutation and has infertility issues. Her father has liver disease, but is an alcoholic and obese. Both parents have hypertension. 

Upon admission, her vital signs were normal, but initial labs showed marked transaminitis, with ALT > 2200 and AST > 1300. An abdominal ultrasound with Doppler showed no abnormalities. Her discharge physical exam was largely unremarkable, with no visible jaundice and no asterixis. 

Lab results showed abnormal RBC count (3.84), MCH (32.9), monocytes (14), eosinophils (8), atypical lymphocytes (1), and ferritin (370). Her ALT, AST, AlkPhos, and total bilirubin levels were all significantly elevated throughout her stay. Her IgG was slightly elevated (1696). Her hepatitis work-up was negative, and her ceruloplasmin was normal. 

The patient was diagnosed with acute hepatitis. Her LFTs were in the AST >1500; ALT > 2500; Bili 3; AlkPhos 200 range. She had a clear viral prodrome, and a recent trip to an unspecified location. No clear drug induced precipitant was identified. Her serologies did not reveal a cause. Pending studies included CMV, EBV, HEV, HSV; anticardiolipans. 

Her medications upon admission included Folgard, Citalopram, Fish Oil, Klonopin, Percocet, Ibuprofen, Acetaminophen, and prenatal vitamins. Her discharge medications included Folgard, omega-3 fatty acids, clonazepam, and citalopram. She was instructed to decrease her doses of Citalopram and Clonazepam, and to avoid Tylenol. 

The patient was discharged home in a clear and coherent mental state, alert and interactive, and able to ambulate independently. She was instructed to follow up with her doctor and to have her labs checked on the day of her appointment.

The patient was diagnosed with acute hepatitis. Her LFTs were in the AST >1500; ALT > 2500; Bili 3; AlkPhos 200 range. She had a clear viral prodrome, and a recent trip to an unspecified location. No clear drug induced precipitant was identified. Her serologies did not reveal a cause. Pending studies included CMV, EBV, HEV, HSV; anticardiolipans. 

Her medications upon admission included Folgard, Citalopram, Fish Oil, Klonopin, Percocet, Ibuprofen, Acetaminophen, and prenatal vitamins. Her discharge medications included Folgard, omega-3 fatty acids, clonazepam, and citalopram. She was instructed to decrease her doses of Citalopram and Clonazepam, and to avoid Tylenol. 

The patient was discharged home in a clear and coherent mental state, alert and interactive, and able to ambulate independently. She was instructed to follow up with her doctor and to have her labs checked on the day of her appointment.

note_context: Pregnancy is recognized as a risk factor for aortic dissection in women in the general population and in women with MFS128,129,273. The risk for aortic dissection is reported both during pregnancy, primarily during the third trimester, and up to several months postpartum273–275.However, pregnancy-associated dissections in the general population are rare 273,276–278. In women with MFS, the risk of dissection is associated with an increased aortic root dilatation rate in pregnancy279. In the majority of reported dissections, the diagnosis of MFS had not been made prior to the aortic dissection273. Thus, a missed diagnosis of MFS is an important risk factor for pregnancy-associated dissections and underscores the crucial role for diagnosis and cascade assessment of family members274. Importantly, women with MFS should have genetic counselling and a cardiovascular assessment prior to pregnancy. note_summary:
The patient is a female who was admitted for post-dates induction of labor at 41 weeks and 3 days gestation. Her past medical history includes depression, migraines, and a homozygous MTHFR mutation. She has a history of infertility and has undergone several procedures including an elective D&E at 13 weeks for Turner syndrome, a D&C following a spontaneous abortion with a late diagnosis of heterotopic pregnancy, and a laparoscopic right salpingectomy for a ruptured ectopic pregnancy. She also had a spontaneous abortion at 6 weeks. Her surgical history includes an ovarian cystectomy and a laparoscopic right salpingectomy. She has no significant family history.

Upon admission, her physical examination was normal. However, her lab results showed several abnormalities. Her WBC count was elevated at 11.5, 20.4, and 21.0. Her RBC count was low at 3.82, 2.88, and 2.98. Her hemoglobin levels were also low at 13.0, 9.7, 9.8, 9.5, and 9.5. Her hematocrit levels were low at 37.4, 28.2, 28.4, 23.4, 27.2, and 27.1. Her MCH was high at 34.1 and 33.7, and low at 33.0, 32.3, and 31.7. Her RDW was high at 13.1, 13.2, 15.1, 16.3, and 16.8. Her AlkPhos was high at 177.

During her hospital stay, she underwent a spontaneous vaginal delivery of a male infant. Post-delivery, she had persistent vaginal bleeding and underwent a dilation and curettage procedure. She was given a dose of kefzol postpartum and PO cytotec to aid in placental expulsion. Postpartum, she was hypotensive and had a decrease in hematocrit from 37.4 to 28.2. She received one unit of pRBCs for blood loss anemia. Her post-transfusion hematocrit was 28.4. Repeat hematocrit on postpartum day one was 23.4 and then 20.9 at which time the patient was symptomatic. She received an additional 2 units of pRBCs for blood loss anemia with an appropriate increase in hematocrit to 27.2. She had a negative evaluation for ongoing bleeding including a normal bedside ultrasound.

Her medications upon admission were citalopram, clonazepam, and prenatal vitamins. Her discharge diagnoses included vaginal delivery, postpartum hemorrhage, retained products of conception, post dilation and curettage, postpartum symptomatic blood loss anemia, and blood transfusion. She was discharged in stable condition.

note_context: women with MFS should have genetic counselling and a cardiovascular assessment prior to pregnancy. Aortic diameter remains a risk factor for dissection, and current recommendations for the care of women with MFS who are considering conceiving are based on aortic root diameter. However, type B dissections associated with pregnancy may occur without substantial aortic dilation273,275The risk of pregnancy-associated type A dissection is considered low when maximal diameter of the aortic root is < 4.0 cm, provided β-adrenergic receptor blockers are used throughout the pregnancy and the postpartum period. When the aortic root diameter is > 4.5 cm, prophylactic valve-sparing aortic root replacement surgery should be considered before the pregnancy280. When the diameter is between 4.0 and 4.5 cm, the decision is made on a case-by-case basis. Importantly, aortic root replacement does not completely eliminate the risk of dissections during preganacy275,281. Vaginal delivery is possible when

Diffuse peripheral arterial aneurysms are well known features of other connective tissue disorders such as the Loeys-Dietz syndrome (genes: TGFBR1, TGFBR2) and Ehlers-Danlos syndrome of the vas- cular type (gene: COL3A1). They have traditionally not been associated with MFS, but the results of two recent studies suggest otherwise. In a retro- spective analysis of about 140 MFS patients (all with FBN1 mutations) who had undergone routine thoraco-abdominal CT or MRI as part of their follow-up, about one third had incidental ﬁndings of peripheral vascular aneurysms. Fifty-ﬁve per cent of the peripheral aneurysms were deemed to require intervention.4 A prospective series using
note_summary:
The patient, a female with a history of depression, migraines, and homozygous for MTHFR mutation, was admitted due to lightheadedness and shortness of breath. She had recently given birth via normal spontaneous vaginal delivery at 41 weeks, which was complicated by manual extraction of placenta requiring dilation and curettage, and postpartum hemorrhage requiring blood transfusion of 3 units of packed red blood cells. Her hematocrit levels dropped to 20.9 and were 27.1 upon discharge. She also reported feeling lightheaded and experiencing tachycardia/palpitations, shortness of breath, and minor chest tightness. She noted minor spot bleeding and significant fatigue. She also reported right greater than left lower extremity swelling during pregnancy and currently, but it was not evaluated. 

Her past medical history includes an elective dilation and evacuation at 13 weeks for Turner syndrome, a spontaneous abortion with dilation and curettage with late diagnosis of heterotopic pregnancy resulting in laparoscopic right salpingectomy for ruptured ectopic pregnancy, another spontaneous abortion at 6 weeks, and infertility. She also had an ovarian cystectomy and laparoscopic right salpingectomy in the past. 

Upon admission, her medications were Citalopram 40mg daily and Clonazepam 2mg daily. Her physical examination showed she was tired-appearing, pale, with dry lips, supple neck, no lymphadenopathy, normal heart sounds, clear lungs, soft and non-tender abdomen, minimal staining on peripad, and palpable pulses with capillary refill less than 1.5 seconds. 

Her lab results showed abnormal values for white blood cell count (11.7), red blood cell count (3.87), hematocrit (35.7), red cell distribution width (16.0), neutrophils (80.0), lymphocytes (12.0), and partial thromboplastin time (38.2). 

During her hospital stay, a CT scan revealed two small acute pulmonary thromboemboli in the right lower lobe and few small subpleural pulmonary nodules and subsegmental atelectasis in the right lower lobe, which may represent atypical infection or underlying inflammatory changes. She was started on therapeutic Lovenox (1mg/kg BID). She remained hemodynamically stable throughout her hospitalization and was discharged on hospital day #2 with instructions to continue Lovenox as prescribed. Her discharge diagnosis was postpartum pulmonary embolism.

note_summary:
The patient, a female under the OBSTETRICS/GYNECOLOGY service, was admitted due to retained products of conception and a right groin hematoma. She has a history of homozygous MTHFR mutation, depression, migraines, and infertility. Her surgical history includes an ovarian cystectomy and a laparoscopic right salpingectomy. She has had four pregnancies, with one resulting in a live birth, one elective D&E at 13 weeks due to Turner syndrome, one spontaneous abortion with D&C due to a late diagnosis of heterotopic pregnancy resulting in a laparoscopic right salpingectomy for a ruptured ectopic pregnancy, and one spontaneous abortion at 6 weeks. Her family history includes a sister who had a stillbirth, possibly due to a clotting disorder.

Upon admission, the patient was on Celexa 40 mg daily, clonazepam 2 mg qhs, PNV, Fe, fish oil, folgard, and lovenox 60mg BID. She reported persistent bleeding since delivery, similar to a menstrual period, with no pain, cramping, lightheadedness, or syncope. She denied having a fever. The bleeding worsened over the last week, and she passed large clots, prompting her to seek evaluation. An ultrasound revealed heterogenous material measuring 6.2 x 2.4 x 4 cm with marked internal vascularity, consistent with retained products of conception. Her hematocrit was 34.3 and platelets were 281.

She also complained of worsening right groin pain for the past five days, with an area of swelling across her inguinal ligament. The pain was exacerbated by weight bearing and hip flexion. She denied leg weakness but reported some numbness and tingling in her right foot. An ultrasound of the right groin revealed a heterogeneous, avascular 5.1 x 5.4 x 2.4 cm collection within the right iliopsoas muscle, likely a hematoma.

During her hospital stay, she underwent an ultrasound-guided D&C and placement of an IVC filter. Her blood work showed abnormal RBC, Hgb, and Hct values on multiple occasions. An ultrasound of the right thigh showed a minimally decreased size of the right upper hematoma. Hematology was consulted and recommended restarting lovenox 40mg SC daily. The patient reported improvement of pain with pain medications and no change in strength of the right lower extremity. She was discharged in good condition with instructions to rest and follow up with her hematologist and ob/gyn.

note_context: (Figure 1)9. In the majority of MFS patients, thoracic aortic disease begins as an asymptomatic enlargement of the aortic root, which progressively enlarges over time to form an aneurysm (a weakening of the artery wall leading to a bulge or distention). Medications can slow the rate of enlargement but do not prevent it. The aortic aneurysm becomes unstable as it enlarges and ultimately may lead to an acute ascending aortic dissection (known as the type A dissection based on Stanford classification), which is a life-threatening complication of MFS and can lead to decreased life expectancy 10,11. A dissection is a tear in the inner lining (intima) of the aorta that allows blood to enter the wall of the aorta and split through the middle layer (media) of the wall, causing the layers of the aorta to separate or dissect. (Figure 1) Type A aortic dissections are associated with high morbidity and mortality, and prior to the availability of aortic surgery, a large proportion of individuals

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to cellulitis in his left foot and leg, which had been accompanied by fevers and chills for two days. His medical history includes spina bifida occulta, which was diagnosed during a spinal exploratory surgery. He also had an artificial urinary sphincter installed twice. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was alert and oriented, with no apparent distress. His lower extremity exam revealed absent protective sensation, absent vibratory testing of the lower limbs, and absent Achilles DTRs. There was no clonus. Dermatologic examination showed ascending redness and swelling up the left medial groin, increased redness and swelling to the left midfoot and forefoot, and a soft tissue mass on the plantar aspect of the left foot beneath the third and fourth metatarsal head regions. There were no open lesions, no fluctuance, and no drainage. Musculoskeletal examination revealed generalized muscle wasting of both calves.

Lab results showed abnormal RBC count at 4.23*, Hemoglobin at 13.0*, Hematocrit at 39.0*, Calcium at 8.3*, and Phosphorus at 2.6*. During his hospital stay, the patient was started on IV clindamycin and PO cipro, which improved the cellulitis. An ultrasound revealed a gelatinous mass measuring 0.5 cm x 1.6 cm x 1.8 cm. A bedside aspiration resulted in a likely hematoma; cultures have not grown any microbiology to date. The patient was not on any medication upon admission. 

The patient was discharged with stable vital signs and labs, and improved cellulitis. He was instructed to weight bear as tolerated, and to perform daily betadine dressing changes to the area of aspiration/callus until cleared by his doctor. His discharge diagnosis was cellulitis in the left foot. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory upon discharge.

note_summary:
The patient is a male with a history of spina bifida localized to his legs, which has gradually progressed to weakness and decreased sensation in his legs bilaterally. He also has bilateral clubbed feet and reports worse weakness in his right leg than left. He has never had any sharp or shooting pain associated with his legs or spina bifida. He also has a history of urinary incontinence for which an artificial sphincter was implanted, and occasional fecal incontinence. 

The patient presented with a one-week history of worsening left neck pain, which began after he traveled to his son's graduation. He reported fatigue, increasing stiffness, and soreness that persisted in his left neck. The pain worsened and prevented him from sleeping, prompting him to seek medical attention. He was admitted to an outside hospital where increased reflexes bilaterally were noted and a CT head scan showed an arachnoid cyst. The CT neck scan did not show any fracture or abscess. He was transferred for further management. He received dilaudid at the outside hospital which he reports lessened the pain intensity and duration significantly. 

Upon admission, the patient reported intermittent sharp, shooting pain and spasm in his left neck that radiates across his shoulders and down his left arm. The pain occurs for 4 seconds duration and goes away without treatment, leaving him feeling sore, but in no pain. The pain can be exacerbated by movement. He denies tingling, numbness, or associated weakness. He denies headache, blurry vision, tinnitus, joint stiffness or pain, or change in his weakness. He reports that the pain comes approximately several times per hour. 

The patient's family history includes a mother who is alive and takes warfarin, a father who died at an unknown age from an unknown cause and took warfarin, an uncle with brain cancer, and a grandfather with an unknown type of cancer. 

Physical examination upon admission showed a well-appearing man with limited range of motion in his neck. He had normal vitals, and his neck was supple with no thyromegaly. His cardiac rhythm was regular with normal S1, S2, and no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. His lungs were clear to auscultation bilaterally with good air movement. His abdomen was soft, non-tender, and non-distended with normoactive bowel sounds. His extremities showed no cyanosis or edema, but he had clubbed feet and toes. Neurological examination showed he was alert and oriented, with normal cranial nerve function. He had normal strength in upper extremities and decreased strength in lower extremities, particularly in hamstrings and right ankle. His reflexes were 3+ and equal in upper extremities, but 0 in Achilles. 

Lab results on admission showed abnormal RBC count at 4.32, Hgb at 13.0, Neuts at 72.4, and Glucose at 106. Phosphorus was also low at 2.2. Imaging showed a focal area of altered signal intensity in the C2 vertebral body with mild enhancement and an adjacent small Schmorl's node. There was also a small amount of fluid/inflammatory thickening in the prevertebral/retropharyngeal location. At C5-6, there was a mild bulge indenting the thecal sac, and at C6-7, there were degenerative changes with disc osteophyte complex indenting the thecal sac and bilateral moderate foraminal narrowing with deformity on the C7 nerves. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with cervical disc disease. His exam was consistent with bilateral upper extremity hyperreflexia and normal strength and sensation in the upper extremities. MRI showed evidence of disc herniation and foraminal narrowing at C7-C8, consistent with the patient's shooting pain. There was no evidence for acute cord compression. He was also found to have asymmetric left-sided muscle spasm. An arachnoid cyst was discovered on head CT, but it was asymptomatic. The patient was not on any medications upon admission.

His abdomen was soft, non-tender, and non-distended with normoactive bowel sounds. His extremities showed no cyanosis or edema, but he had clubbed feet and toes. Neurological examination showed he was alert and oriented, with normal cranial nerve function. He had normal strength in upper extremities and decreased strength in lower extremities, particularly in hamstrings and right ankle. His reflexes were 3+ and equal in upper extremities, but 0 in Achilles. 

Lab results on admission showed abnormal RBC count at 4.32, Hgb at 13.0, Neuts at 72.4, and Glucose at 106. Phosphorus was also low at 2.2. Imaging showed a focal area of altered signal intensity in the C2 vertebral body with mild enhancement and an adjacent small Schmorl's node. There was also a small amount of fluid/inflammatory thickening in the prevertebral/retropharyngeal location. At C5-6, there was a mild bulge indenting the thecal sac, and at C6-7, there were degenerative changes with disc osteophyte complex indenting the thecal sac and bilateral moderate foraminal narrowing with deformity on the C7 nerves. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with cervical disc disease. His exam was consistent with bilateral upper extremity hyperreflexia and normal strength and sensation in the upper extremities. MRI showed evidence of disc herniation and foraminal narrowing at C7-C8, consistent with the patient's shooting pain. There was no evidence for acute cord compression. He was also found to have asymmetric left-sided muscle spasm. An arachnoid cyst was discovered on head CT, but it was asymptomatic. The patient was not on any medications upon admission.

However, in some patients who survived beyond childhood, emphysema can be mild or even absent, even though lung function tests shows obstructive lung disease (Fig. 13.2) (Urban et al. 2009; Su et al. 2015). Precipitating factors to respiratory failure include recurrent respiratory infections, pulmo- nary hypertension, tracheomalacia, and positive pressure ventilation (Urban et al. 2009; Callewaert et al. 2013; Su et al. 2015). Bladder diverticula can be found in about 50% of patients, appear and enlarge progressively, and, in addition to ure- thral weakness and prolapse, contribute to void- ing problems. VUR and fragility of the collective system can induce hydronephrosis. Patients are at risk of upper and lower urinary tract infections (Urban et al. 2009; Callewaert et al. 2013). note_summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted with the chief complaint of a left renal abscess. She has a past medical history of right pyelonephritis and renal abscesses. She reported developing left flank pain and fever approximately 10 days prior to admission. She was initially treated with IV antibiotics for diagnosed left pyelonephritis and was sent home with Levofloxacin 750mg daily. After 7 days of continued fevers, her prescription was renewed at 500mg daily. A CT scan showed a left renal abscess. Upon admission, she reported no fever and no flank pain.

During her hospital stay, she underwent a CT-guided aspiration and drainage of the left renal abscess performed by Interventional Radiology. The patient was placed on Unasyn and Gentamicin, and urine cultures were sent for testing. The cultures showed less than 10K organisms. The patient experienced a fever of 101.8 after the procedure, but had no fevers the following day. She was discharged in stable condition on Bactrim. 

The patient's lab results showed several abnormal findings: WBC-12.6*, RBC-3.53*, Hgb-11.5*, Hct-33.4*, and MCH-32.5*. The CT scan revealed findings in the interpolar region of the left kidney that could represent necrotic changes or an abscess, stable liver hypodensities likely representing hemangiomas, and a fibroid uterus. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Levofloxacin 500mg daily and Microgestin. The patient has no known allergies to drugs. There is no significant family history reported. The patient was advised to seek medical attention for fevers, worsening pain, drainage or excessive bleeding from the puncture site, chest pain, or shortness of breath. She was instructed to call the doctor's office in a week to arrange a voiding cystourethrogram.

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted following a mechanical fall from a ladder while fixing a carport roof. He landed on his left side on top of the ladder. He did not lose consciousness or experience amnesia. The fall was not witnessed. The patient tried to 'sleep it off' overnight and woke up with significant left chest wall pain. He presented to a medical facility where rib fractures were noted as well as a tiny left pleural effusion at the site of his fractures. There were no other injuries identified. He was then transferred to another facility for further management and assessment. 

The patient's past medical history includes psoriasis and COPD. He has undergone bilateral cataract repair and bilateral inguinal hernia repair. His family history includes brain cancer in his daughter. 

Upon admission, the patient was comfortable, with a temperature of 98.4, heart rate of 80, blood pressure of 154/75, respiratory rate of 13, and oxygen saturation of 93. His chest was clear to auscultation, with left-sided rib tenderness. His cardiovascular system was regular in rate and rhythm. His abdomen was soft and non-tender. There was no cyanosis, clubbing, or edema in his extremities. His skin showed no rash. His speech was fluent and his mood was normal. There were no petechiae. 

The patient's blood work showed a glucose level of 100, urea nitrogen of 15, creatinine of 1.0, sodium of 142, potassium of 4.5, chloride of 108, bicarbonate of 24, and anion gap of 10. A subsequent blood test showed a glucose level of 98, urea nitrogen of 16, creatinine of 0.9, sodium of 144, potassium of 4.5, chloride of 108, bicarbonate of 21 (marked with an asterisk as an abnormal finding), and anion gap of 15. 

Imaging results showed no acute findings on EKG, no acute injury on CT scans of the head and cervical spine, tiny left pleural effusion and rib fractures on CT of the chest, and chronic changes related to COPD. The CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis showed no acute injury. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was hemodynamically stable and in no respiratory distress. He was admitted for pain control and pulmonary toileting. Tertiary exam was negative for any additional injuries. By the second day of his hospital stay, his pain was well controlled. He ambulated early and frequently, was adherent with respiratory toilet and incentive spirometry, and actively participated in the plan of care. He received subcutaneous heparin and venodyne boots were used during his stay. 

Upon discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile with stable vital signs. He was tolerating a regular diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and his pain was well controlled. He was discharged home without services. His discharge diagnoses were left rib fractures and a tiny left pleural effusion. 

The patient's medications upon admission were a steroid cream as needed and albuterol as needed.

note_context:
Skeletal complications

The principal skeletal issues that require frequent intervention are deformities of the anterior chest and the spine. Pectus excavatum (depression of the sternum) is common and often asymmetrical. As the ribs grow, the deformity can progress and becomes irreversible once growth is complete. The indications for surgical repair are clinically important diminished lung capacity (dyspnea on exertion), compression of cardiovascular structures, or need for ascending aortic repair.202 A minimally invasive approach (Nuss procedure) is successful in most patients, but in the most severe deformity a substernal bar needs to be inserted until bone healing is complete203.

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to a left bicondylar tibial plateau fracture and partial patellar tendon tear. This injury was sustained during a traumatic incident in white out conditions, resulting in the patient falling into a 4-foot culvert. He was initially evaluated at another facility before being transferred for definitive management. Since the injury, the patient has not been placing weight on his left leg and has been managing his pain with Tylenol. He has no prior history of left leg injury. 

The patient's past medical history includes psoriasis and COPD. He has previously undergone bilateral cataract repair and bilateral inguinal hernia repair. His family history is notable for brain cancer in his daughter. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Tylenol for pain management. During his hospital stay, he underwent a left tibial plateau ORIF with partial patellar tendon repair, which he tolerated well. Post-procedure, he was initially given IV fluids and IV pain medications, and progressed to a regular diet and oral medications. He was also given routine antibiotics and anticoagulation. His hospital course was otherwise unremarkable. 

At the time of discharge, the patient's pain was well controlled with oral medications, his incisions were clean, dry, and intact, and he was voiding spontaneously. His left lower extremity was touchdown weight bearing in an unlocked hinged knee brace. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive, although he required assistance or aid for ambulation. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of left bicondylar tibial plateau fracture and partial patellar tendon tear. His discharge medications included Acetaminophen, Aspirin, Bisacodyl, OxyCODONE, Senna, and Vitamin D. He was instructed to follow up with his doctor as per routine.

note_context: needs assessment by a specialist in physical medicine and rehabili- tation for appropriate therapeutic and prevention strategies. People with chronic (widespread) musculoskeletal pain and neuropathic pain should be managed in a multidisciplinary setting also including the palliative care specialist for the appropriate use of opioids and drugs (neuropathic pain), specialist in physical therapy and rehabili- tation medicine, and psychologist. Rest, cold or hot anti- inflammatory drugs represent the routine clinical care of sprains, strains and soft-tissue injuries. Recurrence may be reduced by physical therapy programs improving proprioception, muscle tone and core stability. In children, neurodevelopmen- tal attributes of JHM (i.e. developmental coordi- nation disorders, learning difficulties, attention deficit/hyperactivity disorder) should be man- aged according standard procedures with focus on a likely underlying defect in proprioception.

context of MFS, eg, in individuals with high myopia). Increased globe length and corneal ﬂattening are seen in MFS, but they have unclear speciﬁcity and are not routinely measured by ophthalmologists. Given that myopia is very common in MFS, is routinely monitored, and tends to show early onset, high severity and rapid progression, myopia of >3 diopters contrib- utes to the systemic score for diagnosis. However, since myopia is quite a common ﬁnding in the general population we have only attributed one point to it in the systemic score.
note_summary:
The patient, a male with a past medical history of psoriasis and mild COPD, presented with acute onset diplopia. He first noticed blurred vision while driving, which he initially attributed to sunspots. He had difficulty focusing on objects, especially when looking to the left, and noticed that things appeared side by side. He did not experience any headaches, vertigo, other weakness or numbness in any other part of his body. He saw an ophthalmologist who advised him to go to the hospital for further evaluation. He denies any fevers, chills, weight loss, tick bites or rash. His family history includes brain cancer in his daughter.

Upon admission, the patient was not taking any medications. His physical examination was largely unremarkable, with normal vitals and no significant findings in the general, HEENT, pulmonary, abdomen, and extremities examinations. Neurologic examination revealed limited abduction of the left eye, which could not be overcome by vestibular ocular reflexes. There were no other deficits in extraocular movements in either eye. There was mild decreased proprioception at the great toes bilaterally. 

Lab results showed abnormal RBC count (4.20), Hemoglobin (12.5), Hematocrit (38.9), and RDWSD (47.8). Other lab results including glucose, urea nitrogen, creatinine, sodium, potassium, chloride, bicarbonate, anion gap, ALT, AST, AlkPhos, total bilirubin, %HbA1c, eAG, triglycerides, HDL, CHOL/HD, LDLcalc, TSH, and CRP were within normal limits.

Imaging studies included a CT head without contrast, CTA head, CTA neck, and MRI brain without contrast. The CT head showed no acute intracranial process and mild paranasal sinus disease involving the ethmoidal air cells and left maxillary sinus. The CTA head showed no occlusion, flow limiting stenosis, or large aneurysm, and patent dural venous sinuses. The CTA neck revealed atherosclerotic calcifications and severe narrowing at the origin of the left vertebral artery which arises directly from the aortic arch. The MRI brain showed no acute intracranial abnormality or acute infarction, mild changes of chronic white matter microangiopathy, and mild pansinus mucosal thickening without air-fluid levels.

The patient was diagnosed with an isolated left sixth nerve palsy. Common etiologies such as microvascular or ischemic sixth nerve palsy, or idiopathic causes were considered. Lyme disease was also considered given his outdoor activities, and serologies were sent but were still pending at the time of discharge. The patient was also noted to have severe centrilobular emphysema on CTA head/neck, which was stable compared to prior. The patient was advised to wear an eye patch as needed to help with diplopia, follow up on Lyme serologies, and consider antihypertensive agent, statin, aspirin. The patient was discharged home with a clear and coherent mental status, alert and interactive level of consciousness, and independent ambulatory activity status.

Discussion

Our	 cohort	 consisted	 of	 86	 eyes	 of	 43	 patients,	 all	 bilateral	 with	 a	 mean	 presenting	 age	 of	 23.9	 ±	 14.8	 years.	According	 to Groth et al., 	the	maximum	global	prevalence	of	MFS	was	 6.5/100,000	 at	 the	 end	 of	 2014.	 The	 median	 incidence	 was	 0.19/100,000	(range:	0.0–0.7).	Groth	et al.[1] found a median age at	diagnosis	of	19.0	years	(range:	0.0–74).	We	could	not	find	 information	 regarding	 the	 prevalence	 and	 incidence	 of	 the	 condition	in	India;	however,	we	found	0.019%	prevalence	at	our	 center	in	the	current	study.	Jang	et al.[10] reported that the overall prevalence	of	MFS	was	2.27	per	100,000	individuals	in	Korea.
