note_summary:
The male patient was admitted due to left hip pain, which was diagnosed as osteoarthritis. His past medical history includes hypertension. He underwent a left total hip arthroplasty, which was uncomplicated and well-tolerated. Post-operative pain was initially managed with a PCA and then transitioned to oral pain medications on the first post-operative day (POD#1). The patient also received lovenox for DVT prophylaxis starting on POD#1. The surgical dressing was changed on POD#2 and the surgical incision was found to be clean and intact without erythema or abnormal drainage. The patient was seen daily by physical therapy and labs were checked throughout the hospital course and repleted accordingly. At the time of discharge, the patient was tolerating a regular diet and feeling well. The patient's hematocrit was acceptable and pain was adequately controlled on an oral regimen. The operative extremity was neurovascularly intact and the wound was benign. The patient's weight-bearing status is weight bearing as tolerated on the operative extremity.

Upon admission, the patient was taking lisinopril 5qd, Vit C, Vit B complex, and MVI. The patient's physical exam showed no acute distress, stable vital signs, well-controlled pain, and no focal deficits. The incision was healing well with staples and scant serosanguinous drainage. The thigh was full but soft with no calf tenderness. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (3.77), Hgb (11.9), Hct (34.3), Glucose (145 and 102), Calcium (8.3), and Phos (2.6). The patient was discharged to home with services in stable condition. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. His activity status was ambulatory but required assistance or aid. The patient was instructed to return to the emergency department or notify his physician if he experienced any severe pain not relieved by medication, increased swelling, decreased sensation, difficulty with movement, fevers greater than 101.5, shaking chills, increasing redness or drainage from the incision site, chest pain, shortness of breath or any other concerns.

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted to the orthopedics service with the chief complaint of right hip pain. He had previously undergone a left total hip arthroplasty (L THA) and was now experiencing pain in his right hip due to osteoarthritis (OA). The patient had failed conservative management and elected to undergo definitive surgical management. His past medical history includes hypertension (HTN), obstructive sleep apnea (OSA), right bundle branch block (RBBB), and chronic back pain, for which he had received multiple steroid injections and opiates. 

Upon physical examination, the patient appeared well and was not in acute distress. His vital signs were stable and his pain was well-controlled. His respiratory, cardiovascular, gastrointestinal, genitourinary, neurologic, and psychiatric systems were all normal. Examination of the lower extremity revealed a healing incision with staples and scant serosanguinous drainage. The thigh was full but soft, with no calf tenderness. The patient had good strength, and his skin was intact with normal vascular and nerve function distally. His toes were warm.

The patient underwent a total hip arthroplasty on his right hip. The surgery was uncomplicated and the patient tolerated the procedure well. He received perioperative IV antibiotics. Postoperatively, the patient's pain was initially controlled with a PCA, which was then transitioned to oral pain medications on postoperative day (POD) #1. He received lovenox for DVT prophylaxis starting on the morning of POD#1. The foley was removed on POD#1 and the patient was voiding independently thereafter. The surgical dressing was changed on POD#2 and the surgical incision was found to be clean and intact without erythema or abnormal drainage. The patient was seen daily by physical therapy. Labs were checked throughout the hospital course and repleted accordingly. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (3.95), Hgb (12.5), Hct (35.4), and MCHC (35.4). The patient's WBC count was also elevated at 14.3. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Enoxaparin Sodium, Gabapentin, Ibuprofen, Lisinopril, OxycoDONE (Immediate Release), Ascorbic Acid, Fish Oil (Omega 3), Vitamin B Complex, and Vitamin E. 

The patient was discharged in stable condition with a diagnosis of osteoarthritis in the right hip. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory but required assistance or aid.

The male patient, with a history of hypertension (HTN), obstructive sleep apnea (OSA), right bundle branch block (RBBB), chronic back pain, and a previous left total hip arthroplasty, was admitted due to a right periprosthetic septic hip. His chief complaint was a right thigh abscess. He underwent a right thigh irrigation and debridement and right hip arthrocentesis. 

Upon physical examination, his right lower extremity showed scant bloody fluid throughout the drain, a soft and compressible thigh, intact sensory in S/S/SP/DP/T, and a DP of 2+. His temperature was 98.3, blood pressure was 113/66, heart rate was 67, respiratory rate was 18, and oxygen saturation was 96% on room air. 

Abnormal lab results included a white blood cell count of 11.6, mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration of 31.7, red cell distribution width standard deviation of 50.3, bicarbonate level of 19, and other body fluid results showed 88% polys and 3% lymphs. 

During his hospital stay, he was initially given IV fluids and IV pain medications, which were later transitioned to a regular diet and oral medications. He was also given antibiotics and anticoagulation as per routine. His home medication, Lisinopril 20 mg PO daily, was continued throughout his hospitalization. 

The fluid from the arthrocentesis performed revealed a right periprosthetic septic hip. His hospital course was otherwise unremarkable, and he was discharged home in a clear and coherent mental state, alert and interactive, and independently ambulatory. His discharge diagnosis was a right periprosthetic septic hip. 

The patient has an allergy to Stock Ragweed Pollen Mixture. No significant family history was noted.

note_summary:
The male patient was admitted due to a right hip prosthetic joint infection. He has a history of hypertension (HTN), obstructive sleep apnea (OSA), right bundle branch block (RBBB), and chronic back pain, which has been managed with multiple steroid injections and opiates. He also has a history of right total hip arthroplasty and left total hip arthroplasty. The patient presented with a right thigh abscess and was sent for evaluation. He underwent incision and drainage (I&D) of the right thigh abscess with vacuum-assisted closure (VAC) placement, followed by a repeat I&D and right hip aspiration. He was found to have a septic right hip around the prosthetic joint. 

During his stay, the patient underwent a right total hip revision with liner and head exchange, and I&D. Postoperatively, he had a peripherally inserted central catheter (PICC) line placed, with the tip in the mid-lower superior vena cava (SVC) as confirmed by chest X-ray. His pain was managed with a combination of IV and oral pain medications. He was also given Aspirin 325 mg twice daily for deep vein thrombosis (DVT) prophylaxis. His surgical incision was clean and intact without erythema or abnormal drainage. 

The patient's lab results showed several abnormal findings including a low red blood cell count (RBC-3.33*), low hemoglobin (Hgb-9.4*), low hematocrit (Hct-29.1*), high red cell distribution width standard deviation (RDWSD-46.4*), high glucose level (Glucose-119*), and low calcium level (Calcium-8.1*). 

Upon admission, the patient was on Lisinopril 40 mg daily, Ceftriaxone 2 gm IV every 24 hours, and Buprenorphine-Naloxone (8mg-2mg) 1 tablet sublingually daily. 

The patient was discharged in stable condition, with clear and coherent mental status, alert and interactive level of consciousness, and ambulatory activity status requiring assistance or aid. He was instructed to return to the emergency department or notify his physician if he experiences severe pain not relieved by medication, increased swelling, decreased sensation, difficulty with movement, fevers greater than 101.5, shaking chills, increasing redness or drainage from the incision site, chest pain, shortness of breath or any other concerns. He was also advised to follow up with his primary physician regarding this admission and any new medications and refills.

Major criteria are represented by severe and progressive aortic and mitral valve problems, skin involvement with hyperextensibility, thin and easy bruising skin, and joint hypermobility of small joints. Minor criteria display pectus excavatum, pes planus, planovalgus, and Hallux valgus, joint dislocations, inguinal hernia. Two major criteria or one major criterion and 2 or more minor criteria allow the clinical diagnosis.33

note_summary:
The patient, a female with a history of GERD, presented with a fever for 3 days, bilateral lower extremity myalgias, and a mild headache. She had been experiencing persistent fevers that did not resolve with ibuprofen/acetaminophen and had new onset of a headache, nausea, and vomiting. She denied any chest discomfort, dyspnea/cough, abdominal pain, skin wounds, dysuria, or focal pain. She had recently undergone dental work. 

Upon examination, she was found to have a soft murmur, which she reported as being old. Her labs were notable for leukopenia with a WBC count of 2.2, H/H 13.1/37.6, and platelets unable to count due to clumping. Her LFTs revealed ALT 49, AST 51, Alkphos 70, and a lactate level of 1.1. An LP showed Protein 24, Glucose 72, WBC 1, and RBC 0 in tube 4. She was given a dose of Gentamicin. 

The patient lives in a wooded area and frequently gardens and hikes in the woods. She lives at home with her husband and four children, none of whom are ill. She denies any family history of autoimmune disease. 

Upon admission, her physical exam revealed erythema of the face on bilateral cheeks under eyelids, crossing the nasal bridge, and a faint, half-dollar sized blanching erythematous macule above the left MTP joint that was nontender and nonpruritic. 

Her admission labs showed abnormal results for WBC-1.9*, RBC-4.00*, Hct-34.8*, MCHC-36.1*, Plt Ct-80*, Glucose-131*, ALT-49*, AST-51*, and RheuFac-19*, CRP-129.0*. Her discharge labs showed abnormal results for WBC-2.9*, Hct-35.6*, MCHC-35.6*, Glucose-116*, and ALT-55*. 

Imaging studies including a CT head scan and chest x-ray were within normal limits. 

During her hospital stay, she was treated empirically for tickborne illness with doxycycline, atovaquone, and azithromycin, which resulted in resolution of her fevers and improvement in transaminitis and leukopenia. 

Her diagnostic workup ruled out meningitis, acute intracranial process, bacteremia, and hepatitis B and C. Serologies for Lyme, anaplasma, and babesia were sent and pending at discharge. 

Upon discharge, her condition was clear and coherent, alert and interactive, and ambulatory - independent. She was not on any medications upon admission.

note_summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted for inpatient chemotherapy due to a recent diagnosis of hairy cell leukemia. She has a history of type 2 diabetes, systolic congestive heart failure with a left ventricular ejection fraction of 40%, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) cirrhosis. She also has a history of squamous cell carcinoma of the lung, for which she underwent a video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) left upper lobe resection. Other medical history includes hereditary hemochromatosis and hypothyroidism. 

The patient's family history includes her father with coronary artery disease, her mother with congestive heart failure, her brother with arrhythmia status post permanent pacemaker, and her paternal grandfather with an unknown type of cancer.

Upon admission, the patient was experiencing chronic dyspnea, hot flashes, and a mild chronic cough. She was also experiencing dyspnea with any exertion and at rest. Her physical exam revealed mild respiratory distress, dry mucous membranes, no oral lesions, no cervical or supraclavicular adenopathy, normal heart sounds, clear lungs, and marked abdominal distention with splenomegaly. 

Admission labs showed abnormal values for white blood cell count (10.7), urea nitrogen (31), creatinine (1.3), chloride (94), anion gap (22), and phosphorus (4.6). Imaging showed no thoracic lymphadenopathy, status post left upper lobe segmentectomy with expected postsurgical findings and volume loss, cirrhotic appearing liver with left lobe hypertrophy, and splenomegaly to 19.8 cm. 

During her hospital stay, the patient's creatinine level acutely elevated to 1.7, but returned to baseline after her ACE inhibitor and torsemide were held. Her diabetes was uncontrolled upon admission, and her insulin regimen was adjusted. She was also treated with cladribine for her leukemia, which she tolerated well. 

Upon discharge, her labs showed abnormal values for white blood cell count (2.6), red blood cell count (3.75), hemoglobin (10.9), mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (31.7), red cell distribution width standard deviation (46.6), glucose (194), urea nitrogen (23), creatinine (1.2), and phosphorus (4.6). 

The patient's medications upon admission included allopurinol, glipizide XL, glargine, lactulose, levothyroxine sodium, lisinopril, metformin XR, metoprolol succinate XL, omeprazole, paroxetine, sitagliptin, and torsemide. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of hairy cell leukemia, splenomegaly, and diabetes. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.

The patient, a female, was admitted for the administration and monitoring of rituxan. She has a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, systolic congestive heart failure with a left ventricular ejection fraction of 40%, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, non-alcoholic steatohepatitis cirrhosis, and a recently diagnosed form of leukemia. She had previously received the first cycle of Cladribine without any acute complications. The patient reported chronic dyspnea, mild cough, and hot flashes, but no fever or chills. She also reported dyspnea with any exertion and at rest, which she stated was currently at baseline.

The patient's past medical history includes a diagnosis of lung cancer (T1aN0M0) for which she underwent a VATS LUL resection. She also has a history of hereditary hemochromatosis and hypothyroidism. The patient is a female with a history of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), chronic low back pain, cervicalgia, chronic daily headaches with muscle spasm, depression/anxiety, and has undergone tubal ligation and abdominoplasty. She presented with a two-week history of epigastric abdominal pain that had worsened the day prior to admission. The pain, described as stabbing, sometimes radiates to her upper and lower back and to her flanks bilaterally. It is constant and was initially worse with food intake, but now food does not affect it. Accompanying symptoms include mild nausea, subjective fevers, decreased appetite leading to a 5-pound weight loss, chronic feelings of lightheadedness, and chronic mid-sternal chest pain. She denies headaches, changes in vision, cough, sore throat, palpitations, shortness of breath, muscle weakness, dysuria, or paresthesias. She also denies past or present alcohol use.

Upon physical examination, she was found to be well-groomed, mildly uncomfortable, and able to move and sit up without pain exacerbation. She had pain on palpation throughout the spine and with percussion throughout the back. Her abdomen was soft, non-distended, and tender to deep palpation in the right and left upper quadrants, with no hepatosplenomegaly. Her motor strength was normal in upper and lower extremities, but her patellar and ankle reflexes were absent bilaterally. 

Lab results on admission showed an elevated creatinine level of 1.2, a low red blood cell count of 3.71, low hemoglobin of 11.4, low hematocrit of 34.6, and elevated lipase and amylase levels of 103 and 122 respectively. Imaging studies, including a right upper quadrant ultrasound and chest x-ray, were normal. 

The patient's family history includes multiple members with diabetes, coronary artery disease, and various types of cancer. Her mother has diabetes and her father died in an accident. 

During her hospital stay, her abdominal pain was managed with morphine as needed. Her lipase levels trended down from 120 to 69, while her amylase levels remained stable at 122-130. Given her chronic use of nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), gastritis was suspected and a daily proton pump inhibitor (PPI) was started. She improved symptomatically and was able to tolerate oral intake upon discharge. 

Upon admission, she was taking ibuprofen 800MG up to three times a day as needed for pain.

note_summary:
The patient, a female of undisclosed age, was admitted due to experiencing pain in her left hand for four days. She reported that the pain began in her shoulder while she was mopping and that her left hand felt cooler and numb. She denied any motor dysfunction, weakness, history of endocarditis, palpitations, fevers, or chills. 

Her past medical history includes chronic daily headaches with muscle spasm, cervicalgia with pain radiating to the bilateral upper extremities, low back pain with radiculopathy, depression/anxiety, and GERD. She has also undergone tubal ligation and abdominoplasty. Her family history is significant for diabetes, coronary artery disease, and various types of cancer. 

Upon physical examination, her left third digit was found to be numb and cyanotic with poor capillary refill, but preserved motor function. Her blood work showed abnormal levels of RBC (3.62), Hgb (9.4), Hct (31.1), MCHC (30.2), RDW (18.0), RDWSD (54.7), and PTT (85.6). 

Imaging studies revealed a nonocclusive thrombus in the right posterior tibial artery, occlusion of the distal right peroneal artery, and a short segment of focal occlusion in the left posterior tibial artery. Cortical scarring of the left interpolar kidney was also noted, suggesting prior inflammatory or vascular insult. A free-floating thrombus was found in the distal aortic arch and proximal descending thoracic aorta. Additionally, a filling defect consistent with thrombus was found at the bifurcation of the ulnar artery and common interosseous artery. 

During her hospital stay, she was started on a heparin drip and later transitioned to a lovenox bridge. Occupational therapy provided recommendations for managing her finger numbness. 

Upon admission, she was taking ferrous sulfate 325 mg once a day, omeprazole 20 mg twice a day, and tizanidine 4 mg twice a day. Her discharge diagnoses were aortic arch thrombus and left third finger ischemia. She was discharged in a clear and coherent state, awake, interactive, ambulatory, and independent.

Diffuse peripheral arterial aneurysms are well known features of other connective tissue disorders such as the Loeys-Dietz syndrome (genes: TGFBR1, TGFBR2) and Ehlers-Danlos syndrome of the vas- cular type (gene: COL3A1). They have traditionally not been associated with MFS, but the results of two recent studies suggest otherwise. In a retro- spective analysis of about 140 MFS patients (all with FBN1 mutations) who had undergone routine thoraco-abdominal CT or MRI as part of their follow-up, about one third had incidental ﬁndings of peripheral vascular aneurysms. note_summary:
The patient is a female with a history of fibromyalgia, chronic daily headaches with muscle spasm, cervicalgia with pain radiating to the bilateral upper extremities, low back pain with radiculopathy, depression/anxiety, and GERD. She has undergone tubal ligation and abdominoplasty in the past. She was recently admitted due to left upper extremity (LUE) pain, found to have an arterial clot at the bifurcation of ulnar art & common interosseous art, and a 2.5 cm distal aortic arch free-floating thrombus. She was discharged home on lovenox and warfarin. 

The patient presented with hypotension and acutely worsening LUE pain. She reported a sudden increase in pain in her left upper extremity, as well as worsening of chronic chest, back, and abdominal pain. Her blood pressure was noted to be in the 90's systolic. She reported no evidence of bleeding, no bloody stools, no weakness anywhere, and no changes in speech or vision. 

In the ED, her temperature was 97.7, heart rate 81, blood pressure 90/58, respiratory rate 18, and oxygen saturation 100% on room air. Labs were notable for INR 1.4, AST 344, ALT 220, AP 106, and Tbili 0.1. A CTA of the torso revealed the absence of a free-floating thrombus. Vascular surgery was consulted and felt there was no surgical indication. She was admitted for transaminitis workup.

Abnormal lab results included glucose at 106, ALT at 301, AST at 186, RBC at 3.56, HGB at 9.4, HCT at 31.1, MCHC at 30.2, RDW at 19.1, RDWSD at 60.1, ALT at 314, AST at 200, glucose at 116, ALT at 344, AST at 220, ALK PHOS at 106, HGB at 10.8, MCHC at 31.3, RDW at 19.2, RDWSD at 60.1, PTT at 40.8, UROBILNGN at 2, and HYALINE at 1.

The patient's right upper quadrant pain was thought to be secondary to statin-induced hepatitis, as she had an elevated AST/ALT since starting simvastatin. This was discontinued and her liver function tests and pain improved. A RUQ US was negative. A CTA of the torso was negative for ischemic hepatitis or clot-associated ischemia. Dopplers were negative. The left hand was re-evaluated by vascular surgery and appeared unchanged. Anticoagulation was continued with warfarin and lovenox.

The patient's family history includes multiple members having diabetes, coronary artery disease, and various types of cancer. Her mother has diabetes and her father died in an accident.

note_context: (Figure 1)9. In the majority of MFS patients, thoracic aortic disease begins as an asymptomatic enlargement of the aortic root, which progressively enlarges over time to form an aneurysm (a weakening of the artery wall leading to a bulge or distention). Medications can slow the rate of enlargement but do not prevent it. The aortic aneurysm becomes unstable as it enlarges and ultimately may lead to an acute ascending aortic dissection (known as the type A dissection based on Stanford classification), which is a life-threatening complication of MFS and can lead to decreased life expectancy 10,11. A dissection is a tear in the inner lining (intima) of the aorta that allows blood to enter the wall of the aorta and split through the middle layer (media) of the wall, causing the layers of the aorta to separate or dissect. (Figure 1) Type A aortic dissections are associated with high morbidity and mortality, and prior to the availability of aortic surgery, a large proportion of individuals
note_summary:
The patient, a female with a past medical history of arterial thrombus, multiple pain syndromes, and a history of tubal ligation, presented with heavy menstrual bleeding and symptoms of fatigue and dizziness. She has been on a regimen of Coumadin, Lovenox, and low dose Aspirin. The patient reported that her periods became heavier after the uptitration of her Coumadin. She has a history of regular periods without intermenstrual bleeding prior to starting anticoagulation. She also has a history of fibroid uterus but denies any sexually transmitted infections or cysts. Other past medical conditions include chronic daily headaches with muscle spasm, cervicalgia with pain radiating to the bilateral upper extremities, low back pain with radiculopathy, depression/anxiety, and GERD. She has undergone tubal ligation and abdominoplasty in the past. 

The patient's family history includes multiple members with diabetes, coronary artery disease, and various types of cancer. Her mother has diabetes and her father died in an accident. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Coumadin, Lovenox, Aspirin, and Metformin. Her physical exam showed no acute distress, and her vitals were stable. She had scant bleeding on pad and her abdomen was soft and appropriately tender with no rebound or guarding. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (3.22), HGB (8.3), HCT (27.9), MCH (25.8), MCHC (29.7), RDW (19.4), RDWSD (60.1), and PTT (36.9). 

During her hospital stay, the patient was admitted to the gynecology service due to acute on chronic anemia in the setting of anticoagulation for an arterial thrombus. Her bleeding had spontaneously decreased upon admission, but she was found to have a hct of 24. A pelvic ultrasound showed multiple small fibroids, suggesting that the bleeding was likely due to anticoagulation. She was transfused 1 unit of pRBC, which led to an appropriate rise of her hct to 27.9. Her bleeding remained stable during her stay and she was restarted on her anticoagulation with an INR of 1.8 on admission. 

The patient was diagnosed with dysfunctional uterine bleeding in the setting of anticoagulation. She was discharged home in stable condition with clear and coherent mental status, alert and interactive level of consciousness, and independent ambulatory activity status.

recommendation136,137. Importantly, pregnancy in women with MFS can augment the rate of growth of aortic root and increase the risk for aortic dissections (Box 3).

Dilatation of the tubular ascending aorta can be observed in patients with MFS and is almost always associated with substantial dilatation of the aortic root. The additional dilatation of the tubular aorta is considered as indicating a higher risk for aortic dissection than dilatation of the aortic root alone138. In individuals with MFS, the aortic dilatation is very rarely greater at the level of the tubular ascending aorta than at the aortic root, whereas this presentation is more common in patients with aortic enlargement associated with a bicuspid aortic valve (BAV; aortic valve with two leaflets instead of three)139. Primary (nondissected) aneurysms involving the arch, descending, thoracoabdominal and abdominal aorta are relatively uncommon in patients with MFS 140,141

The patient, a male with a past medical history of pancreatic adenocarcinoma treated with a pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy, radiation therapy, and chemotherapy (capecitabine and gemcitabine), hypertension, hyperlipidemia, gastroesophageal reflux disease, benign prostatic hyperplasia, and a history of small bowel obstruction, was admitted due to E. coli bacteremia and left knee septic arthritis. His other past medical conditions include mild anemia, arthritis, coronary artery disease treated with a bare-metal stent to the right coronary artery and a drug-eluting stent to D1, bilateral total knee replacements, umbilical hernias, a history of spinal meningitis, bilateral cataracts, and carpal tunnel syndrome. 

The patient's symptoms began with shaking chills, rigors, low-grade fever, and left-sided shoulder pain radiating across his chest. He was febrile upon presentation to the emergency department, but blood cultures returned positive for E. coli, resistant to Unasyn and Ampicillin. The patient was started on Cefepime, later narrowed to Cefazolin per infectious disease recommendations. He developed pain in his left knee, and arthrocentesis showed 42k WBCs with neutrophilic predominance and cultures grew E. coli. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent several procedures including PICC line placement, irrigation and debridement of the septic left total knee twice, revision of left total knee arthroplasty, and ERCP. The ERCP showed no evidence of cholangitis or obstruction. The patient remained afebrile throughout his admission. 

Lab results showed a decrease in RBC (2.88*), Hgb (8.8*), Hct (25.4*), and an increase in glucose (121*), Na (129*), and Cl (95*). The patient's discharge labs also showed a decrease in RBC, Hgb, and Hct. 

The patient's family history includes congestive heart failure in his mother, prostate cancer in his father, and colon cancer in his brother. His medications upon admission included Pancrelipase, Clopidogrel, Metoprolol Tartrate, Omeprazole, Pravastatin, Aspirin, Tamsulosin, Lisinopril, and Melatonin. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of E. coli bacteremia and E. coli septic arthritis of the left knee. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive but required assistance or aid for ambulation. His discharge instructions included continuation of antibiotics and physical therapy.

note_context: In the revised nosology, new diagnostic criteria have been deﬁned for a sporadic patient and for an index patient with a positive family history (box 1). In the absence of a conclusive family history of MFS, the diagnosis can be established in four distinct scenarios: 1. The presence of aortic root dilatation (Z-score $2 when standardised to age and body size) or dissection13 and ectopia lentis allows the unequivocal diagnosis of MFS, irrespective of the presence or absence of systemic features except where these are indicative of SGS, LDS or vEDS (table 1).
note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of small bowel obstruction, GERD, arthritis, CAD, hyperlipidemia, and BPH, presented to the emergency department with generalized abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting for one day. He reported multiple episodes of diarrhea the night prior to admission and his last formed bowel movement was 36 hours before admission. He denies hematochezia or melena, fever, chills, chest pain, or shortness of breath. His past surgical history includes a Whipple procedure for ampullary adenocarcinoma, right total knee replacement, bilateral carpal tunnel release, and umbilical hernia repair with mesh. His family history includes CHF in his mother, prostate cancer in his father, and colon cancer in his brother.

Upon admission, the patient was well-appearing, with irregular but rate-controlled heart sounds, clear lungs, and a mildly tender, moderately distended abdomen with positive bowel sounds. His vital signs were stable. He had an allergy to Morphine. His medications upon admission included Xeloda 500mg BID, Lisinopril 10mg daily, Metoprolol XL 25mg daily, Omeprazole 20mg BID, Lipram EC TID, Simvastatin 20mg daily, and Tamulosin 0.4mg daily.

Lab results showed several abnormalities, including decreased RBC (3.73), HGB (12.0), HCT (35.3), MCH (32.3), and increased RDW (16.5). Later results showed decreased WBC (2.6), RBC (4.21), HCT (37.9), MCH (33.9), and increased MCHC (37.6), RDW (17.7), glucose (166), urea nitrogen (21), and lactate (2.8). Total bilirubin was also elevated at 1.6.

Imaging of the abdomen showed mildly distended loops of small bowel with no definite evidence of obstruction, but early and/or partial small bowel obstruction could not be excluded. A CT scan of the abdomen with contrast showed mildly dilated small bowel without a clear transition point, suggesting possible early or partial small bowel obstruction or ileus.

During his hospital stay, the patient was made NPO and started on IV fluids. An NG tube was placed after an episode of emesis, and a foley was also placed. His diet was gradually advanced from clear liquids to soft solids, which he tolerated well. He was discharged in good condition, tolerating a regular low residue diet, with good bowel function, and with pain well controlled. His discharge diagnosis was partial small bowel obstruction.

note_context:
A minority of MFS patients (10%–20%) show an ectasia of the descended and abdominal aorta, which can lead to a type B dissection.7,8

Another important common manifestation of MFS is mitral valve prolapse (MVP).9,10 Although, only a minority of MFS patients will meet surgery as a consequence of MVP. MVP leads to complications such as severe mitral valve regurgitation, especially in adolescent women, and can result in heart failure and pulmonary hypertension.8 In addition, calcifica- tion of the mitral valve can already be present at a young age.9 However, the same clinical determi- nants (echocardiographic criteria, age, body mass index, and body surface area) that predict outcomes in idiopathic mitral valve prolapse also predict out- comes in mitral valve prolapse associated with MFS.11 Incidence of aortic dilatation and mitral pro- lapse in patients with MFS is comparable in children and adults of the same sex.12

The patient, a male, was admitted due to chest tightness, diarrhea, and syncope. He has a history of coronary artery disease (CAD), pancreatic cancer (treated with a Whipple procedure and neoadjuvant chemotherapy with gemcitabine), and vasovagal syncope. He reported a day of nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and two episodes of syncope. He also mentioned severe lower back pain, substernal chest tightness associated with physical exertion, and an upset stomach. He denied any fevers, chills, cough, abdominal pain, or dysuria. 

His past medical history includes ampullary adenocarcinoma, for which he underwent a pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy with open cholecystectomy. The pathology report indicated a 1.5 cm adenocarcinoma at the junction of the ampullary and duodenal mucosa, described as a T4 lesion, invading the peripancreatic soft tissue or other adjacent organs or structures. He also had a history of small bowel obstruction, gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), arthritis, CAD, hyperlipidemia, and benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH). 

His family history includes congestive heart failure (CHF) in his mother, prostate cancer in his father, and colon cancer in his brother. 

Upon admission, his medications included centrum, compazine, flomax, Lipram PN 16, Metoprolol, Omeprazole, senna, simvastatin, and Zestril. 

In the emergency department, his vitals were temperature 100.3, pulse rate 155/55, and oxygen saturation 97% on room air. He was given 3L of normal saline. His EKG was within normal limits with one set of negative cardiac enzymes. His white blood cell count was 23.7K and he was given empiric vancomycin and ceftriaxone after discussion with oncology. 

His EKG showed normal sinus rhythm at 74, normal axis/intervals, and no ST/T changes. Chest X-ray and KUB showed no acute process or evidence of obstruction. 

During his hospital stay, his syncope was attributed to vasovagal syncope in the setting of nausea and defecation. His chest pain was ruled out for myocardial infarction with three sets of cardiac enzymes and a negative EKG. His nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea improved clinically, with C. diff toxin being negative. These symptoms were thought to be secondary to viral gastroenteritis. 

Upon discharge, his condition was stable with a diagnosis of gastroenteritis and pancreatic cancer.

note_summary:
The male patient was admitted with a chief complaint of abdominal pain. He has a history of hyperlipidemia (HL), hypertension (HTN), gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), and neuropathy. He presented with right-sided, dull, non-radiating abdominal pain and loose, non-bloody, grey stools for two weeks. He also reported itchy yellow skin. His primary care physician noticed abnormal liver function tests (LFTs) and ordered an ultrasound of the right upper quadrant (RUQ). The patient also had a fever of 101 degrees. In the emergency room, his total bilirubin was 8.9, and his alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), and alkaline phosphatase (AP) levels were elevated. An ultrasound of the abdomen showed moderate intra and extrahepatic dilation, but no stones were seen. A CT scan did not reveal any masses. He was given Zosyn for infection control, and pain and nausea management. 

The patient's past medical history includes coronary artery disease (CAD) post coronary artery bypass graft (CABG), myocardial infarction (MI), and chronic chest pain syndrome related to an accident. He has no family history of gallstone disease. 

On physical examination, the patient was well-appearing, awake, alert, and oriented. He had erythema on his soles and tips of toes, and scleral jaundice. His abdomen was soft with mild tenderness on the right. His strength and sensation were intact bilaterally. 

Lab results showed elevated levels of glucose (143), ALT (131), AST (244), alkaline phosphatase (939), total bilirubin (8.9), direct bilirubin (6.5), and lipase (660). His albumin was low at 3.1. His red blood cell count (3.85), hemoglobin (12.6), hematocrit (35.4), mean corpuscular hemoglobin (32.7), mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (35.5), and neutrophils (72.0) were also abnormal. 

A CT scan of the abdomen showed extensive intra and extrahepatic biliary ductal dilation and borderline dilated loops of proximal small bowel. An ultrasound of the RUQ showed moderate intra and extrahepatic biliary ductal dilation with a common bile duct measuring up to 1.8 cm. 

During his hospital stay, an ampullary mass was noted on ERCP, with biopsies pending. There was initial concern for cholangitis given the reported fever prior to admission, but no pus was seen during ERCP. The patient also had erythema limited to his bilateral toes and palms, which was thought to be related to his underlying condition. His statin was held due to elevated LFTs, and his anti-hypertensives were held initially due to concern for infection. 

Upon admission, the patient was on gabapentin, percocet, methadone, Lipitor, atenolol, zestril, imdur, omeprazole, and ASA.

note_summary:
The male patient was admitted for surgery due to a pancreatic mass. The chief complaint was a pancreatic mass. The patient underwent a Pylorus preserving Whipple resection and an open cholecystectomy. Prior to surgery, the patient presented with cholangitis and an ampullary lesion was observed on ERCP. Brushings and ERCP double duct sign were present, suggesting malignancy. The patient's past medical history includes coronary artery disease (CAD) status post coronary artery bypass graft (CABG), myocardial infarction (MI), chronic chest pain syndrome related to an accident (not CAD), hypertension (HTN), gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), and neuropathy. The patient has no known family history of gallstone disease.

Upon physical examination, the patient was alert and oriented, with a mildly tender and distended abdomen. The patient's wound had serosangenous fluid and dry dressings. Abnormal lab results included glucose at 141*, WBC at 11.1*, RBC at 3.65*, HGB at 12.5*, HCT at 35.6*, MCH at 34.4*, MCHC at 35.2*, RDW at 16.7*, PO2 at 169*, PCO2 at 47*, PH at 7.34*, glucose at 147*, lactate at 2.8*, and HGB at 13.4*. CT abdomen with contrast showed resolution of previously seen intrahepatic and extrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation and continued diffuse pancreatic ductal dilatation. CXR showed no pneumothorax after central line placement.

Pathology results showed mild chronic cholecystitis in the gallbladder, no malignancy identified in the segment of small bowel, and moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma involving the ampulla. There was no regional lymph node metastasis, and venous/lymphatic vessel invasion and perineural invasion were absent. Other findings included chronic pancreatitis and pancreatic microcystic serous cystadenoma.

The patient's medications upon admission included Gabapentin, Methadone, Prilosec, Atenolol, Lisinopril, Isosorbide Mononitrate, and Camphor-Menthol lotion for itching. The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of pancreatic ampullary adenocarcinoma. The patient was clear and coherent, alert and interactive, and ambulatory - independent upon discharge.

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted for a ventral hernia. He had previously undergone a Whipple procedure for an ampullary tumor. During a follow-up appointment, two lumps were found in his incision, indicative of small ventral hernias. He was evaluated and scheduled for elective ventral hernia repair. His past medical history includes coronary artery disease (CAD) post coronary artery bypass graft (CABG), myocardial infarction (MI), chronic chest pain syndrome related to an accident (not CAD), hypertension (HTN), gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), and neuropathy. There is no family history of gallstone disease.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Atenolol, Atorvastatin, Gabapentin, Isosorbide Mononitrate SR, Lisinopril, Omeprazole, Aspirin, and MV. During his hospital stay, he underwent ventral incisional hernia repair with Prolene mesh overlay. Post-operative pain was initially managed with Dilaudid PCA, later changed to Morphine PCA due to nausea, and then switched to oral medication when the patient was able to tolerate clear liquids. The patient had difficulty voiding after the removal of the foley catheter, which was reinserted and later removed successfully after the patient was started on Flomax.

The patient's blood work showed a glucose level of 107, UreaN-14, Creat-0.8, Na-135, K-3.9, Cl-97, HCO3-29, AnGap-13, Calcium-8.8, Phos-3.1, and Mg-1.7. At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile with stable vital signs, tolerating a regular diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and pain was well controlled. The patient was discharged home with services to manage JPs and wound monitoring. His discharge diagnosis was ventral incisional hernia.

note_summary:
The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted for a recurrent incisional hernia. He had previously undergone a hernia repair procedure and had been recovering well until a month prior to his current admission, when he noticed a small bulge at the apex of his incision. The bulge was tender to palpation and seemed to bulge more with increased activity. 

His past medical history includes coronary artery disease (CAD) post coronary artery bypass graft (CABG), myocardial infarction (MI), hypertension, gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), neuropathy, a former smoking history, ampullary carcinoma, and hyperlipidemia (HLD). He has also previously undergone a ventral incisional hernia repair with Prolene mesh and a pylorus-sparing pancreaticoduodenectomy with open cholecystectomy (CCY). There is no family history of gallstone disease.

Upon physical examination, the patient was afebrile with stable vital signs. His incision was clean, dry, and intact, without evidence of erythema or drainage. No other abnormal findings were noted during the physical examination.

During his hospital stay, the patient was maintained on IV/PCA pain medication and transitioned to oral narcotic medication with adequate pain control. He remained neurologically intact and alert. His cardiovascular status remained stable, and he was maintained on IV anti-hypertensive medication, transitioning to oral home anti-hypertensives. His respiratory status was also stable, with no episodes of desaturation or pulmonary concerns. His gastrointestinal status was monitored closely, and he was transitioned to a clear liquid diet once flatus resumed. His genitourinary status was also closely monitored, with stable creatinine levels and adequate urine output. His post-op hematocrit was stable, and he showed no signs of infection. His blood glucose was closely monitored, and he was maintained on heparin for DVT/PE prophylaxis.

Upon admission, the patient was on atenolol 50 mg, atorvastatin 80 mg, gabapentin 1200 mg, isosorbide mononitrate 60 mg, lisinopril 10 mg, omeprazole 20 mg, aspirin 325 mg, and a multivitamin. 

The patient was discharged home in stable condition, with clear and coherent mental status, alert and interactive level of consciousness, and independent ambulatory status. His discharge diagnosis was recurrent incisional hernia repair.

He has also previously undergone a ventral incisional hernia repair with Prolene mesh and a pylorus-sparing pancreaticoduodenectomy with open cholecystectomy (CCY). There is no family history of gallstone disease.

Upon physical examination, the patient was afebrile with stable vital signs. His incision was clean, dry, and intact, without evidence of erythema or drainage. No other abnormal findings were noted during the physical examination.

During his hospital stay, the patient was maintained on IV/PCA pain medication and transitioned to oral narcotic medication with adequate pain control. He remained neurologically intact and alert. His cardiovascular status remained stable, and he was maintained on IV anti-hypertensive medication, transitioning to oral home anti-hypertensives. His respiratory status was also stable, with no episodes of desaturation or pulmonary concerns. His gastrointestinal status was monitored closely, and he was transitioned to a clear liquid diet once flatus resumed. His genitourinary status was also closely monitored, with stable creatinine levels and adequate urine output. His post-op hematocrit was stable, and he showed no signs of infection. His blood glucose was closely monitored, and he was maintained on heparin for DVT/PE prophylaxis.

Upon admission, the patient was on atenolol 50 mg, atorvastatin 80 mg, gabapentin 1200 mg, isosorbide mononitrate 60 mg, lisinopril 10 mg, omeprazole 20 mg, aspirin 325 mg, and a multivitamin. 

The patient was discharged home in stable condition, with clear and coherent mental status, alert and interactive level of consciousness, and independent ambulatory status. His discharge diagnosis was recurrent incisional hernia repair.

The patient, a male with a past medical history of dementia (reportedly mild), peripheral vascular disease, bipolar disorder, heart murmur, chronic lower extremity edema, a history of multiple falls, and a recent melanoma resection, was found unresponsive except to painful stimuli in his nursing home. He was noted to be cold to touch, hypotensive, hypothermic, and sinus bradycardic. He was also noted to have diarrhea. He was given 1.5 L of warm fluids and started on a dopamine drip. He was increasingly hypoxic with increased O2 requirement and was placed on a non-rebreather on 10L. A chest X-ray was consistent with fluid overload. He was started on vancomycin and zosyn. 

Upon arrival to the hospital, his vitals were HR 80, BP 185/113, RR 22, O2 sat 100%. His labs were notable for normal WBC, dirty urine, negative serum and urine tox. His chest X-ray was remarkable for bibasilar atelectasis but could not exclude pneumonia. His CT head was unremarkable, but limited by motion artifact. 

His physical exam showed somnolence, dry mucous membranes, quiet heart sounds, clear lungs with possible ronchi left posteriorly, diminished at bases, soft and non-tender abdomen, warm and well perfused extremities, and cog-wheel rigidity in neuro exam. 

His lab results showed a pH of 7.48, glucose of 82, lactate of 1.1, sodium of 147, free calcium of 1.00, urea nitrogen of 18, creatinine of 1.1, lipase of 20, WBC of 5.4, RBC of 4.31, hemoglobin of 12.5, hematocrit of 39.7, RDW of 17.4, PTT of 59.5, and platelet count of 116. His urine showed moderate blood, large leukocytes, RBC of 68, WBC of 49, few bacteria, and 3 hyaline. 

Imaging showed a small left pleural effusion, perihilar vascular congestion, bibasilar opacities, likely atelectasis, however, superimposed infection could not be excluded. His EKG showed a heart rate of 77 with left axis deviation, PR interval of 236, and right bundle branch block. His abdominal X-ray showed a nonobstructed bowel gas pattern with questionable bowel wall thickening within the transverse colon. 

During his hospital stay, he was treated for septic shock, aspiration risk, altered mental status, hypoglycemia, stasis dermatitis, metabolic alkalosis, and lower extremity edema. His medications upon admission included Furosemide, Sinemet CR, Citalopram, Trazodone, Senna, Milk of Magnesia, Miralax, Bisacodyl, Tylenol, Vitamin D3, One Daily Multivitamin, Econazole, Ketoconazole, Clobetasol, and Calmoseptine.

The patient, an elderly male with a history of Alzheimer's disease, an unspecified disease, peripheral vascular disease, a history of aspiration pneumonia and sepsis, and depression and bipolar disorder, was admitted after being found unresponsive at his nursing home. He was intubated and transferred to the medical intensive care unit (MICU) due to hypotension and hypoxia. The patient had been lethargic for three days prior to admission and was found to be hypoxic and hypotensive with runs of ventricular tachycardia. He was treated with amiodarone, norepinephrine, epinephrine, and vasopressin. He also received 4L of intravenous fluids, Zosyn, vancomycin, 100mg hydrocortisone, 2g of magnesium, 2g of calcium, and was paralyzed with vecuronium. He was also found to be hyperkalemic and was treated with calcium, bicarbonate, and insulin, which brought his potassium levels down to 3.2. 

Upon admission, his labs were notable for a white blood cell count of 15.7 with 21% bands, hematocrit of 39.1, platelet count of 159, INR of 1.0, potassium of 3.2, bicarbonate of 20, BUN of 30, creatinine of 2.0, glucose of 435, anion gap of 16, AST of 59, ALT of 8, alkaline phosphatase of 141, total bilirubin of 0.7, albumin of 2.8, troponin T of 0.08, and lactate of 6.1. His urinalysis showed >182 white blood cells, large leukocytes, large blood, negative nitrite, no ketones, and moderate bacteria. 

Imaging showed multifocal consolidations in his lungs, worst in the right upper lobe, and multifocal pneumonia with aspiration, secretions within the trachea and esophagus. There was no acute process in the head CT, no fracture in the CT of the cervical spine, and no definitive pleural effusion on the left, but a small one may be present on the right. 

Upon arrival to the MICU, the patient was intubated, sedated, and unresponsive to commands. His medication record from the nursing home indicated that he was not to be resuscitated, intubated, or receive a feeding tube or dialysis. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with septic shock likely from aspiration pneumonia and a urinary tract infection. He also had hypoxic respiratory failure from acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), altered mental status of unclear etiology, and non-ST elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI). Given his significant illness with poor prognosis and overall poor state of health, a decision was made to transition care to comfort based care. The patient was subsequently extubated and later found to be unresponsive and not breathing. His time of death was recorded as 1635. 

The patient's preadmission medications included melatonin, trazodone, docusate sodium, guaifenesin, senna, carbidopa-levodopa CR, polyethylene glycol, loratadine, acetaminophen, milk of magnesia, bisacodyl, Fleet enema, citalopram, furosemide, multivitamins, and vitamin D.

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and pancreatitis, presented with abdominal pain and fever. He reported a previous episode of pancreatitis, for which he was admitted overnight to a hospital and underwent endoscopy. He also reported a history of alcohol consumption, which he has since reduced. His symptoms began with vomiting and fevers, which improved but then recurred along with right epigastric abdominal pain. Initial vital signs were stable and a CT scan of the abdomen showed inflammation in the retroperitoneum that appeared to originate from the pancreas. Lab results revealed mild transaminitis with a lipase level of 185. He was administered levo/flagyl and admitted for further management.

The patient's physical exam was largely unremarkable, with the exception of tenderness to touch in the right epigastric region. Lab results showed a glucose level of 119, chloride level of 95, ALT of 73, AST of 79, and lipase of 185, all of which were elevated. The patient's RBC count was 3.73, HGB was 11.4, and HCT was 34.8, all of which were low. The patient also had elevated bands at 16 and lymphs at 12, as well as metas at 1. 

Imaging studies, including a CT scan and MRCP, showed extensive pancreatitis with a small to moderate region of hemorrhagic necrosis in the pancreatic neck and head, as well as significant duodenitis secondary to the pancreatitis. There was also persistent mild right hydronephrosis, likely due to involvement of the right ureter by the inflammatory process. 

The patient's hospital course was notable for acute pancreatitis with a prolonged course, including hemorrhagic necrosis on imaging. He was treated with antibiotics and slowly began to tolerate a regular diet. He also experienced a fever while hospitalized, which was likely due to pancreatitis. His hypertension was managed with atenolol and his hyperlipidemia was managed with simvastatin. He also had anemia and reactive thrombocytosis, which were monitored. Additionally, he had a gout flare in his left knee, which was confirmed by fluid analysis and treated with indomethacin and vicodin for breakthrough pain. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking atenolol, aspirin, and simvastatin. His family history was non-contributory and he had no known drug allergies. The patient was discharged in good condition with instructions to avoid alcohol and to use indomethacin as the primary treatment for his gout, with vicodin for breakthrough pain.

DIAGNOSIS Owing to the multi-systemic nature of the disease, establishing the diagnosis of MFS is like piecing together a puzzle. Because aortic and cardiac com- plications are of foremost importance, often the cardiologist will be at the centre of the diagnostic efforts. For a sound evaluation he or she will have to look far beyond just heart and vessels, coordinat- ing a multi-modality and multi-specialist approach (ﬁgure 1).

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of alcoholism, was admitted due to recurrent pancreatitis, presenting with fever and abdominal pain. His first episode of pancreatitis occurred years ago, with recurrences in the past and most recently three weeks prior to this admission. His past medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and a previous appendectomy. He has no significant family history. Upon admission, he was taking atenolol, a statin, celexa, and omeprazole. 

During his hospital stay, he underwent several surgical procedures including an open cholecystectomy with cholangiogram, a pancreatic pseudocyst jejunostomy (Roux-en-Y), and debridement of the tail of the pancreas with external drainage. His hospital course was complicated by post-operative hyperglycemia, which was managed with insulin. He was also monitored for infection, with home Zosyn prescribed for a Streptococcus anginosus infection. 

Abnormal lab results included elevated white blood cell counts (WBC-17.0*#, WBC-15.2*), decreased red blood cell counts (RBC-4.39*, RBC-4.06*), decreased hemoglobin (Hgb-13.0*, Hgb-12.4*), decreased hematocrit (Hct-37.5*, Hct-36.4*), elevated amylase (Amylase-267*, Amylase-288*), elevated lipase (Lipase-102*, Lipase-116*, Lipase-126*), and decreased calcium (Calcium-8.0*). A CT scan revealed pancreatic necrosis and large diffuse fluid collections with apparent fistulous connections to the colon and duodenum. Tissue culture from a right lower quadrant abscess showed moderate growth of Streptococcus anginosus. 

Upon discharge, the patient was hemodynamically stable, tolerating oral intake, and his pain was controlled with an oral regimen. His discharge diagnoses included an infected pancreatic pseudocyst, necrotic pancreas, gallstones, pancreatic leak, and post-op hyperglycemia. He was instructed to keep his incision clean and dry, monitor for signs of infection or other complications, and to continue with dressing changes to the incision. He was also advised to resume all regular home medications and to follow up with his doctor.

note_context: In the revised nosology, new diagnostic criteria have been deﬁned for a sporadic patient and for an index patient with a positive family history (box 1). In the absence of a conclusive family history of MFS, the diagnosis can be established in four distinct scenarios: 1. The presence of aortic root dilatation (Z-score $2 when standardised to age and body size) or dissection13 and ectopia lentis allows the unequivocal diagnosis of MFS, irrespective of the presence or absence of systemic features except where these are indicative of SGS, LDS or vEDS (table 1).

note_summary:
The patient, a right-handed male with a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, anxiety, alcohol abuse, recurrent pancreatitis caused by a complex infected pseudocyst, and gout, was admitted due to an episode suggestive of a seizure. His mother found him in his bedroom with his whole body shaking and unresponsive. He had blood coming out of his mouth but no urinary incontinence. He was disoriented after the episode but regained full consciousness within ten minutes. He reported no aura-like signs before the episode. He also mentioned a recent pedestrian/SUV accident where he was hit in the chest and thrown several feet backwards. He denied having a headache after the accident and has been taking 400mg of Advil three times a day for a week since the accident. He quit alcohol four weeks ago and has not experienced any withdrawal symptoms. 

The patient's family history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, coronary artery disease, and cancer (skin, testicular, throat, and lung). His physical examination revealed a heart rate of 136, blood pressure of 135/93, and oxygen saturation of 99%. He had an old laceration on the volar tongue surface, no bleeding lacerations, and his neck was in a cervical collar. His left shoulder was immobile with edema and echymoses at the left shoulder and left elbow. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (3.38*), Hgb (10.7*), Hct (30.7*), Glucose (141*), CK(CPK) (2495*), ALT (77*), AST (134*), LD(LDH) (413*), and CK-MB (18*). Urine test showed positive for opiates. CSF test showed RBC (1*). 

Imaging results showed a small frontal hyperdensity on the CT scan, which could be either artifactual or hemorrhage. MRI showed no signs of hemorrhage and no cause for the seizure. EEG was unremarkable. X-ray showed a fracture of the proximal humerus predominantly involving the greater tuberosity which is comminuted. 

The patient underwent open reduction/internal fixation of the left shoulder. He had a second seizure prior to the procedure and was therefore started on oxcarbazepine for seizure control. He was instructed not to drive for 6 months. He also had a fever during admission, but lumbar puncture and cultures were negative. His atenolol was increased to 50mg daily for blood pressure control. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking atenolol (dosing unknown), citalopram 40mg daily, simvastatin 80mg daily, and omeprazole 20mg daily.

note_context:
Furthermore, aneurysms are not restricted to the aorta, as cerebral, patent ductus arteriosus, and pulmonary artery aneurysms were also observed. Complete imaging from head to pelvis is therefore recommended in the management of this syndrome. Other cardiovascular features include atrial septal defect, aortic valve regurgita- tion, arterial tortuosity, and mitral valve prolapse, but these features have only been observed once (Meester et al. 2017).

note_summary:
The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted with a chief complaint of esophageal perforation. He has a past medical history of mitral valve prolapse, aortic dilation which is monitored annually via echocardiogram, a surgically repaired detached retina, and possible newly diagnosed reflux. His family history includes a variant of a genetic disorder. 

The patient presented with pneumomediastinum, likely due to esophageal perforation. He reported having undergone an esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) for dilation of a Schatski ring earlier in the day. After the procedure, he consumed some broth and liquids, which was followed by right upper quadrant (RUQ) pain radiating to the back. The pain was exacerbated by eructation, coughing, and deep inspiration. A chest CT scan revealed pneumomediastinum tracking along the right distal esophagus. The patient was treated with ceftriaxone, Dilaudid, and Phenergan before being transferred for further evaluation and treatment. 

The patient also reported a history of intermittent dysphagia for meats, which had worsened over the past few years and recently resulted in regurgitation of chicken. A barium swallow test was performed prior to the EGD, which, according to the patient, was normal. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be in no apparent distress. His vital signs were stable, and his physical exam was largely unremarkable. 

Lab results revealed abnormal RBC count of 4.38, Hct of 39.0, WBC count of 19.7, Cl of 110, and glucose level of 113. An esophagram showed no evidence of extraluminal contrast, suggesting no ongoing esophageal leak. A small distal esophageal diverticulum was noted. A chest X-ray showed no evidence of pneumomediastinum or pneumothorax, with normal cardiomedistinal contours and clear lungs. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was kept NPO and started on IV antibiotics. His condition remained stable with serial CBCs and CXRs. He was later started on a soft solid diet without difficulty swallowing, and his antibiotics were converted to oral administration. 

Upon admission, the patient was on aspirin 81 mg daily, niacin 250 mg three times a day, atenolol 100 mg daily, and topiramate 25 mg twice a day. The patient was discharged in stable condition with a diagnosis of pneumomediastinum. His discharge instructions included calling his doctor if he experienced fever, increased cough or chest pain, hoarseness, or difficulty swallowing, and to continue a soft solid diet until his follow-up appointment in one week.

note_context:
A minority of MFS patients (10%–20%) show an ectasia of the descended and abdominal aorta, which can lead to a type B dissection.7,8

Another important common manifestation of MFS is mitral valve prolapse (MVP).9,10 Although, only a minority of MFS patients will meet surgery as a consequence of MVP. MVP leads to complications such as severe mitral valve regurgitation, especially in adolescent women, and can result in heart failure and pulmonary hypertension.8 In addition, calcifica- tion of the mitral valve can already be present at a young age.9 However, the same clinical determi- nants (echocardiographic criteria, age, body mass index, and body surface area) that predict outcomes in idiopathic mitral valve prolapse also predict out- comes in mitral valve prolapse associated with MFS.11 Incidence of aortic dilatation and mitral pro- lapse in patients with MFS is comparable in children and adults of the same sex.12

The patient, a male, was admitted due to experiencing five episodes of bright red bleeding per rectum. He reported a sudden urge to defecate and evacuation of bright red blood, which he estimated to be about a cup in volume. This was followed by three smaller episodes before arriving at the emergency department. He also reported mild lightheadedness with standing and mild weakness. He denied having fevers, chills, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, chest pain, shortness of breath, abdominal or rectal pain, and hematuria. He also denied any indigestion-like pain or hematemesis. 

The patient has a history of aortic dilation, esophageal perforation, atrial fibrillation managed with Pradaxa, pemphigus managed with prednisone and Cellcept, gastritis, and prostate cancer treated with radiation. He also has a history of benign adenomas and diverticular disease, as revealed by a colonoscopy. He had a schatzkis ring which was dilated. He also has a history of prostate cancer treated with external beam radiation. His last EGD showed gastritis and no ulcers or varices. He has been diagnosed with pemphigus vulgaris in the past year and has been on prednisone and cellcept. 

Upon examination, he was found to have tenderness of lower left ribs with overlying healing ecchymosis, and bright red blood on rectal exam, guiaic positive. His initial vital signs in the ED were: 98.3 63 164/84 18 99% RA. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for WBC (12.8), RBC (4.15), Hgb (13.0), Hct (39.5), Neuts (93.3), Lymphs (3.1), Monos (2.3), Eos (0.1), Baso (0.2), AbsNeut (11.91), AbsLymp (0.40), AbsEos (0.01), Glucose (131), HCO3 (19), AnGap (22), UreaN (22), Na (147), and K (3.4). 

The patient's medications upon admission included PredniSONE, Losartan Potassium, Atenolol, Dabigatran Etexilate, Escitalopram Oxalate, Mycophenolate Mofetil, Tamsulosin, Vitamin D, and Pantoprazole. 

The patient has a family history of a variant of a genetic disorder that affects the body's connective tissue. 

Conditions and symptoms that have been ruled out include fevers, chills, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, chest pain, shortness of breath, abdominal or rectal pain, hematuria, indigestion-like pain, and hematemesis.

note_summary:
The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted to the hospital after experiencing an episode of dizziness. He reported feeling severe congestion, which he occasionally experiences, and while walking across a room, he felt his ears pop, followed by sudden dizziness and a feeling of impending collapse. He also felt nauseated and clammy, but these symptoms dissipated after a few minutes of lying down. He denied experiencing loss of consciousness, numbness, weakness, tingling, difficulty talking, smiling, vision blurriness, vision loss, or a sensation that the room was spinning. He was brought to the emergency department (ED) by ambulance, during which he experienced a migraine aura without a headache. 

Upon evaluation in the ED, his symptoms had completely resolved. A neurologist evaluated him and found no evidence of orthostasis, suggesting the possibility of vestibular neuropathy, but also considering a cardiac cause. His INR was therapeutic and a CT scan was normal. The patient was transferred to the floor with no complaints and reported feeling 'completely fine'. 

The patient's past medical history includes a prosthetic heart valve following surgery for an aneurysm of the thoracic aorta and a benign tremor. His family history includes a father who died of a stroke, a mother with dementia living in an assisted living facility, and a brother with a severe resting tremor. 

Upon admission, the patient's physical exam was largely normal, with a noted mild tremor on finger-to-nose testing bilaterally and very mild cogwheeling at the elbows bilaterally. His admission labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.99), Hgb (13.4), Hct (37.9), MCH (33.6), MCHC (35.3), Neuts (84.3), Lymphs (11.6), and Glucose (108). 

Imaging studies included a CT scan of the head without contrast, which showed no acute intracranial process, and an echocardiogram, which showed a moderately dilated aortic root, a severely dilated ascending aorta, and a well-seated mechanical aortic valve prosthesis with normal leaflet/disc motion and transvalvular gradients. 

The patient's dizziness was thought to be likely related to an inner ear process, given the timing of ear popping and the onset of disequilibrium. However, due to his mechanical aortic valve, a transient ischemic attack was also considered, though deemed less likely given his therapeutic INR upon admission. The patient's echocardiogram showed a well-functioning valve with no clots, but also revealed aortic root dilatation that will require surgical follow-up. 

The patient's medications upon admission included warfarin and multiple vitamins including L-carnitine, fish oil, and selenium. He was discharged with a primary diagnosis of dizziness and a secondary diagnosis of history of mechanical aortic valve replacement. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory upon discharge.

Interventions
note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to a thoracic aortic aneurysm. He has a history of thoracic aortic dissection with graft repair and mechanical aortic valve replacement. The patient was asymptomatic, but an echocardiogram during a recent admission for dizziness showed increased dilation of the aneurysm from 4.0 to 5.5 cm. Further evaluation with CT and CTA confirmed the enlargement of the aneurysm, as well as an ulcer and dissection along the anterior portion. The patient was admitted for IV heparin bridging while off his coumadin prior to upcoming procedures, due to his mechanical aortic valve. 

The patient denies any prior history of stroke, TIA, deep venous thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, bleeding at the time of surgery, myalgias, joint pains, cough, hemoptysis, black stools or red stools. He also denies recent fevers, chills or rigors, exertional buttock or calf pain, chest pain, dyspnea on exertion, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, orthopnea, ankle edema, palpitations, syncope or presyncope. 

His past medical history includes ascending thoracic aortic aneurysm with conduit graft and mechanical AV replacement, Hepatitis C (positive by ab in the past, however negative since), Hyperlipidemia, Hemorrhoids, Benign tremor, and Seasonal Allergies. The patient's family history includes a brother who also has dextrocardia but no genetic testing done, a father who died of a stroke, a mother with dementia, and another brother with severe resting tremor.

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be in no apparent distress, with stable vital signs. His cardiac review was notable for the absence of chest pain, dyspnea on exertion, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, orthopnea, ankle edema, palpitations, syncope or presyncope. 

Lab results on admission showed abnormal RBC count (4.12), Hemoglobin (13.7), Hematocrit (39.4), MCH (33.2), and Glucose (188). Phosphorus was also found to be abnormal (2.4). Imaging showed a large aneurysmal segment of native aortic root surrounding the aortic root graft. The coronary 'buttons' appear to have dehisced from the insertion position on the sides of the endograft. This has led to a 'pseudoaneurysm' surrounding the graft with multiple communications between the tube graft. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent an uncomplicated left heart catheterization with no coronary artery disease found, but a small left groin hematoma resulted which was stable and of no hemodynamic significance. He was bridged with a heparin drip to therapeutic INR. 

Upon admission, the patient was on warfarin, Zyrtec, and Sudafed as needed. The patient was discharged in stable condition with plans for thoracic aneurysm revision in the near future. His primary diagnoses upon discharge were thoracic aortic aneurysm and mechanical aortic valve.

note_summary:
The patient, a white male, was admitted due to a pseudoaneurysm of the aortic root. He had previously undergone a Bental procedure with a Bjo valve, but recently developed dizziness. An echocardiogram and catheterization revealed dilation of the root and a pseudoaneurysm of the aortic root, respectively. His past medical history includes aortic valve replacement, Hepatitis C, hyperlipidemia, hemorrhoids, benign tremor, and seasonal allergies. He has never been tested for a certain genetic disorder, but it is suspected due to physical characteristics. His family history includes a brother with similar physical characteristics and dextrocardia, a father who died of a stroke, a mother with dementia, and another brother with a severe resting tremor.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Warfarin, Cetirizine, a multivitamin, Omega-3 fatty acids with Vitamin E, Phospatidyl Coline, and Pseudoephedrine HCL. His physical examination was largely normal, with no acute distress, dry and intact skin, healed surgical scars, and normal heart and lung function. However, his right groin was noted to be soft and ecchymotic.

An echocardiography report revealed an aortic disruption near the right coronary cusp with a fluid collection consistent with a pseudoaneurysm. The mechanical aortic valve prosthesis was functioning normally. The right coronary artery was not easily identified, leading to the belief that the right coronary button (ostium) was the location of the aortic disruption. This was confirmed intraoperatively. His blood work showed abnormal RBC, Hgb, and Hct levels, as well as an elevated PTT.

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent a reoperation involving a redo sternotomy, resection of the pseudoaneurysm, and reimplantation of the right coronary artery. He tolerated the procedure well and was transferred to the CVICU in a stable condition. He was extubated without incident and began taking beta blockers, Lasix, and Amlodipine for hypertension control. He was also started on Coumadin. A right pleural effusion was noted on a CT scan. He was transfused multiple units for a dropping hematocrit and was taken back to the OR for re-exploration and washout. His postoperative course was largely uneventful, and he was discharged in a stable condition.

There are no formal diagnostic criteria for diag- nosis of LDS. However, TGFBR1/2 genetic testing should be considered in the following scenarios:

1. Patients with the typical clinical triad of hyperte- lorism, cleft palate, and arterial aneurysm. 2. Early-onset aortic aneurysm with variable com- bination of other features including manifesta- tions related to collagen alterations. 3. Families with autosomal dominant thoracic aortic aneurysms, especially those families with precocious aortic/arterial dissection.

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted for the removal of sternal wire. He had previously undergone a redo sternotomy, resection of pseudoaneurysm, and reimplantation of the right coronary artery. He presented with severe pain in the lower half of his sternum, which was intense even with light touch. Despite a normal physical exam and chest x-ray showing no changes and intact sternum and sternal wires, the patient opted for conservative management with pain medication. However, he returned still complaining of pain, especially over two sternal wires. He was admitted for Heparin bridge with Coumadin stopped in anticipation of surgery.

His past medical history includes a Bentall Procedure with Bjork aortic valve replacement, Hepatitis C, Hyperlipidemia, Hemorrhoids, Benign tremor, Seasonal Allergies, and hyperlipidemia. He has never been tested for a certain genetic disorder. His family history includes a brother who also has physical characteristics suggestive of the same genetic disorder and dextrocardia, but no genetic testing has been done. His father died of a stroke, his mother has dementia and lives in an assisted living facility, and his brother has a severe resting tremor.

Upon admission, the patient was on Warfarin, Zyrtec, Multivitamins, Fish Oil (Omega 3), and Pseudoephedrine. His physical exam was normal, with a well-healed sternotomy scar, clear lungs, and a soft, non-distended, non-tender abdomen. His pulse rates were normal.

Abnormal lab results included RBC-4.08*, HGB-13.3*, HCT-39.0*, MCH-32.5*, and PTT-95.9*. A chest radiology report showed interval removal of the sternal wires, no pneumothorax or other complication, mildly hyperinflated but clear lungs, no pleural effusion, and an unremarkable cardiomediastinal silhouette.

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent sternal wire removal and tolerated the procedure well. He was extubated immediately post-operatively and transferred to the stepdown unit for post-operative management. His pre-operative medication management was resumed, and a heparin drip was initiated as a bridge to coumadin management for his prosthetic valve. By the time of discharge, the patient was ambulating freely, the wound was healing, and pain was controlled with oral analgesics. He was discharged to home in good condition with appropriate follow-up instructions.

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to an abnormal aortagram. He has a history of aortic valve replacement (AVR) and resection of aortic root pseudoaneurysm. A recent chest CT showed a thrombus within the graft, which led to the need for further evaluation via aortagram. His past medical history includes Hepatitis C, hyperlipidemia, hemorrhoids, benign tremor, and seasonal allergies. He has never been tested for a certain genetic disorder, despite showing some physical characteristics. 

The patient's family history includes a brother who also exhibits similar physical characteristics and has dextrocardia, but no genetic testing has been done. Their father passed away from a stroke, and their mother is currently living in an assisted living facility due to dementia. Another brother has a severe resting tremor.

Upon admission, the patient's physical exam showed a thin man, speaking in full sentences, with aortic mechanical valve, and no signs of edema or stasis dermatitis. His discharge physical exam showed similar results. 

Admission labs showed abnormal levels of glucose (128), RBC (3.93), HGB (13.0), HCT (35.7), MCH (33.2), MCHC (36.5), and PTT (37.3). Discharge labs showed abnormal levels of RBC (4.09), Hgb (13.4), Hct (37.3), and MCH (32.8). A chest X-ray showed no acute cardiopulmonary process.

During his hospital stay, the patient was transferred to the cardiology service due to weekend staffing. A catheterization showed a large ascending aortic aneurysm. His coumadin was initially stopped in anticipation of surgery and he was placed on a heparin drip. However, the decision was later made to have surgery as an outpatient, so coumadin was restarted and he was discharged with a lovenox bridge until coumadin was therapeutic.

The patient's medications upon admission included Warfarin, Fish Oil (Omega 3), Loratadine for allergies, and Multivitamins. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of pseudoaneurysm and thrombus, with secondary anticoagulation for aortic valve. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory. He was instructed to stop taking his coumadin on a specified date in preparation for his upcoming surgery.
